 xy, 

(4.5.1) For the present example, bath modes are not included. Parameters were chosen to match Raab et al. [183, 188, 193], and are given in Appendix H. x and y are substituted variables for vibrational mode coordinates xi, representing the v6a and v10 a vibrational modes of pyrazine. Quantum registers are defined for each coordinate, with nx = ny = 9 qubits defining a 2 9 × 29 grid in a box of size Lx = Ly = 8 a.u. A vertical excitation is applied from S1 to S2 by rotating the ⌈log 2(2) ⌉ = 1 channel qubit with a single Rx(θ) gate, where 

θ = 2 arccos ␀√P0

. The initial state superposition was set as P0 = 0 .75 , P 1 = 0 .25. The total number of ideal qubits in this classical emulation is therefore 9 + 9 + 1 = 19. The mappings x 7 → (x − Lx/2) and y 7 → (y − Ly/2) centers the origin of the qubit grid, giving additional shifts to coefficients. This does not increase gate count but must be noted for correct dynamics, with on-diagonal terms V00 (x, y ) and V11 (x, y ) being a sum of linear and quadratic terms, extending from the harmonic approximation of the potential wells. 113 The emulation proceed with a total evolution time t = 2500 a.u. ≈ 60 .47 fs with a timestep of t = 1 .0 a.u. ≈ 2.419 × 10 −2 fs. The following steps are performed classically for numerical validation of the propagator. At each timestep, the cross-correlation function is taken as 

C(t) = ⟨ψ1(0) |ψ2(t)⟩ . (4.5.2) This acts as a time-dependent overlap between the initial state and the time-evolved state and can be applied with an electronically-controlled SWAP test, requiring an additional 2 n

qubits. Two damping factors are added to the correlation function, the first being an exponen-tial damping f (t) = e−t/τ damp , giving a phenomenological lifetime broadening as a Lorentzian in the frequency domain. The second is a Blackman window function to suppress the sides of the autocorrelation function and is typical in grid-based signal processing to dampen Gibbs’ ringing. A fast Fourier transform is then applied to recover the absorption spectrum, shown in Figure 4.3. Results can be compared to seminal work by Raab et al. [183] by imposing the spectra onto a digitized extraction of their four-mode MCTDH results, performed using WebPlotDigitizer [194]. The additional two modes ( v1 , v 9a) from their plot act as bath modes and broaden the spectra closer to what would be seen experimentally. An additional peak appears around 233nm, corresponding to the energy at the Franck-Condon excitation point. This is an artifact of initializing away from equilibrium with a two dimensional Gaussian, with no IVR to dissipate energy and prevent its recurrence. The five primary peaks from Raab et al. [183] are faithfully recovered, supporting the proof-of-principle for REQWIEM as an effective method for real-time quantum dynamics in the multi-mode extension. The Berry phase is calculated classically by assuming coherence, first calculating the centroid of the localized wavepacket ⟨xi|xi⟩ (t) = R xi|ψ(r, t )|2dr . The cumulative Berry phase γ(t)for a semiclassical trajectory is given as half the winding angle dθ (t) = ( xdy − ydx )/r 2 as 

γ(t) = γ(t − τ ) + 0 .5dθ .Population dynamics and snapshots of probability amplitudes are shown in Figure 4.4. A fast population transfer is characteristic of initializing mostly in the S2 state. A recurrence 114 Figure 4.3: (left) Spectra comparison for a two-mode pyrazine system simulated as a real-time evolution using REQWIEM (blue) and a four-mode MCTDH simulation conducted by Raab et al. [183]. Data were extracted through digitization [194]. REQWIEM results are renormalized around the peak at 251.5 nm. (right) Centroid trajectory of wavepacket initialized at x = 0 .175 , y = 0 and extracted Berry phase. Spectral intensities are strongly dependent on wavefunction trajectory. period of around 20 fs with slowly decreasing transfer peaks confers the wavepacket spreading out between the two modes without any bath modes or dark states. Agreement between REQWIEM and converged MCTDH results for the five primary spectral peaks confirms that the exact gate-level implementation of all Hamiltonian terms produces physically correct dynamics. 

4.5.2 Four-Mode Pyrazine 

This section works to scale the classical emulation in Section 4.5.1. Two bath modes ( v1, v 9a)are added alongside ( v6a, v 10 a) to follow the reduced dimensional model considered in Raab et al. [183]. The box size was reduced from L = 8 a.u. to L = 4 a.u., in order to reduce the number of qubits per register from n = 9 to n = 8 while retaining the same Nyquist range and grid discretization as the two-mode model, introducing a risk of aliased dynamics from 115 Figure 4.4: (left) Snapshots of probability amplitude in position space over time evolution. Visualization shows log(Probability) for clarity. (right) Population dynamics over evolution. A sharp population transfer at the beginning of the evolution followed by recurrence every 20 fs shows correct qualitative behavior of a two-mode simulation. periodic boundary conditions for wavefunction tails. The total register size for the four-mode model is therefore 8 + 8 + 8 + 8 + 1 = 33 qubits. The total evolution time was t = 2500 a.u. with Trotter step size τ = 1 .0 a.u. The Hamiltonian includes additional terms, including on-diagonal bilinear terms between 

v6a , v 1 , and v9a and off-diagonal bilinear terms between v1 , v 9a and v10 a. Full parameter-ization can be found in Appendix H, using parameters from Raab et al. [183]. A statevector simulation with 33 qubits requires special handling, as one full statevector is represented by 2 33 ≈ 8.6 × 10 9 amplitudes, converting to ∼ 137 .4 GB. This exceeds tractabil-ity for the CPU-based Qiskit SDK, so Nvidia cuQuantum was used, as a Qiskit-compatible GPU-based software kit. As NA-QMD is characterized by strong correlations, this problem is not suitable for tensor network decomposition to give a computational speedup. CuStat-eVec is used for circuit compilation instead of CuTensorNet as a result. The program was compiled and ran on the Georgia Advanced Computing Resource Center’s Sapelo2 cluster, using four Nvidia A100-SXM4-80GB GPUs for the statevector simulation. This is a safe number of GPUs required for this computation, requiring additional space for intermediate computations. The wavefunction was not tracked to reduce I/O overhead. Total wall time 116 for the full 33-qubit emulation is approximately 150 hours per run (600 GPU hours per run), with memory-saving operations for intermediate computations. Wall clock time can be optimized in several ways for future work. The applied circuit is compiled using a static series of primitive gates. Upon multi-GPU compilation, qubits must be repeatedly re-indexed to perform local multiplications on single GPUs. In other words, rows and columns of the density matrix are repeatedly swapped upon classical circuit compilation. Clustering commuting terms in each operator (e.g. CR z operations in Vdiag )according to locally-defined GPU indexes can save large constant factors, especially when compounding between Trotter steps and operations. Such an optimization is beneficial in future application on quantum hardware, significantly reducing routing costs by minimizing the path traversed by all qubits. For a full statevector simulation, computational resources double for each additional qubit. This is a well-known phenomenon in the emulation of quantum circuits, highlighting an advantage of quantum computation. A consequence remains where emulating with n = 9 qubits per mode (33 to 37 qubit emulation) requires 32-64 mutually compatible GPUs with exponential computation time compounded with MPI overhead. For reference, the Frontier supercomputer at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in Oak Ridge, Tennessee, USA, has 37 , 888 AMD Instinct M1250X GPUs. This is sufficient to simulate a 46 − 47-qubit statevector for chaotic systems where negligible efficiency is brought by tensor decomposition), prior to computation time considerations. This is the bottleneck of classical compiling quantum circuits simulating dense Hamiltonians. The resulting spectra and populations are shown in Figure 4.5. 

4.5.3 Multi-channel Validation Beyond Two Electronic States 

A simple four-channel extension considers a Landau-Zener bowtie model, consisting of four linear potential surfaces and four constant couplings. As shown in other examples, the sur-faces need not be linear, nor the couplings constant. In the diabatic basis, the Hamiltonian 117 Figure 4.5: (Left) Spectra comparison for four-mode pyrazine using REQWIEM (red), com-pared to Raab et al. [183] (black). Data from [183] was digitized using WebPlotDigitizer [194]. (Right) Populations of S2 and S1 over time. takes the form ˆH(x) = − ∂2

∂x 2

12μ14 +



V00 (x) λ01 0 λ03 

λ10 V11 (x) λ12 00 λ21 V22 (x) λ23 

λ30 0 λ32 V33 (x)



, (4.5.3) where 

V00 (x) = β1x + E + V0

V11 (x) = −β1x + V0

V22 (x) = β2x + V0

V33 (x) = −β2x − E + V0.

(4.5.4) Diabatic state slopes are given as β1,2. E represents an energy bias as a vertical offset, and V0

is a reference energy. The Landau-Zener bowtie model is exceptionally well-suited to exhibit the multi-channel extension, with classical numerical solutions easy to construct and solve without significant compute time. The four-channel example exercises the XOR-class frag-mentation with M = 4 ( m = 2), meaning three XOR classes and six fragment applications 118 per Trotter step. The focusing Clifford varies across fragments (different Hamming weights) (Algorithm 2). A Gaussian wavefunction with width σ = 0 .5 represents a particle with mass 

μ = 2000 .0 a.u. on a grid with n = 12 qubits in the V00 channel. The box size is L = 80 a.u. with periodic boundary conditions. Electronic channels are stored in ⌈log 2(4) ⌉ = 2 channel qubits, totaling 12 + 2 = 14 qubits for this emulation. Initial position is set to x = 40 a.u. (middle of the box), with p0 = 0 a.u., a total evolution time of 5000 a.u. with a timestep of 

τ = 0 .1 a.u. Quantum circuit compilation is validated against a classical diagonalization on the same finite grid with 2 12 = 4096 grid points. The classical reference constructs the full 4 N ×4N cou-pled Hamiltonian matrix, where the kinetic energy operator is represented as an FFT-based circulant matrix that remains exact on the periodic finite grid, matching the QFT-based kinetic energy used by the quantum circuit. The coupled Hamiltonian is then diagonalized directly (via scipy.linalg.eigh ), yielding all eigenvalues {Ek} and eigenvectors {| ϕk⟩} .From the decomposition, the autocorrelation function is computed as 

C(t) = ⟨ψ0|e−i ˆHt |ψ0⟩ = X

> k

|ck|2 e−iE k t, (4.5.5) where ck = ⟨ϕk|ψ0⟩ are expansion coefficients of the initial state. Fourier transforming with Gaussian damping ( τd = 1000 a.u.) and Blackman window identical to the time evolution yields the autocorrelation spectrum. Since many eigenstates carry nonzero overlap with the initial wavepacket across four coupled potential surfaces, a comb-like eigenvalue structure results; the windowed Fourier transform broadens these into resolved spectral peaks. To quantify spectral convergence across the parameter space, the Wasserstein-1 distance (earth mover’s distance) is computed between the classical reference spectrum and each quan-tum simulation trial spectrum [195]. Both spectra are restricted to a common energy window and L1-normalized to define probability distributions, and the one-dimensional Wasserstein-1 distance 

W1(Pref , P trial ) = inf 

> γ∈Γ( Pref ,P trial )

Z

|E − E′| dγ (E, E ′), (4.5.6) 119 measures the minimum cost of transporting one spectral distribution into the other. γ(E, E ′)acts as a a joint probability distribution on pairs ( E, E ′) with imposed marginals Pref and 

Ptrial . ‘inf’ denotes that this integral is unbounded on γ. This metric is well-suited to spectral comparison, being sensitive to both peak position shifts and intensity redistribution, unlike pointwise metrics requiring spectra to be evaluated on identical grids. A parameter sweep over grid resolution ( n = 7–12 qubits), Trotter step size ( τ = 0 .1, 0 .5, 1 .0 a.u.), and box size (L = 10, 20, 40, 80 a.u.) maps the convergence landscape, with W1 → 0 indicating agreement with the exact result. This convergence analysis is presented in Appendix H. Classical quantum circuit emulation results are compared to the exact diagonalization in Figure 4.6. The classical diagonalization follows the same initial conditions ( N = 4096 grid points, L = 80 a.u., μ = 2000 a.u.) and the same Hamiltonian. The real-time evolution algorithm converges on the same spectral structure as the classical exact diagonalization with sufficient system metrics ( n, t, τ , L), as confirmed by monotonically decreasing Wasserstein-1 distance with increasing grid resolution and decreasing timestep, supporting REQWIEM as a viable time-evolution algorithm in the multi-channel case. This convergence analysis is given in Appendix H This emulation also serves as a numerical proof-of-principle for a quantum algorithm modeling multi-channel quantum dynamics. 

4.6 Summary 

This chapter specifies the REQWIEM algorithm, detailing the application of each term in the Hamiltonian as parameterized quantum circuits. The multi-mode and multi-channel exten-sions are numerically validated against classical methods (MCTDH, Lanczos) for two-mode, four-mode, and four-channel systems spanning 14-33 ideal qubits, using classical compilation of quantum circuits (Figures 4.6, 4.5, and 4.3). The fan-out protocol was not implemented in these validations, requiring additional qubits for intermediate operations. Converged spec-tra, dampened recurrence in the four-mode pyrazine model, and Berry phase accumulation 120 Figure 4.6: (Left) Comparison between spectra obtained from Trotterized quantum dy-namics (REQWIEM, red) and classical exact diagonalization (black). The classical reference constructs and diagonalizes the full 4 N × 4N Hamiltonian on the same finite grid, with spec-tral convergence quantified by the Wasserstein-1 distance. With matching parameters, this distance is W1 = 6 .86 × 10 −7 eV with a Trotter step τ = 2 .42 × 10 −3 fs. (Right) Population dynamics for each diabatic surface. Xs denote classically derived populations at select times, with a < 0.5% difference from quantum circuit compilation. around the conical intersection numerically validate REQWIEM as an algorithm capable of handling NA-QMD (Figures 4.3 and 4.4). Gate count scales with the number of variables, as O(kM 2d2n2) with d modes, n qubits per mode, and M electronic channels, with k ∝ t. REQWIEM contains additive terms that are subdominant to O(kM 2d2n2), including M log m Toffoli gates for truth value ancilla computation, and O(log d) layers of CNOTs for channel register fan-out. Both are given to increase algorithmic parallelism. Even though proof is not given that REQWIEM has optimal gate count, optimal gate scaling is clear for a grid-based representation from the information theoretic bound. A detailed resource analysis is given in the next chapter, directly comparing REQWIEM to the phase gradient approach of Motlagh et al. [43], following several adjustments for consistent system complexity and parameterizations. Symmetry constraints and channel register fan-out scalably reduce circuit depth to O(kM χn 2), where χ ∝ d, with explicit depth calculations performed in the forthcoming chapter . 121 Chapter 5 Resource Analysis 

Multidimensional, multi-channel quantum algorithms for NA-QMD have just begun to be considered. Ollitrault et al. [44] suggests extensivity of their rotation-based, first-quantized approach, but is restricted to one mode with linearly approximated coupling, while Motlagh et al. [43] relies on phase gradients loading data from a QROM oracle, with published re-source estimations omitting Duschinsky mixing and bilinear vibronic coupling terms. Both restrict themselves to a second-order expansion of the vibronic Hamiltonian. REQWIEM di-rectly extends Ollitrault et al. [44] and incorporates improved application of multi-controlled gate operations. This chapter gives a quantitative resource comparison between REQWIEM and Motlagh et al. [43], assuming validity of the latter sans numerical validation. Resources are recalculated from the existing literature [43], following discussion on sufficient model complexity and system size requirements (Section 5.2). Detailed methodology for this recal-culation can be found in Appendix G. Once a comparison is made, an estimated quantum advantage is found for REQWIEM when compared against compute times for ML-MCTDH. To review notation, d represents the number of vibrational modes, M is the number of electronic channels, m = ⌈log 2 M ⌉ is the number of channel qubits in |schannel ⟩, and n is the number of qubits per mode register. All d registers are taken as having n qubits for ease of calculation, though optimization can result in large constant factors of depth reduction for both methods. Five benchmark systems are defined, four taken from Motlagh et al. [43], being Anthracene ( d = 21, M = 6, n = 10) [196], a ( N O )4−Anthracene dimer ( d = 19, M = 5, 

n = 10) [197], an Anthracene-Fullerene dyad ( d = 246, M = 4, n = 10) [198], and a reduced dimensional Anthracene-Fullerene dyad ( d = 11 , M = 4 , n = 10) [198]. The fifth benchmark 122 is pyrazine, as given in Raab et al. [183] with d = 24 , M = 2 , n = 10. A position register size 

n = 10 is kept constant between modes despite potential for optimization to clarify scaling behaviors. As discussed in Chapter 2, the size of each register should be chosen according to the characteristic energy, optimizing grid resolution and Trotter error. Resources given here are used as a standard between REQWIEM and Motlagh et al. [43] for an apples-to-apples comparison between QROM-loading and rotation synthesis. Resource estimates primarily rely on Non-Clifford depth and T -gate count, extended to T -depth and full algorithmic depth. Focus is directed toward the exact gate counts of REQWIEM, with additional information on resource estimates in Appendix G. A broader comparison against quantum signal processing and qubitization methods, which offer asymp-totically optimal query complexity but incur oracle-construction overhead, is given in Chap-ter 8. 

5.1 REQWIEM Gate Counts and Circuit Depth 

Exact gate counts for rotations, CNOTs, and Toffolis are derived for each component of the REQWIEM Trotter step, alongside per-step cost formulas in terms of the symmetry index sets G1, G2 (on-diagonal) and G3, G4 (off-diagonal). Throughout, a mode indexed by r is taken to have nr qubits (grid points Nr = 2 nr , spacing ∆ r = Lr/N r). On-diagonal and off-diagonal potentials are polynomials in the position operators xr. In the computational basis, |xr⟩ = |∆r(xr − Nr/2) ⟩, with the integer xr = Pnr −1

Full derivations for these operations are given in Appendix B. 123 The term ‘depth’ initially neglects all rotation synthesis costs for fault-tolerant architec-tures. This is later converted into T -depth under rotation synthesis heuristics, where a full depth is later calculated.

For each correction surface s,the (1+ n) CR z gates per mode share a read-only control but target distinct vibrational qubits, executing in parallel at depth 3; after fan-out, all d modes execute simultaneously, giving per-surface correction depth 3 independent of both d and n. 5.1.4 Per-Trotter-Step Fan-Out Costs 

CNOT costs of the fan-out and fan-in operations, aggregated over one full Trotter step, are collected below. Each of the two half-step applications e−iV dτ / 2 requires one fan-out and one fan-in of the 

m channel register qubits and M − 1 − m truth-value ancillas (totaling M − 1 source qubits. The per-invocation cost is ( M − 1)( d − 1) CNOTs, giving a total of 

C(d) fan = 4( M − 1)( d − 1) (5.1.22) CNOTs at depth 4 ⌈log 2 d⌉. Channel register fan-out enables mode-parallel execution of Tiers 2 and 3 (via local UCR (m) 

> Z

copies), while fan-out of the truth-value ancillas enables mode-parallel execution of Tier 1 corrections. Each of the 2( M − 1) fragment applications fans out and fans in the m channel qubits (after the focusing Clifford Uζ ). The per-invocation cost is m(d − 1) CNOTs, giving a total of 

C(od) fan = 4( M − 1) m(d − 1) (5.1.23) CNOTs at depth 4( M − 1) ⌈log 2 d⌉.130 Reduction from ␀d

> 2

 sequential bilinear layers (without graph coloring) to χ ≤ d parallel layers yields a depth improvement factor of 

␀d

> 2



χ′(Kd) = d

2 (d even) , d − 12 (d odd) , (5.1.24) representing an additional ∼ d/ 2 reduction beyond mode-parallel fan-out. The condition Γ α ⊗Γβ = Γ i ⊗Γj selects edges between modes of complementary represen-tations for off-diagonal bilinear terms. The resulting graph is generally a union of complete bipartite subgraphs KnΓα ,n Γβ , one for each pair (Γ α, Γβ ) satisfying the selection rule. By K¨ onig’s theorem [192, 199], the chromatic index of a bipartite graph equals its maximum degree: 

χ′(KnΓα ,n Γβ ) = max( nΓα , n Γβ ), (5.1.25) For the full off-diagonal conflict graph God , the chromatic index satisfies 

χ′(God ) ≤ ∆( God ) + 1 , (5.1.26) where ∆( God ) is the maximum number of symmetry-allowed off-diagonal bilinear partners of any single mode. When the component bipartite subgraphs share no vertices (i.e., each mode participates in at most one complementary-symmetry pairing), the bound tightens to 

χ′(God ) = ∆( God ). The aggregate fan-out CNOT cost per Trotter step is 

C(step) fan = C(d) fan + C(od) fan = 4( M − 1)( d − 1)(1 + m), (5.1.27) with total fan-out depth 4 M ⌈log 2 d⌉.The depth reduction factor per invocation (on-diagonal or off-diagonal) is 

Dsequential 

Dfan-out 

= d D mode 

Dmode + 2 ⌈log 2 d⌉ 

> Dmode ≫log d

−−−−−−−→ d, (5.1.28) where Dmode = O(M n 2) from the Gray-code UCR decomposition, which dominates log 2 d

for all benchmark systems considered. Effective depth reduction is therefore d-fold for intra-mode terms within each invocation. 131 The off-diagonal block benefits from this reduction within each of the 2( M − 1) frag-ment applications: mode-parallel intra-mode UCR depth per fragment is ( M +3) ␀n

> 2

 +(M +1)( n+1), independent of d. Without fan-out, the same UCR circuits would require d

sequential passes, increasing off-diagonal depth by a factor of d and making it the domi-nant bottleneck for systems with many vibrational modes. The on-diagonal block benefits identically: after the Tier 2 fix (Section 5.1.2), the intra-mode contribution ␀n

> 2

(2 M +1) is mode-parallel, and only the bilinear Tier 3 is serialized over the chromatic schedule. This is the most significant depth reduction in REQWIEM, being an absolute factor of d reduction relative to gate count, unavailable to phase-gradient architectures, which are restricted to serial execution. 

5.1.5 Ancilla Requirements and Reuse 

On-diagonal fan-out uses ( M − 1)( d) − m ancilla qubits to store both channel register copies and truth value ancilla copies. While not required, these truth value ancilla reduce the number of CNOT gates per UCR ladder from m + 2 to 1 + 2 for degree-2 polynomial terms controlled on the m-qubit channel register. Off-diagonal fan-out requires m(d − 1) ancilla qubits for d−1 copies of the m channel qubits. Since M −1 ≥ m for all M ≥ 2 (with equality at M = 2), on-diagonal allocation is the larger of the two. Off-diagonal fragments reuse a subset of the on-diagonal ancilla pool, so total fan-out ancilla budget is 

Afan = ( M − 1)( d − 1) . (5.1.29) These ancillas are allocated once and shared across all on-diagonal and off-diagonal invoca-tions; no additional qubits are introduced by the fan-out protocol beyond this pool. After each on-diagonal half-step, truth values are uncomputed by reversing the Toffoli cascade, freeing those work qubits for reuse as scratch space during the off-diagonal block if needed. 132 5.1.6 Assembled per-step cost 

The second-order Trotter step consists of 2 kinetic half-steps, 2 on-diagonal half-steps, and 1 off-diagonal full step. Per step rotation count: 

Rstep = 2 Rkin + 2 R(half) d + Rod . (5.1.30) The off-diagonal contribution Rod = M (M −1) Ssites scales as O(M 2 d n 2) and dominates for 

M ≥ 4, exceeding the on-diagonal contribution 2 R(half) d = O(M d n 2) by a factor of O(M ). For M = 2, off-diagonal and on-diagonal rotation counts are comparable. Per step Toffoli count: 

Tstep = 2 T (half) d = 2( m · 2m − 2m+1 + 2) . (5.1.31) The only Toffoli gates in the circuit are those required for truth-value ancilla computation in the on-diagonal half-steps. They are not “necessary” for computation, but act to reduce overall depth. Neither the kinetic nor off-diagonal block contributes any Toffoli gates. For all benchmark systems ( m ≤ 3), Tstep ≤ 20 per step, making the Toffoli contribution to the fault-tolerant T -gate budget negligible. Per step CNOT count: 

Cstep = 2 Ckin + 2 C(half) d + C(d ,half) fan 

 + Cod , (5.1.32) where C(d ,half) fan = 2( M −1)( d−1) is the per-half-step fan-out/fan-in cost for the on-diagonal block. The off-diagonal CNOTs Cod (Eq. 5.1.18) include the Clifford, fan-out, and UCR contributions. Per step depth: 

Dstep = 2 Dkin + 2 D(half) d + Dod . (5.1.33) Off-diagonal depth Dod = O(M 2n2χ(G4)) arises from 2( M −1) sequential fragment applica-tions, each contributing O(M n 2χ(G4)) after intra-fragment fan-out. 5.1.8 Role of symmetry 

The symmetry index sets G1–G4 directly control the number of nonzero terms: 

• On-diagonal linear (G1): only totally symmetric modes contribute to tuning dis-placements. For D2h, |G 1| = dAg . Under the hybrid decomposition, Tier 1 multiplexing reduces the correction rotation count from ( M −1) dn (UCR) to ( M −1) |G 1|n, a saving of ( M −1)( d − |G 1|)n per half-step. 136 • On-diagonal quadratic : Always allowed. 

• On-diagonal bilinear (G< 

> 2

): only same-irrep pairs. For pyrazine ( d = 24, D2h): |G < 

> 2

| =31 vs. ␀24 2

 = 276 without symmetry. 

• Off-diagonal linear (G3): modes with Γ( xr) = Γ s ⊗ Γs′ .

• On-diagonal quadratic : Always forbidden. 

• Off-diagonal bilinear (G4): mode pairs with Γ r ⊗ Γs = Γ s ⊗ Γs′ . For pyrazine (24-mode): |G 4| = 29. For C1 symmetry, |G 1| = d and |G < 

> 2

| = ␀d

> 2

, so the Tier 1 linear savings vanish and the hybrid reduces to the pure-UCR formulae with a negligible constant-phase correction of (M −1)( d−1) per half-step. Tables 5.1 and 5.2 collect the on- and off-diagonal cost structures.

Each vibrational coordinate xr is encoded in an nr-qubit register as xr = ∆ r

Pnr −1 

> ℓ=0

2ℓ−nr qℓ +

x(min)  

> r

, where qℓ ∈ { 0, 1} is the ℓ-th qubit. Substituting this binary expansion into polyno-mial diabatic potentials produces rotation angles that factorize exactly as a state-dependent coupling constant times a binary weight factor: 

θ(s) 

> ℓ

= c(s) × 2f (ℓ, n), (5.1.46) where c(s) is the potential energy surface parameter on surface s and f (ℓ, n) is a linear function of qubit indices and register widths. Linear terms yield nr single-qubit rotations per mode, quadratic terms yield ␀nr

> 2

 intra-mode cross-pair rotations, and bilinear terms yield 

nrns inter-mode cross-pair rotations (see Appendix B). Number of rotation sites per mode is fixed by polynomial degree and grid size, inde-pendent of functional form. Gate counts in Sections 5.1.2–5.1.3 are tight for the polynomial model, not approximations subject to smoothness or truncation conditions. Binary weight factors pass through the channel-register Walsh–Hadamard transform as a site-independent multiplicative constant. For on-diagonal UCR (m) 

> Z

, the M -point Walsh trans-form acts exclusively on the M -dimensional vector of coupling constants ( c(0) , . . . , c (M −1) ); for off-diagonal UCR (m−1)  

> Z

within each XOR-class fragment, the ( M/ 2)-point transform acts on M/ 2 coupling values addressed by spectators. Since M is small and fixed (typically 2– 8), all Walsh coefficients are retained exactly and no spectral truncation is performed. This contrasts with grid-based Walsh encodings [111], where Walsh transforms act on 2 n grid-sampled potential values and the resulting coefficient decay depends on the smoothness of 

V . This is discussed further with regard to scattering potentials in Chapter 6. 

5.1.11 Circuit Depth Summary 

Beyond gate counts, circuit depth determines execution time and error accumulation. RE-QWIEM exploits several layers of parallelism inherent in the vibronic Hamiltonian structure, 138 though the XOR-class fragmentation of the off-diagonal block introduces sequential fragment applications that were absent in the two-state case. The kinetic operator acts independently on each vibrational register. All d modes execute simultaneously, reducing kinetic depth from O(dn 2) to O(n2). For potential terms, channel-register fan-out enables parallel execution across modes: 

• On-diagonal (all three tiers): Phase 3 fans out both m channel register qubits and 

M − 1 − m truth-value ancillas to d local copies. Tier 1 corrections execute in parallel across modes using the truth-value copies. Tiers 2 and 3 execute their UCR (m) 

> Z

circuits on local copies of the channel register; the Gray-code CNOT schedule depends only on m (not on mode-specific coupling constants), so all d copies remain synchronized throughout the traversal. Tier 2 is therefore fully mode-parallel, and Tier 3 is mode-parallel and color-serial. 

• Off-diagonal (per-fragment): Within each fragment application, the m channel qubits are fanned out after the focusing Clifford Uζ , enabling mode-parallel UCR (m−1) 

> Z

execution. This reduces the per-fragment intra-mode UCR depth from O(d2M 2n2) to 

O(dM n 2). Bilinear cross-mode terms xrxs and xr′ xs′ with {r, s }∩{ r′, s ′} = ∅ act on disjoint registers and execute simultaneously. The |G < 

> 2

| on-diagonal and |G 4| off-diagonal mode pairs reduce to χ(G< 

> 2

) and χ(G4) sequential layers respectively, where χ is the chromatic index of the mode-pair conflict graph. For C1 symmetry, χ ≤ d − 1, giving a further reduction from ␀d

> 2



to ( d − 1) layers. For on-diagonal Tier 1, quantum multiplexing applies the base surface V00 with zero channel overhead and reduces correction depth from O(M ) sequential controlled blocks to 

O(m) levels in the binary hierarchy, with disjoint control sets executing in parallel on fanned-out copies. For off-diagonal potential terms, each fragment’s UCR (m−1)  

> Z

applies M/ 2 rotations per site via Gray-code decomposition, serialized at the single-qubit level within each UCR gate but parallelized across modes via fan-out. 139 The M − 1 XOR-class fragments do not commute with one another and must be applied sequentially. The symmetric Trotter product Vod (τ ) applies each fragment twice (forward and backward) for a total of 2( M − 1) sequential fragment applications per Trotter step. This sequential composition is the principal depth overhead introduced by the XOR-class fragmentation relative to the (invalid for M > 2) single-pass Hadamard approach. Per-step depth with all parallelism enabled is 

Dstep = 2Dkin 

| {z } 

> kinetic half-steps

+ 2Dd

|{z} 

> on-diag half-steps

+ Dod 

|{z} 

> off-diag full step

, (5.1.47) with contributions: 

Dkin = O(n2), (5.1.48) 

Dd = 2 ⌈log 2 d⌉ + ( n+1) + 3( M −1) 

| {z }

> Tier 1 (mode-parallel)

+ (2 M +1) 

n

2

| {z }

> Tier 2 (mode-parallel)

+ χ(G< 

> 2

) (2 M +1) n2

| {z }

> Tier 3 (color-serial)

, (5.1.49) 

Dod = 2( M −1) D(UCR) + 2 ⌈log 2 d⌉ + 2 mM, (5.1.50) Per-fragment intra-mode UCR depth is 

D(UCR) = (2 m+3) 

n

2



+ (2 m+1) n + (2 m+1) + χ(G4) (2 m+3) n2. (5.1.51) Off-diagonal depth dominates for M ≥ 4 and scales as O(M 2n2χ(G4)), since each of the 2( M − 1) fragments contributes O(M n 2χ(G4)). For the on-diagonal block, the color-serial Tier 3 contributes O(χ(G< 

> 2

) M n 2); Tier 2 is mode-parallel via fan-out and contributes only O(M n 2), which is subdominant for systems with bilinear coupling. The overall depth complexity per simulation is 

Dsim = O␀kM 2n2χ(G4) + χ(G< 

> 2

) M n 2 , (5.1.52) where k is the Trotter step count. The off-diagonal fan-out within each fragment provides a depth reduction of approximately d relative to a mode-serial implementation of the same fragmented scheme, preserving utility of fan-out architecture despite the 2( M − 1)-fold frag-ment serialization. 140 In accumulator-based implementations such as the QROM-loaded phase gradient reg-ister of Motlagh et al. [43], every coupling term within a Trotter step writes to a shared phase register sequentially, imposing depth Ω(# terms × DQROM+add ) with no possibility of parallelization. For the off-diagonal block, this gives accumulator depth O(M 2dn · DQROM ), incurring a factor of O(d) not present in REQWIEM. Depth advantage of the UCR-based approach over the accumulator architecture therefore persists under XOR-class correction, since fan-out parallelism operates within each fragment independently of how many frag-ments are needed. Table 5.3: Gate scaling terms in REQWIEM Trotterization. SQC refers to ‘single-qubit Clifford’ operators, as {NOT, Y, Z, H, S }. Depth complexities assume channel-register fan-out is enabled; χ denotes the chromatic index of the symmetry-allowed mode-pair conflict graph, satisfying χ = Θ( d) for C1 symmetry and χ ≤ ∆( G) + 1 for non-degenerate point groups, where ∆( G) is the maximum vertex degree. χd and χod refer to the chromatic indices of the on-diagonal and off-diagonal conflict graphs, respectively; in general χd ≤ χod .

5.1.12 Gate-Count and Depth Comparison 

XOR-class fragmentation required for M > 2 electronic states changes the resource landscape of the off-diagonal block relative to the two-state case. This section considers these gate-count and depth properties and compares them with the accumulator-based approach of Ref. [43], 141 which shares the same XOR-class fragmentation and focusing Clifford structure but differs in how the diagonalized fragment Hamiltonians are exponentiated. Results are summarized in Table 5.3.

Within a single sweep direction, each fragment contributes ( M/ 2) Ssites rotations, and M − 1fragments sum to M (M −1) /2 · Ssites = R(min) od , exactly saturating the lower bound. The factor-of-2 overhead remains as an unavoidable cost of second-order Trotter accuracy, shared equally by any symmetric product-formula approach. The per-fragment focusing Clifford Uζ consists of h(ζ) − 1 CNOTs and one Hadamard gate, where its adjoint U † 

> ζ

has the same cost. The total focusing CNOT count across all 2( M − 1) fragment applications is 

Cfocus = 4 m · 2m−1 − M + 1  = O(mM ), (5.1.55) and the total focusing depth is 2 mM . These are non-parameterized gates that encode no coupling-function information, reducing a multi-qubit Pauli string XS to a single-qubit Xs1

amenable to Hadamard diagonalization. Focusing overhead is asymptotically negligible, where Cfocus /R od = O(m/ (M S sites )) van-ishes as the grid size or mode count grows. For benchmark vibronic systems considered in here, focusing CNOTs contribute fewer than 0 .1% of the total gate count. Nevertheless, focus-ing overhead represents a structural distinction from on-diagonal blocks, where the channel register is never modified and no non-parameterized channel-register gates are needed. 

Architectural comparison: UCR versus phase gradient accumulator 

Both REQWIEM and the approach of Motlagh et al. [43] employ the same XOR-class frag-mentation and focusing Clifford to block-diagonalize off-diagonal fragments. For the on-diagonal potential, REQWIEM uses the three-tier hybrid decomposition, while the phase gra-dient method uses a QROM–arithmetic–accumulator pipeline as for the off-diagonal terms. The two methods therefore diverge in how diagonal operators are exponentiated: 

• REQWIEM (UCR): Each diagonal block (whether on-diagonal or a focused off-diagonal fragment) is decomposed into classically precomputed rotation angles via Gray-code UCR (p) 

> Z

(p = m for on-diagonal, p = m−1 for off-diagonal fragments). Phase 3 of the on-diagonal protocol fans out both the m channel register qubits and the M −1−m truth-value ancillas; within each off-diagonal fragment, m channel qubits are fanned out after the focusing Clifford. In both cases, all d modes execute their UCR 143 Table 5.4: Architectural comparison of diagonal-block exponentiation strategies within the shared XOR-class fragmentation framework. 

Property PG accumulator REQWIEM 

Channel register Read-only (QROM address) Read-only (UCR control) Phase register Shared write target N/A (no phase register) Focusing Cliffords Cfocus (identical) Cfocus (identical) On-diag mode parallelism None (serializes) Yes (all three tiers) Off-diag mode parallelism None (serializes) Yes (per-fragment fan-out) Per-fragment off-diag depth Ω( d · DQROM+add ) D(UCR) + 2 ⌈log 2 d⌉

On-diag depth per half-step Ω( d · Dterms ) D(half) d (Eq. 5.1.14) Fan-out depth reduction None ≈ d-fold (intra-mode) Ancilla overhead b bits + QROM (M −1)( d−1) fan-out copies circuits in parallel on independent copies of the control qubits. The channel register (or its fan-out copies) is read-only throughout the UCR block. 

• Phase gradient accumulator (Ref. [43]): Each diagonal eigenvalue is loaded via QROM into a shared phase gradient register, and phases are accumulated through controlled semi-classical additions. The accumulator is a shared write target: every coupling term, whether on-diagonal or off-diagonal, intra-mode or bilinear, must write to it sequentially, precluding mode-parallel execution regardless of available ancillas. Consequences of this architectural distinction are summarized in Table 5.4. Distinction lies in circuit depth rather than gate count. Both approaches incur the same 

O(M 2 Ssites ) rotation count (up to constant factors determined by the QROM precision model), and both share the O(mM ) focusing Clifford overhead. UCR gains its advantage by treating the channel register as a passive control bus: since UCR targets only vibrational qubits (never the channel register or a shared accumulator), all d modes can be targeted concurrently through fan-out. 144 A per-fragment depth reduction of ∼ d results for intra-mode terms, yielding an overall off-diagonal depth of O(M 2n2χ(G4)). Bilinear cross-mode terms require qubits from two modes simultaneously and cannot be fully mode-parallelized; they are instead serialized over 

χ(G4) chromatic layers of the mode-pair conflict graph, with all disjoint pairs in each layer executing simultaneously on independent fan-out copies. For the on-diagonal block, full channel register fan-out enables mode-parallel execution of all three tiers (Section 5.1.2). Tier 1 corrections execute at depth 3( M −1) (read-only truth-value controls with distinct vibrational targets). Tier 2 intra-mode UCR circuits execute at depth ␀n

> 2

(2 M +1), independent of d, since each mode operates on a local copy of the m-qubit control set. Tier 3 bilinear terms are color-serialized at depth χ(G< 

> 2

) n2 (2 M +1). On-diagonal depth is therefore O(χ(G< 

> 2

) M n 2), dominated by Tier 3. In the accumulator architecture, all on-diagonal terms including intra-mode sites that REQWIEM parallelizes must write serially to the shared phase register, giving on-diagonal accumulator depth O(d · DQROM+add ) per evaluation. 

5.2 Direct Comparison with Phase Gradient Methods 

REQWIEM is compared to the phase gradient approach of Motlagh et al. [43] on five bench-mark vibronic systems spanning a range of electronic-state counts ( m = 1–3) and vibrational mode counts ( d = 11–246). As detailed in the cited work (Pradhan and Zeng, [197]), the (N O )4-Anthracene dimer is more accurately modeled with quartic diabatic surfaces and Morse-like couplings ( ∝ (1 − e−x)2). Resource estimations for this system therefore do not reflect potential simulability, only serving as an additional pedagogical comparison between REQWIEM and phase gradient methods. Once again, d denotes the number of vibrational modes, M = 2 m the number of padded electronic states ( m = ⌈log 2 M ⌉, with M the physical state count), n = 10 the grid qubits per mode, and χ = χ′(Kd) the chromatic index of the complete mode-pair graph ( d − 1 for 145 even d, d for odd d. All estimates use a precision-adjusted phase accumulator register size 

b = 45 (Appendix G). For a comparison between the two methods, architectural analysis first compares both methods in units of non-Clifford layers, as Toffoli layers for the phase gradient method and rotation ( Rz ) layers for REQWIEM, isolating the structural advantages of each circuit architecture independently of fault-tolerant compilation details. Second, the fault-tolerant analysis decomposes both circuits into a universal gate set, providing an apples-to-apples comparison in T -depth and total decomposed depth, accounting for different per-operation costs of Toffoli gates and synthesized rotations. 

5.2.1 Phase Gradient Method: Corrected Cost Formulas 

The phase gradient algorithm implements each diagonal-block exponentiation (whether on-diagonal or a focused off-diagonal fragment) through a four-stage pipeline [43]: (i) a QROM lookup loads a classically precomputed coefficient into a quantum register; (ii) arithmetic circuits (out-of-place squarers or multipliers) compute the grid-point–dependent factors x2

> r

or xrxs; (iii) controlled semi-classical additions accumulate the resulting phase into a shared 

b-bit phase gradient register; and (iv) the QROM and arithmetic are uncomputed. Every coupling term in every fragment traverses this pipeline sequentially, since the shared phase gradient register is a write target that serializes all terms. 

Corrected parameters 

Resource estimates published by Motlagh et al. [43] use n = 4 qubits per vibrational mode (N = 16 grid points) and a b = 20-bit phase gradient register. Four corrections are applied to bring these estimates to physically justified values (full derivations in Appendix G): 1. Grid resolution (Chapter 2): n = 4 → n = 10 ( N = 16 → 1024). Converged MCTDH calculations require 32–64 primitive basis functions per mode [183, 184]; n = 4 146 violates the Nyquist criterion for nonadiabatic dynamics nmax ∼ 8–10 (up to 15 − 20 for extremely energetic systems, Chapter 2). 2. Phase precision : b = 20 → b = 45. At n = 10, bilinear term one-norms grow by a factor of 64 relative to n = 4; b = 20 (precision ≈ 6 × 10 −6) becomes insufficient, while 

b = 45 (precision ≈ 1.8×10 −13 ) provides a comfortable margin for accumulation across 

O(M 2) terms per step. This margin safely handles coherent accumulation of errors and additional Trotter overhead. 3. Bilinear terms included: Published estimates omit all bilinear cross-mode terms 

xrxs, which account for ␀d

> 2

 on-diagonal and ␀d

> 2

 off-diagonal mode pairs in C1 symmetry (See Motlagh et al. [43] Supplemental Material). These terms dominate gate count for most systems and are essential for correct physics (Chapter 4). 4. Trotter error recalibrated : The combined effect of a grid-induced norm increase (βgrid = 64) and the bilinear commutator contributions ( βBVC = 1 + η2N (tot) BVC /M )amplifies the commutator sum by a factor of β = βgrid × βBVC , requiring √β× more Trotter steps to maintain a given target error. 

Per-term Toffoli costs 

At the corrected parameters ( n = 10, b = 45), out-of-place squaring costs Csq = k(k−1) /2 = 45 Toffoli, the out-of-place multiplier costs Cmult = n2 = 100 Toffoli, and the controlled semi-adder into the b-bit phase register costs Csemi (nb) = nbb−nb(nb−1) /2, giving Csemi (10) = 405 and Csemi (20) = 710 Toffoli [201, 202]. Each term additionally incurs two QROM lookups (forward + uncompute) at cost 2 C(ζ)QROM , where C(ζ)QROM = 2 m−h(ζ) for XOR class ζ with Hamming weight h(ζ). A linear term costs 421 Toffoli, a diagonal quadratic 816, and a bilinear cross-mode term 926 (all for the diagonal fragment ζ = 0 at M = 6, C(0) QROM = 8). 147 Per-step Toffoli count 

Second-order Strang splitting with kinetic merging gives a per-step cost of Ωstep = Ckin + 2 CVd + CVod , (5.2.1) where Ckin , CVd , and CVod are the kinetic, on-diagonal, and off-diagonal fragment costs. The off-diagonal cost CVod sums over all M − 1 XOR classes, each with QROM cost reduced by the focusing Clifford. In C1 symmetry, the bilinear terms dominate: for Anth/C 60 (d = 246, 

M = 4), the bilinear contribution accounts for > 99% of Ω step .

Serial circuit depth 

The phase gradient architecture is inherently serial: within each term, the QROM → arith-metic → accumulate → uncompute pipeline forms a single data-dependency chain; across terms within a fragment, the shared phase gradient register enforces sequential execution; and across fragments, Trotter splitting is sequential. The non-Clifford (Toffoli) depth there-fore equals the Toffoli count: 

DPG = Ω step . (5.2.2) The combined effect of the four corrections increases the per-step Toffoli count by 5– 123 × (depending on the system) and the Trotter step count by 8–60 ×, yielding total Toffoli counts 361–17 ,100 × larger than the published values, dependent on the benchmark system’s number of modes and electronic channels. 

5.3 Architectural analysis: non-Clifford layer comparison 

The two algorithms employ different non-Clifford primitives: every non-Clifford operation in the phase gradient circuit is a Toffoli gate, while every non-Clifford operation in REQWIEM is a parameterized Rz rotation. Isolating circuit-architectural advantages of each approach begins with a comparison in units of non-Clifford layers, counting Toffoli layers for the phase gradient method and rotation layers for REQWIEM. This comparison reveals the depth 148 separation mechanism but overestimates fault-tolerant advantage, because one rotation layer and one Toffoli layer have different costs when decomposed into T -gates ( C(rot)  

> T

= 23 versus 

d(Toff)  

> T

= 4, a 5 .75 × asymmetry that favors the Toffoli-based method) (Appendix J). Again deviating from published estimates [43], all estimates in this section use the physical molecular point group symmetry of each benchmark system. Symmetry enters in two ways by restricting the number of nonzero bilinear coupling pairs ( |G < 

> 2

| on-diagonal, |G 4| off-diagonal), reducing the rotation count in both methods, and by determining the chromatic index of the mode-pair conflict graph ( χ′(G< 

> 2

), χ′(G4)) governing the depth of color-serialized bilinear layers in REQWIEM. The first effect benefits both methods equally; the second benefits REQWIEM exclusively, being able to parallelize. 

5.3.1 Non-Clifford Depth 

Clifford gates (CNOTs, Hadamards, S gates) are excluded from the depth count; in a fault-tolerant architecture, the assumption is made that these operations can be implemented transversally at negligible cost relative to non-Clifford operations synthesized from T -gates. Precise calculation of H , S gates is determined on native hardware gate sets, where the entanglement gate CNOT is decomposed into transversal gates and native entanglement operations (echo cross-resonance, Mølmer-Sørensen, CZ). The phase gradient architecture is purely serial: within each Trotter step, the QROM– arithmetic–accumulate pipeline writes to a shared phase register, and all terms execute sequentially. Its non-Clifford depth is therefore DPG = Ω step (one Toffoli layer per Toffoli gate). Non-Clifford depth counts only Rz layers in REQWIEM. A Gray-code UCR (p) 

> Z

with P =2p angles contributes P rotation layers (the interleaved CNOT transitions are Clifford and do not contribute to non-Clifford depth). A CNOT–UCR (p) 

> Z

–CNOT sandwich for cross-pair terms has the same P rotation layers; the outer CNOTs add only Clifford depth. REQWIEM 149 non-Clifford depth with full fan-out is 

DR = 2 D(rot) kin + 2 D(rot) d + D(rot) od , (5.3.1) with contributions:

α ≈ Ωstep /R step = Ω /R (recall Ω as circuit depth). This identity confirms that α captures the intrinsic per-term cost ratio between the QROM–adder pipeline (Toffoli gates per term) and the UCR scheme (rotations per site) when both operate serially. 

5.3.2 Parallelism 

The fan-out factor ϕ captures mode-level parallelism exclusive to REQWIEM. Since the phase gradient architecture writes all terms to a shared accumulator, it has no analogous parallelization opportunity; ϕ therefore represents a structural advantage that cannot be closed by improvements to the phase gradient pipeline. Table 5.5 presents the REQWIEM rotation count Rstep and non-Clifford depth D(fan) R

for each benchmark system under physical molecular symmetry. The fan-out factor ϕ =150 D(ser) R /D (fan) R is determined entirely by REQWIEM’s circuit architecture and can be computed independently of the phase gradient cost. Three sources of parallelism contribute to ϕ:

• Mode fan-out (intra-mode sites in both blocks): Fan-out of the channel register and truth-value ancillas parallelizes across d modes, giving ϕintra = d for intra-mode terms in Tiers 1–2 (on-diagonal) and all intra-mode terms within each off-diagonal fragment. 

• Site parallelism (on-diagonal Tier 1 only): Within each correction surface, (1+ n)rotations on distinct target qubits execute in a single non-Clifford layer, collapsing (1+ n) sequential rotation layers into 1. Truth-value control ancillas are read-only, but the same argument does not apply to UCR sites in Tiers 2–3 or the off-diagonal block, where the Gray-code traversal serializes rotations within each UCR. 

• Graph coloring (bilinear sites in both blocks): |G < 

> 2

| (on-diagonal) and |G 4| (off-diagonal) mode pairs are parallelized into χ′(G< 

> 2

) and χ′(G4) color layers respectively, giving ϕbilin = |G| /χ ′.The composite ϕ is a weighted average of contributions, dominated by bilinear cross-mode terms (which account for >95% of per-fragment rotation count for d ≥ 11). For systems with C1 symmetry (Anth/C 60 ), the bilinear contribution gives ϕbilin = ␀d

> 2

/χ ′(Kd) ≈ d/ 2, as confirmed by ϕ = 123 .3 ≈ 246 /2 for the full system. For systems with nontrivial point group symmetry, the restricted mode-pair graph has a lower |G| /χ ′ ratio than Kd (the disjoint union structure admits fewer pairs per color class), yielding a smaller ϕ. Pyrazine ( D2h) achieves 

ϕ = 8 .4, compared to the hypothetical C1 value of ϕ ≈ 12, reflecting the reduction from 

|G| /χ ′ ≈ 6 (physical) versus d/ 2 = 12 ( C1) in the bilinear sector. Absolute depth, however, decreases much more steeply than ϕ: pyrazine’s fan-out depth D(fan) R = 3 ,406 is 4 .2× smaller under D2h than under C1, because symmetry eliminates the majority of bilinear terms from both the numerator and denominator of ϕ while shrinking D(fan) R .151 5.3.3 Per-term overhead α

The per-term overhead α = Ω step /R step requires a symmetry-corrected Toffoli count Ω step 

from the phase gradient method. Since α captures a ratio of per-term costs between the two pipelines, its value is expected to be approximately preserved under symmetry reduction, where the per-term cost of a bilinear QROM lookup+multiplier+adder ( ∼ 900 Toffoli at 

n = 10, b = 45) versus a bilinear UCR ( M or M/ 2 rotations) is intrinsic to each architecture. Symmetry-corrected Ω step values for each benchmark system are derived in (Appendix) and will complete the entries in Table 5.5. For the present analysis, α = 1 .16 − 5.13, being approximately preserved under symmetry reduction ±7%, confirming that the capture of an intrinsic per-term cost ratio. The range of α holds for all systems (since per-term Toffoli cost exceeds the per-term rotation count for any m), and the total non-Clifford layer advantage 

αϕ is bounded below by ϕ alone. 

5.3.4 Limitation of the non-Clifford layer metric 

Non-Clifford layer advantages reported in Table 5.5 overstate the fault-tolerant execution time advantage due to a false equivalence between one rotation layer and one Toffoli layer, made intentionally to highlight structural properties. In a fault-tolerant architecture, each Toffoli decomposes into d(Toff)  

> T

= 4 sequential T -gate layers, whereas each Rz rotation synthe-sized via Ross–Selinger-PyZX-TODD pipeline decomposes into C(rot)  

> T

sequential T -gate layers (baseline CT = 23) (Appendix J). One rotation layer is therefore CT /d (Toff)  

> T

= 23 /4 = 5 .75 ×

more expensive in T -depth than one Toffoli layer. To obtain a more accurate metric for fault-tolerant advantage, the non-Clifford layer ratio must be corrected by this factor. 152 Table 5.5: REQWIEM non-Clifford depth components and fan-out factor under physical molecular symmetry. χ′

5.4 Error Budget 5.4.1 Error sources and budget allocation 

Total simulation error in REQWIEM arises from three independent sources: Trotter de-composition error from the split-operator scheme, discretization error from the finite grid representation, and rotation synthesis error from the fault-tolerant implementation of pa-rameterized gates. The triangle inequality bounds the total error as 

εtotal ≤ εTrotter + εdisc + εsynth , (5.4.1) where each term represents the operator norm distance between the ideal and implemented evolution. For chemical applications, a typical target is εtotal ≤ 1.6 × 10 −3 EH (1 kcal/mol, “chemical accuracy”). An error budget is allocated as εTrotter ≤ 8×10 −4, εdisc ≤ 5×10 −4, and 

εsynth ≤ 3 × 10 −4. This allocation reflects relative controllability of each error source: Trotter error dominates for short simulations but is reduced by increasing step count k; discretization 153 error is set by the physical problem and qubit budget; synthesis error is determined by T -gate resources. 

5.4.2 Trotter error 

The second-order Suzuki–Trotter decomposition implements ˜U (τ ) = e−iT τ / 2 e−iV τ e−iT τ / 2, (5.4.2) as an approximation to U (τ ) = e−iHτ for H = T + V and time step τ = t/k .The second-order Trotter error is bounded by nested commutators [126, 127]: 

εTrotter ≤ t3

24 k2

␀∥[V, [V, T ]] ∥ + 2 ∥[T, [T, V ]] ∥. (5.4.3) For kinetic operator T = P 2/2μ on a grid with N = 2 n points and pmax = πN/L , direct calculation gives (Appendix K): [ T, [T, V ]] vanishes for linear terms but scales as O(N 2) for quadratic and bilinear terms, with [ p2, {p, x }] = −4ip 2, introducing an additional factor of 

p2max ∝ N 2.For a vibronic Hamiltonian with bilinear couplings, the Trotter error bound becomes: 

εTrotter ≤ t3

24 k2

␀C0 + C2N 2 , (5.4.4) where C0 = O(α2/μ ) collects linear contributions and C2 = O(γ/μ 2) collects bilinear contri-butions. To maintain target error ε, the required step count scales as: 

kbilinear = O

t3/2N

√ε



. (5.4.5) Step count increases for a given target error as the number of grid points increases. The system’s characteristic energy (Nyquist limit) doubles with every additional qubit, commen-surately increasing spectral norms present in the timestep calculation. For large-scale simula-tion, it therefore becomes necessary to optimize each mode’s register size to a characteristic energy scale to minimize Trotter error. For the two-mode pyrazine validation simulation 154 (N = 512, n = 9), the k = 2500 steps used provides substantial margin for Trotter accuracy, chosen primarily for sufficient spectral resolution ( δω = 2 π/ (k τ )). The full derivation of the commutator norm bounds is given in Appendix K. A safe margin of n = 10 provides a margin for similar systems with greater energetic properties, particularly for systems whose diabatic states have large energy gaps. In many cases, n = 10 may unnecessarily increase spectral norm, but it used as a benchmark between methods to ensure converged observables. 

5.4.3 Rotation synthesis error 

On fault-tolerant quantum computers, arbitrary single-qubit rotations Rz (θ) are synthesized from the discrete Clifford+ T gate set. The Ross–Selinger algorithm achieves optimal scaling for the per-gate approximation error εθ, requiring NT (εθ) = 3 log 2(1 /ε θ) + O(log log(1 /ε θ)) 

T -gates [203, 204]. With additional improvements from mixed fallback synthesis, PyZX inter-block optimization, and T-Optimization by Diagonal Decomposition (TODD) optimization, a conservative estimate yields a T -gate reduction factor of ∼ 6.8× relative to bare Ross– Selinger [204]. For a circuit with NR parameterized rotations, each synthesized to precision εθ, the total synthesis error accumulates coherently as εsynth ≤ NR · εθ, giving the per-gate precision requirement εθ ≤ εsynth /N R.The total number of parameterized rotations per Trotter step is Rstep ; the total rotation count over the full simulation is NR = k · Rstep . For the two-mode pyrazine validation model (d = 2, M = 2, n = 9), Rstep ≈ 1.2 × 10 3, yielding NR ≈ 3.0 × 10 6 at k = 2500 Trotter steps. For the stated threshold of εsynth ≤ 3 × 10 −4 EH, the per-gate precision is εθ ≈ 1.0 × 10 −10 ,which is comfortably achievable with CT ≈ 100 T -gates per rotation via Ross–Selinger, or 

CT ≈ 15 after the 6 .8× optimization factor. For the benchmark systems of Section 5.8, the rotation counts are substantially larger (Table 5.16), and the per-gate precision tightens accordingly. The most demanding case is Anth/C 60 (d = 246), with NR ≈ 2.0 × 10 11 rotations at k = 3274 steps, requiring εθ ≈

155 1.5×10 −15 and CT ≈ 148 via bare Ross–Selinger ( CT ≈ 22 after optimization). This is within the range where the baseline compilation parameter CT = 23 used throughout Section 5.8 to be self-consistent. The classical emulations presented in Chapter 4 use double-precision floating-point arithmetic for all rotation angles ( εθ ≈ 10 −16 ), and εθ = 10 −14 is used for all 

T -gate estimations given here, providing conservative margin over the precision required by the error budget. 

5.5 Cost metrics 

The phase gradient method incurs Ω step Toffoli gates per Trotter step. REQWIEM incurs 

Rstep parameterized rotations (Eq. 5.1.30) and a negligible number of Toffoli gates ( Tstep ≤ 20 for all benchmark systems; Eq. 5.1.31). The T -gate budget is

Under physical symmetry, the ratio Ω /R ranges from 1 .16 ((NO) 4-Anth, m = 3) to 5 .13 (pyrazine, m = 1), yielding crossover values C⋆T =4.7–20 .5 (Table 5.10). 

5.6 Fault-tolerant analysis: T-depth comparison 

To compare execution time, both circuit design approaches are decomposed into the Clifford + T gate set. Two depth metrics are reported: 1. T -depth : number of sequential T -gate layers (Clifford layers contribute zero T -depth). 156 2. Total decomposed depth : the number of sequential layers of any type ( T , CNOT, or single-qubit Clifford). This decomposition converts both algorithms to a common unit of cost, eliminating per-operation asymmetry between Toffoli gates and synthesized rotations that inflates the non-Clifford layer comparison of Section 5.3. 

5.6.1 Decomposition rules 

Each Toffoli gate decomposes into a circuit on three qubits with T -count C(Toff)  

> T

= 4 (via the temporary logical AND [201]), T -depth d(Toff)  

> T

= 4, and total decomposed depth d(Toff) tot = 12 (including interleaved CNOTs and Hadamards). Each Rz (θ) rotation is given to precision ε

using C(rot)  

> T

T -gates via the Ross–Selinger protocol [204], producing an alternating sequence of T and Clifford gates on a single qubit with T -depth d(rot)  

> T

= C(rot)  

> T

and total depth d(rot) tot =2C(rot)  

> T

− 1. 

5.6.2 QROM (phase gradient) 

The architecture is purely serial and composed entirely of Toffoli gates; the decomposed depths are therefore: 

D(T )PG = d(Toff)  

> T

× Ωstep = 4 Ω step , D(tot) PG = d(Toff) tot × Ωstep = 12 Ω step . (5.6.1) 

5.6.3 REQWIEM 

This circuit method interleaves rotation layers and CNOT layers. Let D(rot) R denote the num-ber of rotation layers in a fan-out-enabled circuit (Eqs. 5.3.2–5.3.4) and D(CNOT) R the number of CNOT layers (Gray-code transitions, fan-out, focusing Cliffords). After decomposition, each rotation layer expands into C(rot)  

> T

T -layers and C(rot)  

> T

− 1 interleaved Clifford layers, 157 while each CNOT layer remains unchanged: 

D(T )R = C(rot)  

> T

× D(rot) R , (5.6.2) 

D(tot) R = (2 C(rot)  

> T

−1) × D(rot) R + D(CNOT) R . (5.6.3) The per-step rotation-layer count D(rot) R = D(fan) R is identical to the fan-out-enabled non-Clifford depth of Table 5.5; negligible Toffoli contributions from truth-value computation (Tstep ≤ 20) is omitted. 

5.6.4 Connection to the architectural analysis 

T -depth ratio connects directly to the non-Clifford layer decomposition of Section 5.3:

> T

= 4 /23 ≈ 0.17 at baseline is the “tax” REQWIEM pays for using synthesized rotations instead of Toffoli gates: each non-Clifford layer in REQWIEM costs 5.75 × more T -depth than a Toffoli layer. The architectural advantage αϕ must exceed 

C(rot)  

> T

/d (Toff)  

> T

= 5 .75 for REQWIEM to hold a T -depth advantage. For all benchmark systems, αϕ ranges from 10 × (dimer) to 283 × (Anth/C 60 full), with pyrazine at 43 × (Table 5.5), comfortably exceeding this threshold. 

5.6.5 T -depth results 

Table 5.6 presents the T -depth comparison. REQWIEM achieves a T -depth advantage across all five systems, ranging from 1 .7× ((NO) 4-Anth Dimer, d = 21, m = 3) to 49 × (Anth/C 60 

full, d = 246, m = 2), with pyrazine at 7 .5×. These estimates account for the full per-operation cost asymmetry between the two non-Clifford primitives. The T -depth advantage improves as the rotation synthesis cost C(rot)  

> T

decreases (Ta-ble 5.9), because the per-rotation T -depth shrinks while the per-Toffoli T -depth ( d(Toff)  

> T

= 4) is fixed. At the Hamming weight phasing regime ( CT = 10), the T -depth advantage improves 158 from 1 .3× to 3 .9× for the dimer and from 49 × to 113 × for the full Anth/C 60 system. In the limit C(rot)  

> T

→ 4 (equal to the Toffoli T -count), the T -depth ratio converges to the non-Clifford layer advantage αϕ : from 10 × to 283 ×. Future advances in rotation synthesis will therefore benefit REQWIEM in both T -gate count and T -depth simultaneously. 

5.6.6 Total decomposed depth results 

Table 5.7 presents the total decomposed depth (including CNOT and single-qubit Clifford layers) after Toffoli and rotation decomposition. Total-depth advantages are uniformly larger than the T -depth advantages: 2 .5–72 × versus 1 .7–49 ×. This amplification arises from an asymmetry in depth inflation factors and improved depth scaling in REQWIEM. Every layer in the phase gradient circuit is a Toffoli gate, so depth inflates uniformly by d(Toff) tot /d (Toff)  

> T

=12 /4 = 3 × going from T -depth to total depth. In contrast, REQWIEM’s circuit is composed of both rotation layers ( ∼ 29–38% of pre-decomposition depth) and CNOT layers ( ∼ 62– 71%); only rotation layers inflate (by (2 CT − 1) /C T = 45 /23 ≈ 2×), while the CNOT layers contribute no T -depth and add only modestly to total depth. The effective inflation factor for REQWIEM ( ∼ 14–18 ×; Table 5.8) is comparable to a phase gradient approach’s 12 ×, but the phase-gradient method’s pre-decomposition depth is already αϕ times larger; decomposition preserves and slightly amplifies this gap. 5.7 Interpretation and regime map 

The phase gradient algorithm resides in the Toffoli cost sector, where every improvement must come from better QROM constructions, arithmetic circuits, or Toffoli factory through-put. REQWIEM costs improve for better rotation synthesis protocols. 

5.7.1 Depth advantage (structural, universal) 

The UCR Gray-code cascade is intrinsically less deep per coupling site than the QROM– arithmetic pipeline ( α ≥ 1, i.e., fewer non-Clifford layers per term even in serial execution). Further, intra-fragment fan-out parallelizes across modes ( ϕ ≈ d/ 2), exploiting a structural feature of the read-only channel register. The combined non-Clifford layer advantage αϕ 

ranges from 10 × to 283 × (Table 5.5). After accounting for a 5 .75 × per-operation cost asym-metry between rotations and Toffoli gates, fault-tolerant T -depth advantage remains with 162 REQWIEM, with 1 .7–49 × improvement at baseline synthesis (Table 5.6), growing to 4– 283 × in the Hamming weight regime ( CT = 10). The total decomposed depth advantage (2 .5–72 ×; Table 5.7) is uniformly larger than T -depth advantage due to an asymmetric in-flation of Toffoli versus rotation layers, both being robust across all five systems and all compilation parameters examined. 

5.7.2 Regime map 

Results trace out largely independent forms of advantage. as compiled in Table 5.12.. The fan-out factor scales as ϕ ≈ d/ 2 for large d (bilinear graph-coloring reduction dominates), yielding T -depth advantages that grow from 1 .7–2 .3× at d = 11–21 to 49 × at d = 246 (baseline CT = 23). Serial overhead α ≥ 1 for all systems ensures that fan-out amplifies an existing advantage rather than compensating for a deficit. Off-diagonal rotation count scales as M (M − 1) Ssites , producing Ω /R ratios that decrease from 5 .1 ( m = 1) to 1 .2 ( m = 3). At baseline synthesis costs, crossover shifts from C⋆T = 20 .5 (REQWIEM-favourable) down to C⋆T = 4 .7 (phase-gradient-favourable). Reasonable future reduction in rotation synthesis cost alleviate the penalty for m ≤ 2 but not for m = 3. Table 5.12: Summary of per-step comparison at baseline compilation ( C(rot)  

> T

= 23, C(Toff)  

> T

=4). Non-Clifford layer advantages reflect the architectural parallelism of REQWIEM but overestimate the fault-tolerant advantage by a factor of CT /d (Toff)  

> T

= 5 .75. T -depth and total decomposed depth are apples-to-apples fault-tolerant metrics; both show REQWIEM advan-tage across all systems. T -gate comparison is m-dependent: systems with m = 1 are nearly neutral, while m = 3 systems incur a substantial rotation-count overhead. All estimates use physical molecular symmetry.

163 For the materials discovery applications suggested by Motlagh et al. [43], singlet fission chromophores with d = 19–246 modes and N = 4–6 states, REQWIEM offers a 1 .7–49 ×

T -depth advantage (increasing to 4–283 × in the Hamming weight regime) and a 2 .5–72 ×

total decomposed depth advantage, at the cost of a 2–5 × T -gate penalty at baseline synthesis that can be partially or fully offset by advances in rotation synthesis. 

5.8 Fault-Tolerant Overhead Estimates 

All previous estimates establish properties for a single Trotter step. To assess practical feasi-bility, this section propagates these per-step advantages through the full simulation pipeline: Trotter step counts determined by the Hamiltonian commutator norms (identical for both methods), logical qubit budgets, and fault-tolerant overhead including surface code distance, physical qubit counts, and wall-clock execution time. All estimates use physical molecular symmetry, the corrected phase gradient method parameters ( n = 10, b = 45, bilinear terms included (Appendix G), moderate bilinear coupling ratio ( η = 1 /3), and baseline compilation (C(rot)  

> T

= 23, C(Toff)  

> T

= 4). The bilinear coupling ratio η is given as an estimated fraction of bilinear terms that are non-negligible. 

5.8.1 Trotter step counts 

The second-order Strang splitting with kinetic merging yields step counts determined entirely by the Hamiltonian commutator norms, independent of the circuit implementation. The dominant commutator [ T, [T, V BVC ]] scales as O(N 2) in the grid size, and the bilinear terms amplify the commutator sum by a factor βBVC = 1 + η2N (tot) BVC /M . At n = 10 ( N = 1024) and η = 1 /3, the combined grid and bilinear amplification requires √β more steps than the n = 4 calibration to maintain ε = 1% target error over t = 100 fs. The resulting step counts (Table 5.13) range from 790 (Anth/C 60 , d = 11) to 12 ,290 ((NO) 4-Anth), reflecting the system-dependent commutator norms. These step counts are identical for both circuit implementations, since both simulate the same Hamiltonian under the same Trotter splitting. 164 Table 5.13: Trotter step counts for the second-order Strang splitting T / 2 → Vd/2 → Vod →

Vd/2 → T / 2 with kinetic merging. All entries: t = 100 fs, ε = 1%, moderate bilinear coupling 

η = 1 /3. The step count is determined by the Hamiltonian commutator norms and is identical for both circuit implementations.                              

> System dMmnsteps
> Pyrazine 24 21∼5 000 (NO) 4-Anth 19 5312 290 (NO) 4-Anth Dimer 21 632 792 Anth/C 60 (d= 11) 11 42790 Anth/C 60 (d= 246) 246 423 274

5.8.2 Logical qubit comparison. 

The two methods have comparable logical qubit counts but different compositions (Ta-ble 5.14). The phase gradient method requires d·n mode register qubits, m electronic register qubits, a b = 45-bit phase gradient register, and arithmetic scratch space (squarer/multiplier output registers and dirty QROM ancillas), totaling xPG = dn + m + b + O(n + 2 m). The REQWIEM scheme requires d · n mode register qubits (identical), d · m channel register qubits (including d−1 fan-out copies for mode-parallel execution), and one truth-value an-cilla, totaling xR = d(n + m) + 1. For systems with small m relative to b, the fan-out copies (d · m ≤ 3d) are cheaper than the phase gradient register ( b = 45) plus arithmetic scratch, giving REQWIEM a qubit advantage of 0 .59–0 .84 ×. For Anth/C 60 (d = 246, m = 2), the fan-out copies total 492 qubits, yielding a 1 .15 × qubit penalty for REQWIEM. In all cases, the mode registers ( dn ) dominate both counts. 

5.8.3 Wall-time and physical qubit estimates. 

Depth advantage propagates directly into the fault-tolerant regime. An optimistic logical cycle time of 1 μs per non-Clifford layer is used (Table 5.15) [205]. System scales exist far beyond current hardware capabilities, so the logical cycle time is set to be below current 165 Table 5.14: Logical qubit comparison. Phase Gradient [43] d mode registers ( n qubits each) + m electronic register + b = 45 phase gradient register + arithmetic scratch (squarer/multiplier output registers, QROM dirty ancillas). REQWIEM: d mode regis-ters ( n qubits each) + d × m channel register (original + fan-out copies for mode-parallel execution) + truth-value ancillas. REQWIEM avoids the phase gradient register and arith-metic scratch entirely; the dominant additional cost is the d × m fan-out copies.

> Pyrazine 24 1356 265 0.74 ×
> (NO) 4-Anth 19 3308 248 0.81 ×
> (NO) 4-Anth Dimer 21 3328 274 0.84 ×
> Anth/C 60 (d= 11) 11 2227 133 0.59 ×
> Anth/C 60 (d= 246) 246 22 577 2 953 1.15 ×

benchmarks. Cycle time is taken to be equivalent between phase-gradient and REQWIEM architectures, despite all qubits in the former relying on toffoli cascades from a single QROM oracle. Qubit routing costs are not thoroughly considered in this dissertation, but are non-negligible in deciding how to apply a NA-QMD quantum algorithm in the future. At tcycle =1 μs per non-Clifford layer and δ = 0 .01 target logical error, total wall-clock time for a single shot is twall = Dtotal × tcycle , where Dtotal = nsteps × Dstep . For the phase gradient method, Dstep = Ω step (serial Toffoli count); for REQWIEM, Dstep = D(fan) R (fan-out-enabled non-Clifford depth). REQWIEM’s reduced Dtotal subsequently reduces single-shot wall times from minutes– days (phase gradient) to seconds–minutes (REQWIEM). Reduced depth also lowers the required surface code distance by 2–4 units, partially compensating for any additional logical qubits and reducing the physical qubit count. For three of the four benchmarks, REQWIEM requires fewer physical qubits than the phase gradient method (Table 5.15); the exception is Anth/C 60 (d = 246), where the 1 .15 × logical qubit penalty is partially offset by the reduced code distance. 166 With 10 4 shots for 1% population accuracy, the contrast is dramatic, with the phase gra-dient method requiring 28 days to 144 years, while REQWIEM requires 2 days to 186 days. The largest system (Anth/C 60 , d = 246) remains foreseeably intractable, though not cat-egorically infeasible, with a total simulation time reduction from 144 years to 186 days), representing a qualitative change in the simulation practicality. 5.8.5 Comprehensive per-step and total resource comparison 

Table 5.16 collects all per-step and total resource metrics for the four benchmark systems given in Motlagh et al. [43] under physical molecular symmetry. Per-step columns confirm the architectural analysis of Section 5.3: REQWIEM’s non-Clifford depth per step ( D(fan) R )is 10–283 × smaller than the phase gradient Toffoli count Ω step , while per-step T -gate count is 2–5 × larger (for m = 2–3). Multiplying by the common Trotter step count preserves these ratios exactly in the total columns. The total T -gate comparison reflects an m-dependent trade-off identified in Section 5.6.7: for m = 3 systems ((NO) 4-Anth, Dimer), REQWIEM’s total T -gate count exceeds the phase gradient approach by ∼ 5×; for m = 2 systems (Anth/C 60 ), the excess is ∼ 2.5×. The total depth comparison, by contrast, is uniformly favourable to REQWIEM across all systems, with advantages ranging from 10 × to 283 ×. This separation between T -gate cost (where 168 phase gradient methods are competitive or better) and depth (where REQWIEM dominates) is the central quantitative finding of this resource analysis. 

5.9 Quantum Advantage Assessment 

The per-step and total resource comparisons of Sections 5.3–5.8 establish the relative ef-ficiency of REQWIEM and phase gradient Trotterization. Practical relevance and the id-iomatic “million-dollar question” is the following: Does REQWIEM have a total execution time that is competitive with the classical state of the art? For nonadiabatic vibronic dynam-ics, the dominant classical method is multilayer multiconfigurational time-dependent Hartree (ML-MCTDH) [50, 206, 207], propagating the full nuclear wavefunction on a tensor-train– like tree of single-particle function (SPF) bases. This section compares the fault-tolerant wall-clock time of both quantum algorithms against ML-MCTDH runtime estimates for the benchmark systems of Section 5.2 and several projected targets, identifying the crossover regime in which quantum simulation becomes competitive. 

5.9.1 Classical–quantum comparison framework 

Classical ML-MCTDH propagation produces the full wavefunction |Ψ( t)⟩ in a single run: all electronic populations, coherences, and spectral observables are simultaneously available at no additional cost. A quantum simulation, by contrast, returns a single bitstring per shot; extracting an observable to precision εobs via direct sampling requires O(1 /ε 2obs ) repeated measurements. For 1% population accuracy ( εobs = 0 .01), this translates to ∼ 10 4 shots per observable. This shot overhead can be reduced using quantum amplitude estimation [208] (QAE), which achieves O(1 /ε obs ) total oracle queries at the cost of increasing circuit depth by 

O(1 /ε obs ). The algorithm constructs a Grover iterate Q = U S0 U† Sψ, where U is the time-evolution circuit and S0, Sψ are reflections; each application of Q requires one forward and 169 one inverse evolution, doubling the base circuit depth per iterate. Standard amplitude es-timation uses M = O(1 /ε obs ) sequential Grover iterates [208], yielding a maximum circuit depth of DQAE ≈ 2M × Dbase per shot. With ∼ 10 repetitions for confidence boosting, the total sequential wall time becomes 

tQAE ≈ 10 × 2M × Dbase × tcycle , (5.9.1) compared with tdirect = 10 4 × Dbase × tcycle for direct sampling. For εobs = 0 .01 ( M ≈ 100), this yields tQAE /t direct ≈ 1/5: a 5 × wall-time improvement. The deepest QAE circuit has ∼ 200 × the non-Clifford gate count of a single direct-sampling shot, necessitating a larger surface code distance to maintain the same target logical error rate δ = 0 .01. In practice, dcode increases by 2–4 (e.g., from 19 to 23 for pyrazine, or from 21 to 25 for the dimer), inflating the physical qubit count by ∼ 40%. The 5 × wall-time gain thus trades against ∼ 1.4× spatial overhead. Variants of QAE that do not have phase estimation, such as iterative quantum ampli-tude estimation (IQAE) [209] and maximum likelihood amplitude estimation (MLAE) [210], achieve the same asymptotic query complexity without controlled multi-qubit operations, but retain the O(1 /ε obs ) maximum circuit depth. Low-depth amplitude estimation algo-rithms [211] offer a tunable tradeoff N D = O(1 /ε 2obs ) between shot count N and maximum depth D, but this product is conserved: reducing circuit depth below O(1 /ε obs ) restores the classical shot count, yielding no net wall-time improvement. The 5 × gain from full-depth amplitude estimation therefore represents an estimated maximum achievable within the Heisenberg-limited measurement framework. Throughout Table 5.17, quantum runtimes are reported under both direct sampling (10 4

shots, base depth) and amplitude estimation ( ∼ 10 shots, 200 × depth), using tcycle = 1 μsper non-Clifford layer, δ = 0 .01, and the surface code distance dcode ≥ 2⌈log 10 (Dmax /δ )⌉ − 1appropriate for the deepest circuit in each protocol. All simulations use t = 100 fs, n = 10 grid qubits per mode, and ε = 1 .6 mE H.170 5.9.2 ML-MCTDH runtime estimates 

ML-MCTDH computational cost is governed by the tensor contraction at each integration step, scaling roughly as O(n p 

> SPF

deff nt), where nSPF is the number of single-particle functions per mode, p depends on the tree topology (typically p ∼ 2M for M electronic states at the top tensor), deff is the effective dimensionality after tree compression, and nt is the number of integrator time steps. The multi-layer tree structure handles weakly coupled bath modes efficiently, compressing d physical modes into deff ≈ dactive + dbath /f tree effective dimensions with tree compression factor ftree ∼ 5–20 [49]. The principal bottleneck is the electronic-state scaling: for M diabatic states, the top-layer A-tensor grows combinatorially with M and the per-mode SPF count, producing steep cost increases for M ≥ 4. Runtime estimates are bounded between optimistic and pessimistic scenarios (Ap-pendix M). ‘Optimistic’ estimates assume an efficient ML tree decomposition with converged SPF basis and sparse coupling topology. ‘Pessimistic’ estimates assume dense interstate cou-pling, slow SPF convergence, or topologies that resist efficient tree decomposition. These bounds are calibrated against published benchmarks: the 24-mode pyrazine propagation of Raab et al. [183], which completes in ∼ 1–30 min on modern hardware; spin-boson models with ∼ 100 bath modes requiring hours under ML-MCTDH [206]; and multi-state singlet fission chromophores ( d ∼ 20–30, M = 4–6) requiring days to weeks of CPU time for converged dynamics [212, 213].
