ing a vertical excitation. The width matches the harmonic zero-point amplitude σZPE =

pℏ/(mω OH ) = 0 .197 a0 to within 2%. The ( n + 1)-qubit register encodes four observable sectors defined by two qubits: the channel qubit selects the electronic surface ( |0⟩s → Vquart , S 1; |1⟩s → Vharm , S 0), while the most significant qubit (the “high bit”) of the position register selects the spatial half (|0⟩high → x < 0, enol well; |1⟩high → x > 0, keto well). At t = 0 the full population resides in the enol/S 1 sector. ESIPT corresponds to adiabatic population flow from enol/S 1

to keto/S 1, while electronic relaxation is the nonadiabatically-mediated property. The simulation uses n = 10 position qubits ( N = 1024 grid points, ∆ x = 4 .88 × 10 −3 a0)plus one ancilla qubit encoding the electronic surface. Time propagation employs a second-order (Strang) Trotter splitting, 

e−iHτ / ℏ ≈ e−iT τ / (2 ℏ) e−iV dτ / (2 ℏ) V2(τ )e−iV dτ / (2 ℏ) e−iT τ / (2 ℏ) , (7.5.3) 228 Figure 7.1: Potential energy surface schematic showing the asymmetric quartic (purple), harmonic (red), and Gaussian coupling V01 (×10, dashed green). The Franck–Condon point at x = −q0 sits on a slope directed toward the keto minimum. where the off-diagonal V2(τ ) is an exponentiated Gaussian. This equation takes a similar form to the XOR-fragmented term in Chapter 4, but a two-channel system only has one XOR class, reducing this term to V2(τ ) = e−iV od τ Step size is set to τ = 0 .1 a.u. (2 .4 as) for a total propagation time of T = 10 ,000 a.u. (242 fs). 

Exact Diagonalization Benchmark 

A Trotter-error-free reference is obtained by instantiating the two-surface Hamiltonian 7.5.1 on a real-space grid of N = 2048 points spanning x ∈ [−2.5, 2.5] a0 (∆ x ≈ 2.4 × 10 −3 a0). The kinetic energy is discretized with a three-point finite-difference stencil, yielding a real symmetric matrix of dimension 2 N × 2N = 4096 × 4096 with block structure 

H =



T + Vquart (x) V01 (x)

V01 (x) T + Vharm (x)

 . (7.5.4) 229 Full diagonalization returns the eigenspectrum {Ek, |χk⟩} , from which the autocorrelation function is obtained without time-stepping error as C(t) = P 

> k

|ck|2e−iE k t/ ℏ, where ck =

⟨χk|Ψ(0) ⟩. The spectrum is computed using the same Blackman window, exponential damp-ing ( τd = 5000 a.u.), and fourfold zero-padded FFT as the quantum circuit simulation, so that any differences between the two spectra arise solely from the Trotter decomposition. 

Trotter Error Analysis 

Second-order Strang splitting 7.5.3 introduces a per-step error governed by the nested com-mutators of the kinetic and potential operators [127]. For a wavepacket with root-mean-square momentum prms = ℏ/(2 δ) ≈ 2.5 a−10 (kinetic energy Tphys = p2rms /(2 m) = 46 meV) propagating on a potential of depth Vmax ≈ 3.75 eV, the per-step error magnitudes are 

ε2 ∼ τ 3

12 Tphys Vmax 

␀Vmax + Tphys 

 ≈ 5 × 10 −4 meV , (7.5.5) The physically relevant phase accumulation per step, τ p 2rms /(2 mℏ) = 1 .7×10 −3 rad, remains well within the stable regime. Over Nsteps = 10 5 steps, the symmetric structure of the Strang splitting ensures partial cancellation of odd-order error contributions, suppressing cumulative phase error relative to the first-order decomposition. 

Proton Transfer Dynamics 

Figure 7.2(b) presents the time-resolved population dynamics in the three principal register sectors: enol/S 1, keto/S 1, and total S 0.The proton transfer proceeds ballistically under the asymmetric quartic gradient. The keto/S 1 population rises monotonically from zero, reaching approximately 0 .53 at 

t ≈ 2000 a.u. (48 fs), at which point the enol and keto populations on S 1 are approximately equal. This population transfer is modulated by the coupling strength, with equal popula-tions representing an a point in time where the molecular configuration is in a superposition between tautomers. This timescale matches the experimental keto ∗ stimulated emission rise of 30–50 fs measured by femtosecond fluorescence up-conversion [256] and mid-infrared 230 Figure 7.2: Observable benchmarks for the HBT ESIPT model. (left) Autocorrelation spec-trum from |C(t)|: black, exact coupled diagonalization; red, isolated quartic; blue, second-order Trotterized quantum circuit ( τ = 0 .1 a.u., 10 position qubits + 1 ancilla qubit). The quartic progression is shifted by ∼5–15 meV by the Gaussian coupling; the discrepancy near 

−4.3 to −4.5 eV arises from avoided crossings between quartic states and harmonic m = 11. (right) Three-sector population dynamics from the Trotterized quantum circuit simulation: the keto/S 1 population rises to ∼0.53 at 48 fs, continuing to ∼0.91 by 97 fs, while the total S0 population remains below 5%. Coherent recurrences modulate the proton transfer with periods consistent with the dominant quartic eigenstate beat frequencies. transient absorption [257], confirming that the asymmetric quartic potential produces the correct ballistic driving force. Following the initial transfer, the wavepacket exhibits coherent oscillations between the two tautomers. The enol/S 1 population continues rising to a maximum of approximately 0.91 near t ≈ 4000 a.u. (97 fs), then drops sharply to ∼0.5 near t ≈ 5000 a.u. (121 fs) as the wavepacket partially returns to the keto well. A second recurrence carries the enol population back to ∼0.83 by t ≈ 7500 a.u. (181 fs), followed by a third partial return around 

t ≈ 9000 a.u. (218 fs). The oscillation amplitude is damped by dephasing among the multiply populated quartic eigenstates: the initial Franck–Condon wavepacket projects predominantly onto n = 2 ( |c2|2 = 0 .46), with strong secondary contributions from n = 3 ( |c3|2 = 0 .32) and n = 4 ( |c4|2 = 0 .13), spanning a spectral width of ∆ E = 46 meV. Dominant beat 231 frequencies between these levels predict recurrence periods of T2↔3 = 2 πℏ/∆E23 = 128 fs and T2↔4 = 57 fs, consistent with the observed oscillation timescales and within the 60–140 fs range of coherent vibrational dynamics reported in time-resolved fluorescence of HBT [256]. The total S 0 population (harmonic surface) remains below 10% throughout the 242 fs simulation, oscillating without secular growth. This suppression is consistent with the off-resonant Rabi picture. At the Franck–Condon point, the coupling-to-gap ratio is 

V01 (−q0)/∆Egap = 6 .9 meV /2.65 eV = 0 .003, predicting negligible transfer probability. The few-percent leakage arises when the wavepacket transiently passes through x ≈ 0 dur-ing proton migration, where the coupling peaks at 408 meV against a gap of 3 .75 eV, giving (V / ∆) 2 ≈ 0.012. The S 0 population distributes approximately equally between the enol and keto spatial halves, indicating that electronic leakage occurs primarily in the barrier region where V01 is maximal, and the leaked amplitude then spreads symmetrically on the harmonic surface. This confirms that proton transfer proceeds largely adiabatically on S 1

with only small, though non-negligible nonadiabatic leakage, consistent with the strongly coupled regime of intramolecular proton transfer [265]. ESIPT systems that have significant nonadiabatic character will have a stronger coupling constant, tunable as a single value in classical preprocessing. 

Spectral Benchmarks 

Figure 7.2(a) compares autocorrelation spectra from three calculations: exact diagonaliza-tion of the full coupled two-surface Hamiltonian (black), exact diagonalization of the isolated quartic potential (red), and the second-order Trotterized quantum circuit simulation of the full Hamiltonian (green). In the main quartic progression between −3.7 and −4.2 eV, the three traces are in close agreement, with peak positions matching to within the spectral res-olution. The quartic-only spectrum (red) is displaced by ∼2–15 meV from the coupled result (black), reflecting perturbative level shifts induced by the Gaussian coupling; the Trotter-ized circuit (blue) tracks the coupled reference with comparable fidelity. The most significant 232 discrepancy between the traces appears in the noise floor. Isolated features attributable to harmonic-surface eigenstates appear in the coupled diagonalization (black) near −3.2 eV, where they are absent from the quartic-only trace, confirming the expected redistribution of spectral weight by the interchromophore coupling. The quantum circuit Trotterization is not initialized with a momentum ( p0 = 0), making these states largely inaccessible and not present in the spectrum. 

7.5.2 Morse-Repulsive System: NaI Model Model Specification 

The Morse-repulsive model is parameterized after sodium iodide (NaI), with femtosecond photodissociation dynamics first resolved by Zewail et al. [266, 267] and is a standard bench-mark for nonadiabatic wavepacket methods [268–270]. The model has one coordinate ( d = 1), being the Na–I internuclear separation R, and two diabatic electronic states ( M = 2): an ionic ground state (Na +I−, X 1Σ+) modeled by a Morse potential, and a covalent excited state (Na ∗ + I) modeled by a repulsive exponential. The diabatic Hamiltonian is 

H = p2

2μ 12 +



Vionic (R) V01 (R)

V01 (R) Vcov (R)

 , (7.5.6) with μ = 35 ,480 a.u. (19 .464 amu, the Na–I reduced mass) and 

Vionic (R) = De

1 − e−β(R−Re)2 − De + ∆ E∞, (7.5.7a) 

Vcov (R) = A e −αR , (7.5.7b) 

V01 (R) = W0 exp −γ (R − Rc)2. (7.5.7c) The energy zero is the covalent asymptote Na(3 s 2S1/2) + I( 2P3/2). The Morse dissociation limit is therefore the ionic asymptote ∆ E∞ = IP(Na) − EA(I) = 2 .080 eV [271], and the Morse minimum lies at ∆ E∞ − De = −1.12 eV below the covalent zero. All parameters are collected in Table 7.4. 233 Equilibrium bond length Re = 5 .122 a0 is taken from microwave spectroscopy [272], with well depth De = 3 .20 eV (0 .1176 Eh) from photofragment translational spectroscopy [273] corrected for zero-point energy. The Morse exponent β = ωe

pμ/ (2 De) = 0 .459 a−10 repro-duces the harmonic frequency ωe = 259 cm −1 [272, 274]. The repulsive amplitude A = 0 .1236 Eh (3 .363 eV) and decay constant α = 0 .04288 a−10

are jointly constrained the vertical excitation energy at Re,∆Eabs = Vcov (Re) − Vionic (Re) = A e −αR e − (∆ E∞ − De) = 0 .1404 Eh ≈ 3.82 eV (325 nm) ,

(7.5.8) matching the experimental absorption maximum [275]. They are also constrained by the dia-batic crossing at the Coulomb radius Rc = 1 /∆E∞ = 13 .1 a0, giving Vionic (Rc) = Vcov (Rc) = 1.918 eV. The decay constant α = 0 .04288 a−10 reflects Na ∗ + I covalent repulsion, whose range is set by the Na(3 s) valence orbital radius. Gaussian coupling (7.5.7c) is centered at the crossing radius Rc = 13 .1 a0. The amplitude 

W0 = 3 .85 × 10 −4 Eh (10 .5 meV) is chosen to reproduce the experimentally observed per-crossing escape fraction of ∼ 11% [266, 267], as verified by the Landau–Zener calculation in Section 7.5.2. The adiabatic gap at the crossing is 2 W0 = 21 meV. The width γ = 0 .01 a−20

confines the nonadiabatic interaction to within ∆ R ≈ 1/√2γ ≈ 7 a0 of the crossing. The initial state is a coherent equal-amplitude superposition of both diabatic surfaces, testing propagation fidelity on both surfaces and benchmarking nonadiabatic population transfer at the crossing. The simulation box spans R ∈ [0 , L ] with L = 100 a0 and N = 2 12 = 4096 grid points (∆ R = 0 .0244 a0), using n = 12 position qubits plus one channel qubit. The propagation time t = 10 5 a.u. (2 .42 ps) with Trotter step τ = 1 .0 a.u. (24 .2 as) captures at least two nonadiabatic crossing events. 234 Table 7.4: Model parameters for the NaI Morse-repulsive system in atomic units. Predicted Dynamics and Spectral Signatures 

The covalent component accelerates outward on the repulsive surface. Classical integration of the equations of motion gives an arrival time at Rc of tarr ≈ 11 ,900 a.u. (288 fs) with radial velocity

= e−0.128 = 0 .88 , (7.5.10) corresponding to a per-crossing trapping probability of Ptrap = 1 − Pesc ≈ 12%, consistent with the experimental value of ∼ 11% [266–268]. The Gaussian coupling region spans ∆ R ≈ 7 a0. With a mean velocity of vc = 1 .27 ×

10 −3 a.u., the wavepacket requires ∆ ttrav ≈ 5,500 a.u. (133 fs) to traverse it. Free-particle spreading time tsp = 2 μδ 2 ≈ 1,600 a.u. is much shorter than the transit time, giving a spatial width at Rc of σ(tarr ) ≈ 1.1 a0. The crossing event therefore extends over ∼5,500–6,000 a.u., producing a temporally resolved population-transfer ramp. The ∼ 12% of the nonadiabatically transferred covalent state at Rc is deposited into high-lying Morse vibrational states. The crossing energy Vionic (Rc) = 1 .918 eV lies 3 .04 eV above the Morse minimum. The outer turning point of v = 154 lies at Rout = 13 .1 a0 ≈ Rc: the trapped wavepacket’s classical orbit grazes the crossing radius at every oscillation, enabling Zewail-type recurrent population transfer. Following Zewail (1993) [266], a “staircase” population transfer requires a dephasing time not less than the mean oscillation period. At v = 154, TMorse ≈ 23 ,700 a.u. (573 fs), being a factor of 4 .4× longer than the harmonic period 2 π/ω e = 5 ,300 a.u. The Gaussian coupling deposits population over a range of v-states spanning the energy width ∆ E ≈ | dV ionic /dR |Rc ×

∆R ≈ 520 meV ( ± 70 vibrational quanta). The classical period varies significantly ( T ≈

9,000 a.u. at v = 80 to T ≈ 700 ,000 a.u. at v = 195). The resulting dephasing time, 

tdeph = π/ ∆ω ≈ 4,600 a.u. (111 fs), is 5 × shorter than the mean oscillation period. The trapped wavepacket loses spatial coherence before a single classical orbit [277], disallowing a clean staircase structure. Individual v-components still follow outer turning points and traverse the coupling region, but arrive at staggered times, producing a continuous, sub-percent population drift rather than resolved steps. 236 The main covalent fraction ( ∼ 88% of the initial covalent population) that escapes past 

Rc continues outward with asymptotic velocity v∞ = p2 Vcov (Re)/μ = 2 .37 × 10 −3 a.u. This fragment reflects from the “hard wall” R = L, formed by the back of the diabatic surfaces, and returns through Rc after a round-trip time

≈ 73 ,500 a.u. (L = 100 a0), (7.5.11) producing a second population-transfer step near t ≈ tarr + Trt ≈ 85 ,000 a.u. A larger box would push this recurrence beyond the simulation window ( Trt ≈ 116 ,000 a.u. > T ), recovering single-crossing behavior.

Observable Benchmarks 

Figure 7.3(right) shows diabatic populations from the circuit emulation. Both surfaces begin at P = 0 .50 by construction. Two discrete population-transfer events are visible: 1. First crossing (t ≈ 12 ,000 a.u., 290 fs). The covalent wavepacket reaches Rc and transfers ∆ P ≈ 0.065 to the ionic surface, raising Pionic from 0 .500 to 0 .565. The effective trapping fraction ∆ P/P cov = 0 .065 /0.50 = 13% agrees with the Landau– Zener prediction of 12%. 2. Box-reflection recurrence (t ≈ 90 ,000 a.u., 2 .18 ps). The covalent fragment returns through Rc and transfers an additional ∆ P ≈ 0.059. The ratio of successive step heights ∆P2/∆P1 = 0 .059 /0.065 = 0 .91 agrees with the expected geometric decay Pesc = 0 .88: each crossing removes the same fraction (12%) of the remaining covalent population. The step separation of ∼ 78 ,000 a.u. is consistent with the round-trip estimate (7.5.11) of 73 ,500 a.u. The inset in Figure 7.3(right) reveals the first Landau–Zener event. Rather than a sharp step, the ionic population rises over a ramp spanning ∼ 6,000 a.u. (145 fs), consistent with the time for the dispersed wavepacket ( σ ≈ 1.1 a0 at Rc) to traverse the 7 a0-wide coupling region at vc = 1 .27 ×10 −3 a.u. A gradual upward drift of ∼ 0.001 in Pionic over ∼ 50 ,000 a.u. is clearly visible, coming from the ∼ 6% of population trapped onto high-lying ionic Morse states (v ≈ 80–180) at the first crossing. Trapped v-components oscillate with their own classical period and individually traverse Rc at staggered times, indicating anharmonic dephasing. Figure 7.3(left) shows the absorption spectrum obtained from the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function. Under the FFT convention ( ˜C(f ) = P 

> n

C(tn) e−2πif t n ), spectral 238 peaks appear at displayed energies Edisp = −Ek × Eh + Eoffset , where Eoffset = 2 .70 eV is the vertical excitation gap. 1. Morse vibrational progression (3.0–3.85 eV). A comb of narrow peaks corresponds to the ionic Morse vibrational states v = 0 , 1, 2, . . . The v = 0 peak at 3 .80 eV carries the largest weight ( |c0|2 ≈ 0.48). Peak spacing of ∼ 32 meV (259 cm −1) reflects ωe, with the Franck–Condon envelope decaying steeply: v = 1 is suppressed by ∼ 20 × relative to v = 0, and states beyond v ≈ 5 fall below 10 −6 relative intensity. 2. (1.7–2.5 eV). A deep minimum separates the Morse progression from the covalent continuum. The minimum at ∼ 1.86 eV maps to Morse v ≈ 50, which carries negligible Franck–Condon weight from either surface. The exact benchmark reaches ∼ 10 −11 in this region. The circuit emulation shows a smooth floor at ∼ 10 −6 to 10 −8, reflecting Trotter error raising the noise floor. 3. Covalent continuum ( ∼ 0 eV). The broad feature centered near Edisp ≈ 0 arises from the covalent half of the initial state. Vcov (Re) = 2 .70 eV maps to Edisp ≈ 0 under the sign inversion, populating a range of classical scattering states in the continuum. A peak at Edisp = 2 .70 eV corresponds to the f = 0 (DC) bin of the FFT. This feature shifts or vanishes under alternative DC-removal strategies and carries no physical content. The circuit emulation’s spectrum agrees with the exact benchmark for all features above 

∼ 10 −5 relative intensity. Below this, the circuit trace exhibits a smooth noise floor, arising from spectral weight leakage. 

7.5.3 Modified H´ enon-Heiles Potential Model Specification 

The modified H´ enon-Heiles system tests the multilinear expansion on an inseparable mul-tidimensional potential surface. Any function f on the grid {0, . . . , 2n−1} × { 0, . . . , 2m−1}

Extending from the univariate case, since the binary encoding maps the grid bijectively to {0, 1}n+m, any function on the grid has a unique expansion in this basis. The circuit consists of at most 2 n1+··· +nd multi-controlled Rz gates, each with controls drawn from up to d registers simultaneously. The H´ enon-Heiles system, when decomposed into gates using the multivariate expansion, introduces rotation angles that are zero. Counting the nonzero terms shows that a multivari-ate multilinear expansion, if reducible, will output a minimum number of rotation angles to construct the circuit, corresponding to the minimum number of controlled operations. The model comprises two coupled nuclear modes ( d = 2) with n = 12 qubits per mode (24 total qubits, 4096 × 4096 grid), simulated by GPU-accelerated state-vector emulation on ideal, noiseless qubits via cuStateVec [278]. The potential is 

V (x, y ) = 12 k(x2 + y2) + λ(x2y − y3/3) + ε (x2 + y2)3 , (7.5.18) where the standard H´ enon-Heiles cubic coupling λ(x2y − y3/3) is the inseparable interaction, and the sextic regularization ε (x2 + y2)3 maintains bounded classical dynamics at energies near and above the classical escape energy Eesc = k3/(6 λ2). Parameters are collected in Table 7.5. Time evolution employs symmetric second-order Trotter splitting (Strang splitting), al-ternating half-step potential phases e−iτ V / 2 with a full-step kinetic propagator e−iτ T applied in momentum space via the centered QFT. The kinetic energy uses the gate-based polynomial decomposition (Tier 1–2 of Chapter 4), while the potential energy is applied as a diagonal phase operator on the position register. This is the Trotter structure of the REQWIEM architecture with M = 0 channels, now applied to a two-register inseparable potential. 241 Table 7.5: Parameters for the modified H´ enon-Heiles model. Parameter Symbol Value Qubits per mode n 12 (4096 grid points per axis) Total qubits ntot 24 Mass μ 2000 a.u. Harmonic constant k 0.2 a.u. Anharmonic coupling λ 0.09–0.10 a.u. Sextic regularization ε 10 −4–10 −5 a.u. Box length Lx = Ly 16–30 a.u. Wavepacket width σ 0.2–0.3 a.u. Time step τ 0.5 a.u. Propagation time T 500–10,000 a.u. Figure 7.4 presents an overview of the system. The modified H´ enon-Heiles potential (upper right) exhibits C 3 symmetry with three saddle points at radius rsaddle = k/λ from the origin, through which the wavepacket can escape when its energy exceeds Eesc . The red dashed contour marks the classical escape energy. An instantaneous snapshot of the position-space probability density (upper left) captures the wavepacket partially escaping through all three exit channels, confirming that the inseparable cubic coupling correctly routes amplitude along the symmetry-equivalent valleys. The corresponding momentum-space density (lower left), obtained by applying the QFT to both registers, displays the conjugate six-fold structure reflecting the three preferred directions of outward flow and three of inward return. The time-averaged density (lower right) accumulates probability over the full propagation interval, revealing the triangular confinement geometry and enhanced density along the classical periodic orbits connecting the potential center to each saddle point. 242 Recurrence Structure and Classical Correspondence 

The inseparability of the H´ enon-Heiles potential prevents factorization into single-coordinate functions g(x) · h(y), making the system available as a benchmark for multidimensional potentials with nontrivial classical phase-space structure [279]. This section validates the circuit-generated dynamics by comparing quantum recurrence timescales to the classical periodic orbit of the y-axis, quantifying the expected deviations from dimensional effects and eigenstate dephasing. The y-axis ( x = 0) is an invariant subspace of the H´ enon-Heiles dynamics: since 

∂V /∂x |x=0 = 2 λ · 0 · y = 0 for all y, a trajectory initialized with x = 0, px = 0 remains on the y-axis for all time. The motion reduces to a one-dimensional oscillation in the effective potential V (0 , y ) = 12 ky 2 − 13 λy 3 + εy 6, whose period is uniquely determined by the energy. At E = 0 .10 a.u., the asymmetric turning points are ymin = −1.00 and ymax = 1 .61 a.u., reflecting the cubic term that softens the potential for y > 0 and steepens it for y < 0. The classical period, obtained by symplectic integration from the upper turning point, is 

Tcl = 712 .0 a.u. Full variational integration of the 20-dimensional orbit-plus-monodromy system yields the Gutzwiller invariants [280]: classical action S = 69 .2, Maslov index μ = 4 (from four zero crossings of det(Φ qq ) per period), stability type “stable” with Greene’s residue R = 0 .030. Symplecticity is preserved to machine precision (det(Φ) = 1 .000), and the trivial eigenvalue pair deviation |λ − 1| = 1 .3 × 10 −3 confirms closure of the periodic orbit. The anharmonic period dispersion curve T (E) (Figure 7.6, upper panel), computed at 300 energies by sym-plectic integration, increases monotonically ( dT /dE = 1413 a.u.), a negative anharmonicity inherited from the cubic term. Figure 7.5 presents the autocorrelation function C(t) = |⟨ ψ(0) |ψ(t)⟩| 2 and its power spectrum for an initial Gaussian wavepacket at E = 0 .10 a.u. ( E/E esc = 0 .61). The autocor-relation exhibits a strong initial recurrence near t ≈ 663 a.u., with a scar strength (maximum recurrence amplitude divided by the ergodic baseline ¯C ≈ 0.022) of 11 .4×. Subsequent re-243 currence peaks arrive at times slightly earlier than integer multiples of this fundamental period, a contraction effect analyzed below. The power spectrum identifies a dominant peak at TFFT = 694 a.u., which represents a time-averaged frequency over the full propagation window and differs from the first-recurrence period due to dephasing dynamics. Secondary spectral peaks appear at Trec = 347, 231, 173, 130, 109, 95, 77, and 63 a.u. The first quantum recurrence occurs at Tfirst ≈ 663 a.u., establishing a clear offset from the classical y-axis orbit period: 

Tfirst ≈ 663 < T FFT = 694 < T cl = 712 a.u. (7.5.19) This 6 .9% shift to shorter quantum periods rules out the standard anharmonic dispersion mechanism, which would predict Tquantum > T cl for a potential with dT /dE > 0, and instead reflects the transverse stiffening of the effective potential by the cubic coupling. The classical orbit is confined to the one-dimensional invariant subspace x = 0, where it experiences only V (0 , y ). The quantum wavepacket, by contrast, has finite width σ =0.15 a.u. in both coordinates and samples the full two-dimensional potential from the first timestep. The cross-term λx 2y generates a transverse curvature ∂2V /∂x 2 = k + 2 λy , which adds a correction 2 λy to the harmonic curvature k = 0 .2. At the time-averaged displacement 

⟨y⟩ ≈ 0.8 a.u. along the orbit, this correction is 2 λ⟨y⟩ ≈ 0.14, a 72% enhancement of the transverse confinement. Since the wavepacket spends the majority of each oscillation cycle in the y > 0 region (the upper turning point ymax = 1 .61 is 60% farther from the origin than 

|ymin | = 1 .00), the time-averaged transverse stiffening is net positive and substantial. The stiffer effective potential increases the eigenstate spacings ∆ En of the full two-dimensional Hamiltonian relative to those of the one-dimensional invariant-subspace Hamiltonian, short-ening the beat periods 2 π/ ∆En and producing quantum recurrences at T < T cl .For comparison, the sextic regularization contributes less than 0 .2% of the potential energy at the turning points ( εy 6 ≈ 1.7 × 10 −4 a.u. versus Vtotal = 0 .10 a.u.) and a curvature correction 30 εy 4 ≈ 2×10 −3 that is two orders of magnitude smaller than the cubic transverse 244 stiffening. The sextic term serves a structural role (ensuring a fully discrete spectrum at all energies) but does not contribute to the observed period shift at E/E esc = 0 .61. Peak-by-peak analysis of the autocorrelation function (Figure 7.6, lower panels) reveals a contracting recurrence structure: successive peaks arrive at times slightly earlier than integer multiples of Tfirst , indicating that the effective inter-peak period decreases with each subsequent recurrence. This contraction reflects the progressive isolation of the dominant eigenstate spacing by dephasing. The initial wavepacket projects onto multiple eigenstates whose pairwise beat frequen-cies span a range δω . The first recurrence reflects the population-weighted average of all contributing eigenstate spacings, producing a period Tfirst ≈ 663 a.u. that represents the full coherent superposition. At later times, eigenstate pairs whose beat frequencies are incom-mensurate with the dominant spacing destructively interfere, and the surviving constructive interference is increasingly dominated by the single most strongly populated pair. If this dom-inant pair has a slightly larger spacing ∆ E (shorter period) than the population-weighted mean, the effective recurrence period contracts toward the spacing of the dominant pair. The time-averaged FFT period TFFT = 694 a.u. does not correspond to a single physi-cal timescale but represents an intermediate value biased by the window function and the time-varying coherence. When the C 3-symmetric green reference lines are placed at multi-ples of Tfirst ≈ 663 a.u. rather than at TFFT , the first recurrence peak aligns to within the spectral resolution, while later peaks progressively precede the reference, consistent with the contracting inter-peak period. The close agreement between the first quantum recurrence and the classical orbit period (within 7%), combined with the correct sign of the transverse stiffening correction, the ex-pected contraction dynamics from multistate dephasing, and the reproduction of the correct C3-symmetric escape topology, provides strong evidence that the two-register Trotterized propagation faithfully reproduces the inseparable multidimensional dynamics of the H´ enon-Heiles system. For comparison, a Trotter error large enough to produce a 7% period shift 245 would require a step size τ ≳ 5 a.u., an order of magnitude larger than the simulation value 

τ = 0 .5 a.u. 

Quantum Scarring 

The time-averaged position density ¯ ρ(x, y ) accumulated over the propagation interval is compared to the microcanonical ergodic density ρmc ∝ pE − V (x, y ) to identify quantum scarring signatures [281]. The scar excess ratio log 10 (¯ ρ/ρ mc ) localizes regions of enhanced (red) or depleted (blue) probability density relative to the ergodic baseline. Figure 7.7 presents the scar excess for five initial energies spanning the range E/E esc =0.30 to 1 .09. At low energies ( E = 0 .05 a.u.), the scar excess is strongly concentrated along the y-axis linear orbit, with the wavepacket density exceeding the ergodic prediction by up to two orders of magnitude along this trajectory. As the initial energy increases, the classically accessible region expands and the scar pattern evolves: at E = 0 .10 a.u. ( E/E esc = 0 .61), secondary scar features associated with the triangular orbit family become visible at the lower vertices of the potential. Near the escape threshold ( E = 0 .15 and 0 .18 a.u.), the scar excess broadens as the wavepacket begins to access the exit channels, and the triangular scarring intensifies as the classical dynamics approaches the homoclinic tangle of the saddle points. The scar strength, quantified by the kurtosis excess of ¯ ρ/ρ mc (lower right panel of Fig-ure 7.7), decreases monotonically with increasing energy [281]. This trend is consistent with the expected transition from regular to chaotic classical dynamics in the H´ enon-Heiles sys-tem: at low energies, phase space is dominated by stable islands surrounding the periodic orbits, producing strong localization; as E → Eesc , the chaotic sea expands and the wave-function distributes more uniformly, reducing the scar contrast. The circuit-generated scar maps reproduce the known energy dependence of quantum scarring in this system, con-firming that the Trotterized two-register propagation correctly captures the semiclassical localization structure. 246 Circuit Sparsity and Gate Counts 

The H´ enon-Heiles potential (Eq. 7.5.18), despite being inseparable, is a finite-degree polyno-mial. The harmonic term is degree 2 (Tier 1–2 in the REQWIEM decomposition), the cubic coupling is degree 3, and the sextic regularization is degree 6. When the two-register multi-linear M¨ obius expansion (Eq. 7.5.14) is applied to this potential, the expansion coefficients 

cS,T are exactly zero for all subsets with |S| + |T | > 6 (or |S| + |T | > 3 if the sextic term is omitted). The full 2 nx+ny = 2 24 ≈ 1.7 × 10 7 coefficient expansion collapses to Pak=0 

␀nk



nonzero terms, where a is the polynomial degree per coordinate. Figure 7.8 quantifies this reduction. This calculation uses commuting tensor products to reduce the number of gates where possible, extending to long-double precision (80-bit with 64-bit mantissa) for higher accuracy. A threshold value of 10 −16 was set for numerical stability, with only extremely small values contributing to the asymptotically flat behavior of f (x, y ) = 1/(xy ) being truncated. The left panel displays the coefficient magnitude distribution from the M¨ obius transform, organized by subset order |S| + |T |. The left panel shows the nonzero fraction of coefficients, corresponding to the fraction of gates that need be applied for the 2D potential. Separable quadratic functions retain O(n2) gate scaling, and separable reciprocal functions retain O(2 n) scaling. The unmodified H´ enon-Heiles potential has a cubic inter-mode term ∝ x2y that significantly increases gate count, but retains polynomial complexity as O(n3), while the modified variant scales as O(n6). A simple example of a maximally structured inseparable potential is f (x, y ) = 1 /(xy ), requiring all 4 n gates have nonzero coefficients. The right panel shows the corresponding rotation gate count as a function of register size n, comparing the full 2 2n expansion (dashed) against the polynomial-reduced count (solid), confirming the collapse from exponential to polynomial scaling. This validates the central sparsity claim of the hybrid strategy (Section 7.4.3): the general 

O(2 n) multilinear expansion, when applied to a degree-a polynomial potential, automatically recovers the O(na) scaling of the dedicated polynomial decomposition. Table 7.6 gives nu-merical values for representative register sizes. 247 Table 7.6: Nonzero coefficients for the modified H´ enon-Heiles model at various n. The count matches the polynomial site prediction exactly, confirming automatic sparsity recovery. The cubic-only column gives the count for ε = 0 (degree 3); the full column includes the sextic regularization (degree 6). 

n 22n (full expansion) Cubic only ( a = 3) Full ( a = 6) Sparsity 7 16,384 238 1,869 88.6% 8 65,536 352 4,636 92.9% 10 1,048,576 680 21,044 98.0% 12 16,777,216 1,168 71,150 99.6% The H´ enon-Heiles system also clarifies the interaction between single-register and cross-register terms. The harmonic part 12 k(x2 +y2) is separable and produces only |Sx| ≤ 2, |Sy| =0 and |Sx| = 0, |Sy| ≤ 2 terms. The cubic coupling λ(x2y−y3/3) produces cross-register terms with |Sx| + |Sy| ≤ 3, including the genuinely inseparable sites |Sx| ≥ 1, |Sy| ≥ 1. The sextic regularization ε(x2 + y2)3 produces terms up to |Sx| + |Sy| = 6, partially separable under algebraic expansion. Tier 2 ′ dominates the total site count at n = 12, but the exponential suppression of higher-order coefficients for polynomial potentials ensures that the circuit depth remains O(poly( n)) rather than O(2 n). 

7.6 Summary 7.6.1 Scope of Application 

The REQWIEM architecture, as extended in this chapter, can simulate any molecular Hamil-tonian of the form 

H = T + X

> s

|s⟩⟨ s| ⊗ V (s)(x) + X

> i<j

(|i⟩⟨ j| + |j⟩⟨ i|) ⊗ Gij (x) , (7.6.1) where T is the kinetic energy operator (separable in Cartesian coordinates), V (s)(x) is the on-diagonal potential on a diabatic surface s, and Gij (x) are off-diagonal coupling functions. The only structural requirement is that the coordinates are encoded in position registers 248 via the standard binary encoding, such that the kinetic operator T remains dependent on momentum p, not position x. Chapter 9 discusses coordinate transformations and variable placement to maintain this condition, as commonly exhibited in many-body systems. On-diagonal and off-diagonal potentials can be arbitrary combinations of polynomials of any degree, handled by the binary decomposition for degree ≤ 2 and by the multilinear (or custom) expansion for degree > 2; transcendental functions of single coordinates; inverse polynomials and rational functions, treated via analytical, rational arithmetic as established in Chapter 7; and tabulated ab initio data with no analytical form required (one is guaran-teed on a discretized finite interval). Different functional forms can coexist within the same Trotter step: harmonic modes use the O(n2) polynomial decomposition, dissociative or re-active modes use the O(2 n) multilinear expansion, and inverse polynomial modes use exact rational arithmetic, multiplexed through the same UCR protocol and fan-out infrastructure. The span of physical problems accessible to REQWIEM therefore currently includes nonadiabatic photochemistry (internal conversion, intersystem crossing, conical intersection dynamics), molecule–surface scattering with Morse/exponential potentials and analytical coupling functions, gas-phase reactive scattering on multiple Born–Oppenheimer surfaces, Coulombic and long-range charge-transfer dynamics, and model Hamiltonians (spin–boson, Holstein, Peierls) where bath spectral densities determine potential shapes that need not be polynomial. 

7.6.2 Comparison with Existing Methods 

The smooth-potential extension positions REQWIEM relative to QROM methods, including a recent proposal for Morse potentials [43, 251]. The full architectural comparison for vibronic dynamics, including the per-term non-Clifford overhead αsmooth = (2 + b)/M , the fan-out parallelism ϕ ≈ d/ 2, and the T -gate crossover analysis, is given in Chapter 5. The combined advantage (2 + b) d/ (2 M ) grows linearly with both the phase register precision and the mode count, and holds for any functional form. 249 QROM caching optimization of Ref. [251] fails for non-separable surface-dependent po-tentials, when potentials motivate a smooth-potential extension. For polynomial terms where the caching optimization succeeds, the two architectures have comparable per-term overhead, and the REQWIEM advantage reduces to the fan-out factor alone. A broader comparison against QSP with Wigner–Haagerup LCU block encoding, QSP with kinetic–potential split-ting, QSP in a truncated Fock space, and the end-to-end estimate of Eklund et al. [112], is given in Chapter 8. That analysis finds T -gate ratios ranging from 1 .5× (QSP Fock) to 

∼ 3.1 × 10 9× (Eklund et al.) on the pyrazine benchmark, with the gap amplifying at relaxed synthesis precision due to REQWIEM’s optional-Toffoli architecture. 

7.6.3 Limitations 

The O(2 n) site count per analytical function per mode is the dominant cost of the smooth-potential extension. This exponential scaling is information-theoretic and shared with the QROM approach, being a well-known bottleneck of first-quantized, grid-based ap-proaches [84, 282]. As a chosen representation, merely placing the system on a grid defined by qubit basis states does not change this bottleneck, though often branded as O(N ) scaling, where N = 2 n. The limitation is mitigated given an observation in molecular modeling, where most molecular systems require analytical functions on only a small number of reactive modes ( N (κ) 

> f

= 1 for ∼1–6 modes), with remaining modes handled at polynomial cost. The kinetic energy operator separability enabling QFT-based kinetic propagator requires Cartesian coordinates. Curvilinear coordinates introduce coordinate-dependent kinetic en-ergy terms that are not diagonal in any simple basis (e.g. Ammonia, [283]). Extending REQWIEM to naturally consider curvilinear coordinates is nontrivial and is not addressed in the present work. REQWIEM is intrinsically a Trotterized time evolution method. Second-order symmetric Trotter splitting incurs error O(τ 3) per step, requiring NTrotter = O(t/τ ) steps for total propagation time t. If advances in qubitization, quantum signal processing, or lower-error 250 split-operator methods are integrated with nonadiabatic coupling, fan-out, and coherent evolution, a more practical NA-QMD algorithm may result for future quantum computers [35, 36]. Perhaps most importantly, gate counts and depths assume ideal gate execution. The fault-tolerant T -depth analysis of Chapter 5 accounts for rotation synthesis overhead but does not include surface code, error-correcting code costs, or routing overhead. The relative compari-son between REQWIEM and QROM is preserved under any uniform compilation strategy, with an aforementioned note on the excess routing costs brought by QROM (Chapter 5). 

7.6.4 Conclusion 

Multilinear M¨ obius expansion allows any real-valued function of the nuclear coordinates to be encoded as a diagonal phase operator on the vibrational register, multiplexed across electronic surfaces via the UCR angle vector, and executed in parallel across vibrational modes via channel-register fan-out. The harmonic regime of Chapters 4 and 5 is recovered as a special case aS = 0 for |S| ≥ 3; the inverse polynomial regime of Chapter 6 is recovered as the special case where exact rational arithmetic eliminates floating-point error; and the general smooth-potential regime of this chapter subsumes both. The three claims stated in Section 7.1 are established. A multilinear expansion provides a closed-form, truncation-free decomposition of arbitrary f (x) into 2 n diagonal phase oper-ators, implementable as CNOT cascades with a single Rz (or UCR (m) 

> Z

) rotation per term. UCR composition absorbs surface-dependent coefficients a(s) 

> S

into the angle vector at zero gate overhead, providing a factor-of-M composition advantage over QROM methods for non-separable analytical potentials. Depth advantage is universal, holding for all functional forms, amplifies with the introduction of analytical potentials ( αsmooth ≥ αpoly ), and is preserved under additive decomposition of Hamiltonians containing both polynomial and analytical terms. 251 The smooth-potential extension transforms REQWIEM into a general-purpose architec-ture for quantum simulation of nuclear dynamics on arbitrary potential energy surfaces. The architecture is now limited not by the functional form of the potential but by the grid res-olution ( n ≤ 14–15), the Cartesian coordinate restriction of the kinetic operator, and the Trotter step count. Prior to Chapter 9, which considers the complete REQWIEM framework holistically, Chapter 8 provides a quantitativecomparison of the Trotter-based architecture against QSP-based alternatives, establishing that the depth and T -gate advantages demon-strated for QROM methods extend when measured against QSP and qubitization. Chapter 9 then considers future quantum hardware application in the fault-tolerant era, future research directions for further optimization, and how the developed architecture extends to quantum mechanical problems placed in a finite basis representation (FBR) / discrete variable repre-sentation (DVR) dualism. 252 Figure 7.4: Overview of the modified H´ enon-Heiles system at n = 12 qubits per mode (24 total qubits). At n = 12, the reduction exceeds 99.6%, from ∼1.7 × 10 7

to ∼71 ,000 gates. 257 Chapter 8 Comparison with Polynomial Quantum Simulation Methods 

The preceding chapters developed REQWIEM as an oracle-free, Trotter-based propagator for nonadiabatic quantum molecular dynamics on diabatic potential energy surfaces. Gate counts were derived in Chapter 5, circuit depth was analyzed in Section 5.1, and the extension to arbitrary smooth potentials was completed in Chapter 7. A natural question resides in the “oracle-free” nature itself, and whether powerful algorithms for Hamiltonian simulation like quantum signal processing (QSP) could achieve lower cost for the same physical problem [35, 37]. QSP achieves optimal query complexity for Hamiltonian simulation: the number of calls to a block-encoding oracle scales as O(αt + log(1 /ε )), where α is the block-encoding normaliza-tion and t the evolution time [35, 36]. Translation from query complexity to gate complexity, then calculating T-gates and T-depth requires specifying the oracle, where block-encoding costs for a diabatic Hamiltonian on a position-space grid become the dominant expense. As Childs et al. note, product formulas can outperform asymptotically optimal methods precisely when commutator structure makes the Hamiltonian “easier than its norm sug-gests” [127]. This chapter decomposes this note in model systems. This chapter provides a quantitative, five-way comparison between: (i) REQWIEM (Chapters 2–7), (ii) QSP with Walsh–Hadamard LCU block encoding (WH-LCU), (iii) QSP with a two-term T +V split block encoding, (iv) QSP in the Fock (harmonic oscillator) basis, 258 (v) the full-Coulomb end-to-end algorithm of Eklund et al. [112]. All comparisons use the pyrazine S1/S 2 four-mode QVC model of Raab et al. [183] at converged parameters ( d = 4 modes, M = 2 electronic states, n = 10 qubits per mode, 

t = 4000 a.u., ε = 10 −3), with physical parameters given in Appendix H. The chapter concludes with a consideration of the non-perturbative convergence barrier near conical in-tersections and its implications for perturbative and polynomial simulation methods. 

8.1 QSP on Grid-Discretized Diabatic Surfaces 8.1.1 Walsh–Hadamard LCU block encoding 

The vibronic Hamiltonian (Eq. 4.1.10) can be decomposed as a linear combination of unitaries (LCU) by expanding the diagonal potential surfaces in the Walsh–Hadamard basis [284]. On a grid of N = 2 n points per mode in d dimensions, the diagonal surface Vss (Q1, . . . , Q d) has up to N d = 2 nd distinct values. The Walsh–Hadamard expansion requires up to 2 nd terms, giving a block-encoding normalization 

αWH = X

> ℓ

|αℓ| = ∥V ∥(WH) 1 , (8.1.1) which grows exponentially with nd . Explicitly, for a QVC Hamiltonian with maximum bi-linear coefficient |ars |max and grid spacing ∆, 

αWH ∼

d

2



|ars |max ∆2 · 22( n−1) = O(d2 · 4n). (8.1.2) At n = 10, αWH ≈ 3.45 × 10 4; at n = 12, αWH ≈ 5.52 × 10 5, confirming a factor of ∼ 16 ×

per two additional qubits. The QSP degree for Hamiltonian simulation is [35] 

dQSP = α t + log 2(1 /ε ), (8.1.3) and each query to the WH-LCU oracle requires ∼ 32 Toffoli gates for the PREPARE/SELECT network. At the pyrazine benchmark parameters, the WH-LCU encoding produces dQSP ≈

259 1.38 × 10 8 queries and ∼ 1.77 × 10 10 total T-gates, being 138 .5× more expensive than REQWIEM. 

8.1.2 Two-term T +V split block encoding 

A more efficient strategy block-encodes H = T + V as a two-term LCU, with separate encodings for the kinetic and potential operators. T is diagonal in the momentum basis (accessed via QFT) and V is diagonal in the position basis, each can be block-encoded at normalization cost equal to its spectral radius: 

αT +V = ∥T ∥max + ∥V ∥max . (8.1.4) The kinetic cutoff follows from the grid’s maximum representable momentum (cf. Eq. 2.2.2): 

Tmax = X

> r

> r

∝ 4n

L2 , (8.1.5) where ∆ r = Lr/2n is the grid spacing for mode r. This characteristic energy (Chapter 2) acts as a spectral range penalty : αT +V is dominated by Tmax at high resolution, even though physically populated kinetic energy ⟨T ⟩ ∼ 0.004 a.u. is many orders of magnitude smaller. Each QSP query to the T +V block encoding requires ∼ 1,800 Toffoli gates (for arithmetic circuits and QFT rotations within the SELECT oracle). At n = 10, the total T-gate cost, including both Toffoli decomposition ( C(Toff)  

> T

= 4 per Toffoli) and QFT rotation synthesis (C(rot)  

> T

= 47 per Rz at ϵsynth = 10 −10 via Ross–Selinger [204]), is ∼ 1.68 × 10 9: a ratio of 13 .2×

over REQWIEM. 

8.1.3 T-gate accounting 

Both Toffoli gates and Rz rotations consume magic states on a fault-tolerant architecture. The Toffoli gate decomposes into C(Toff)  

> T

= 4 T-gates [201], and each Rz (θ) rotation is synthesized via the Ross–Selinger protocol [204], reduced by the mixed-fallback and spider fusion (Appendix J to 23–30 T-gates per rotation [204]. Both sources of T-gates consume 260 one magic state each [285], making the total T-gate count is cost metric applied throughout this chapter. 

8.2 QSP in the Fock Basis 

An alternative to grid discretization represents nuclear degrees of freedom in the harmonic oscillator (Fock) basis, truncated at Nmax quanta per mode. Each mode requires nfock =

⌈log 2 Nmax ⌉ qubits. The QVC Hamiltonian in this basis takes the form 

H = X

> k

ωk

␀a†

> k

> ij,k

κ(ij ) 

> k

(ak + a†

> k

) |i⟩⟨ j| + · · · (8.2.1) where creation/annihilation operators have bandwidth (sparsity) s = 2 d2 + 1 nonzero entries per row in the Fock representation. Block encoding via sparse-access qubitization [282] gives normalization α2Q = d2Nmax max( |κ|, |γ|, . . . ). For the four-mode pyrazine model at the MCTDH-converged Fock cutoff Nmax = 30 [183, 184]: s = 33, α2Q = 22 .0, dQSP = 88 ,164, and total logical qubits = 70 N.1. At the converged cutoff Nmax = 30, QSP(Fock) produces 1 .88 × 10 8 T-gates against RE-QWIEM’s 1 .28 × 10 8: a ratio of only 1 .5×. This is the most competitive QSP encoding for harmonic or near-harmonic systems. The advantage grows rapidly with both Nmax (anhar-monicity) and d (dimensionality): at d = 24 the ratio increases to 38 .1× in the harmonic limit. The sparsity formula s = 2 d2 + 1 counts only on-diagonal electronic block connections; including off-diagonal coupling increases s by O(d) per state pair, making the reported QSP(Fock) costs conservative lower bounds. 

8.3 The Anharmonic Wall 

The QSP(Fock) near-parity at Nmax = 30 is specific to the harmonic limit of the QVC model. For arbitrary smooth potential energy surfaces V (Q1, . . . , Q d) (Ch. 7), the Fock-basis oracle must encode V via QROM lookup of a d-dimensional tensor. The effective sparsity 261 then scales as 

seff = O(N d

> max

), (8.3.1) because every grid point in the d-dimensional Fock space must be individually addressed. REQWIEM avoids this entirely, whereby the multilinear expansion of V (Eq. 7.3.1) com-poses with the UCR multiplexing protocol of Chapter 7, optimizing Toffoli-free application through the Gray-code construction (Eq. 7.3.9). The QROM scaling with dimensionality is shown in Table 8.5. For the physically general problem (arbitrary smooth PES, d > 4), QSP(Fock) suffers the same exponential blowup as QSP(WH-LCU). The effective sparsity seff is an optimistic estimate; the true QROM size is O(N d

> max

), again making reported ratios conservative lower bounds for non-polynomial potentials. 

8.4 Full-Coulomb Baseline: Eklund et al. 

Eklund et al. [112] provide the first end-to-end, bounded-error resource estimate for full quantum chemical dynamics under the molecular Hamiltonian in first quantization. Their algorithm uses an LCU block encoding of the molecular Hamiltonian in a plane-wave basis, QSP for time evolution, and quantum amplitude estimation (QAE) for observable readout, targeting photodissociation quantum yields for small atmospheric molecules. Their Table 4 reports resource estimates for C 4H6O (crotonaldehyde, η = 49 spin-orbitals) at ε = 0 .095: 3,000–8 ,000 logical qubits and 10 13 –10 16 Toffoli gates. Because pyrazine has η = 52 spin-orbitals, the C 4H6O entry serves as a proxy for a pyrazine-scale system. Eklund et al. treat the full Coulomb Hamiltonian (no Born–Oppenheimer approximation) in first quantization with plane-wave basis sets, whereas REQWIEM operates on a pre-computed diabatic model Hamiltonian. The ∼ 10 10 ratio therefore reflects the combined cost of (i) the full-Coulomb vs. model-Hamiltonian gap, (ii) QAE overhead for observable estimation, and (iii) the first-quantization plane-wave basis choice. It is presented to illustrate 262 the benefit between the two paradigms when a reliable diabatic model is available, making each method complementary in their own limits. 

8.5 Head-to-Head Comparison 

Table 8.7 consolidates all five methods at the converged benchmark, using T-gate count as a primary metric; logical qubit count and circuit depth are secondary. 

8.5.1 Scaling with grid resolution 

The ratio growth from n = 8 through n = 12 demonstrates the O(4 n) divergence through 

Tmax (Table 8.1). REQWIEM’s Trotter step count grows as O(∥[T, V ]∥1/2) via the commu-tator bound [127], where ∥[T, V ]∥ ≪ ∥ T ∥ · ∥ V ∥ for the structured vibronic Hamiltonian. 

8.5.2 Scaling with dimensionality 

The WH-LCU ratio decreases with d because αWH has a fixed exponential floor from 4 n while REQWIEM grows linearly in d. The T +V ratio, by contrast, increases with d, reflecting the additive growth of Tmax over modes. 

8.5.3 Rotation precision sensitivity 

At practical synthesis precision ( C(rot)  

> T

= 23–30 per rotation [204]), REQWIEM’s all-Rz cost decreases proportionally. QSP, however, has an irreducible Toffoli floor : the per-query Toffoli cost ( C(Toff)  

> T

= 4 per gate, as in Appendix J) is fixed and does not scale down with relaxed rotation precision. At C(rot)  

> T

= 23, the T-gate ratio increases from 13 × to 15–17 × because only REQWIEM benefits from the reduced synthesis cost. 

8.6 Structural Arguments 

Five independent structural features underlie REQWIEM’s quantitative advantage. 263 8.6.1 Spectral range penalty 

QSP’s polynomial degree dQSP = αt is proportional to the block-encoding normalization 

α ≥ ∥ T ∥max = Tmax ∝ 4n/L 2 (Eq. 8.1.5). REQWIEM’s Trotter step count grows as [127] 

k = O

t3/2 ∥[T, V ]∥1/2

ε1/2



, (8.6.1) where the commutator norm ∥[T, V ]∥ is validated by Richardson extrapolation to Γ ≈ 3.2 ×

10 −4 a.u. 3 for d = 4 pyrazine (Appendix K). Because ∥[T, V ]∥ ≪ ∥ T ∥ · ∥ V ∥, the ratio αt/k 

diverges as O(4 n/ 2) with grid resolution. 

8.6.2 Gap anti-correlation 

Near avoided crossings (gap ∆ 0 → 0), the Trotter commutator ∥[Vd, V od ]∥ = ∆ 0 · W → 0while the Dyson order required for QSP polynomial accuracy grows as W 2/(∆ 0 v) → ∞ .This gap anti-correlation where Trotter accuracy improves where nonadiabatic transitions are important is formalized in Section 8.8. 

8.6.3 Fan-out parallelism 

REQWIEM’s channel-register fan-out protocol (Chapter 4, Section 4.3) enables scalable cir-cuit depth for M -state coupling via binary-tree controlled rotations, while QSP’s sequential query structure does not admit this parallelization: each query must complete before the next QSP angle rotation is applied. 

8.6.4 Multi-state obstruction 

For M > 2 electronic states, each off-diagonal coupling Vij contributes independently to the block-encoding normalization: 

α ≥ X

> i<j

∥Vij ∥ ␀M (M −1) 2 terms . (8.6.2) REQWIEM handles M states via the multiplexed UCR structure at cost linear in the num-ber of symmetry-distinct coupling terms |G 3| + |G 4| (Eqs. 4.2.4–4.2.5), not in α. For the 264 anthracene-fullerene system ( d = 246, M = 4), the QSP(Fock) ratio grows to 25 ,065 ×

(Table 8.11). 

8.6.5 Zero-Toffoli property and the Toffoli floor 

REQWIEM’s circuit uses exclusively Rz and CNOT gates: T (Toff) step = 0 (Eq. 5.1.13). Ev-ery QSP encoding requires Toffoli gates for QROM lookup, arithmetic circuits, and block-encoding ancilla management. At C(Toff)  

> T

= 4 T-gates per Toffoli, QSP has an irreducible Toffoli floor that cannot be reduced by improving rotation synthesis precision (Section 8.5.3). 

8.7 Circuit Depth Analysis 

The T-gate count measures total work; circuit depth measures wall-clock time on a fault-tolerant device. REQWIEM’s depth benefits from fan-out parallelism, along with simultane-ous application of commuting diagonal phases, and the absence of sequential query structure. QSP’s depth is lower-bounded by dQSP .At realistic molecular scale, the depth advantage is dramatic. For the anthracene–fullerene interface ( d = 246, M = 4, n = 10), this gap becomes insurmountable (Table 8.11). At an optimistic logical cycle time of 1 μs per non-Clifford layer [205], REQWIEM com-pletes a pyrazine propagation ( d = 4) in ∼ 3.2 seconds. QSP( T +V ) requires ∼ 143 seconds for the same system; for the anthracene–fullerene system, the QSP wall-clock time exceeds any practical limit. 

8.8 The Non-Perturbative Convergence Barrier 

The structural arguments of Section 8.6 are quantitative but contingent on specific parame-ter values. This section establishes two theorems that provide a representation-independent reason for Trotter’s superiority near conical intersections. 265 8.8.1 Setup: Landau–Zener model 

Consider the canonical two-state Landau–Zener Hamiltonian 

H(t) = v t 

2 σz + W

2 σx, (8.8.1) where v = |F1 − F2| · ˙Q is the diabatic level velocity and W is the diabatic coupling. The adiabaticity is given by Γ = W 2

2 v |∆F |, (8.8.2) and the transition probability is [276, 286, 287] 

PLZ = exp( −2πΓ) . (8.8.3) 

8.8.2 Non-perturbative convergence barrier 

Let a12 (t → ∞ ) = exp( −πΓ) denote the diabatic transition amplitude. The interaction-picture Dyson series 

a12 =

> ∞

X

> n=0

(−i)n

> −∞

⟨2| VI (t1) · · · VI (tn) |1⟩ dt 1 · · · dt n (8.8.4) has partial sums SN = PNn=0 cn satisfying: (i) Growth phase. For N < ⌊πΓ⌋, the terms |cN | are increasing: |cN | ∼ (πΓ) N /N !; partial sums oscillate with |SN | ≫ exp( −πΓ). (ii) Optimal truncation. The smallest term occurs at N ∗ = ⌊πΓ⌋, with magnitude 

|cN ∗ | ∼ (2 π2Γ) −1/2 exp( −πΓ). (iii) Convergence. For N > e · πΓ, the Lagrange remainder satisfies 

|RN | = |SN − exp( −πΓ) | ≤ 

eπΓ

N

N

exp( πΓ) → 0 (8.8.5) super-exponentially. 266 The Dyson series for the LZ problem is a rearrangement of the Taylor series of exp( −x)at x = πΓ. By Stirling’s approximation, the N th term satisfies 

xN

N ! ≈ 1

√2πN 

e xN

N

, (8.8.6) which is increasing for N < x and decreasing for N > x , with maximum at N = ⌊x⌋. Before the maximum, partial sums oscillate with amplitudes far exceeding exp( −x). After N ≈ ex,terms decrease fast enough for convergence. This is a Stokes phenomenon [288, 289], where the non-perturbative amplitude exp( −πΓ) is invisible to any finite-order perturbation theory with N < ⌊πΓ⌋ terms. 

8.8.3 Numerical verification 

The convergence barrier is verified by direct computation of the Taylor partial sums of exp( −πΓ), tabulated in Table 8.12. For the tractable cases (Γ ≤ 5), Nmin /π Γ ≈ 3.2–3 .5, consistent with the asymptotic bound 

Nmin ≤ ⌈ eπΓ⌉. For Γ ≥ 10, convergence cannot be verified in double precision because partial sums reach ∼ 10 25 while the answer is ∼ 10 −14 , as a numerical effect of the non-perturbative barrier. Consider the simulation of a Hamiltonian containing a two-state avoided crossing with adiabaticity parameter Γ. (i) Perturbative methods. An algorithm based on a truncated Dyson series or interaction-picture perturbation expansion (e.g., Ref. [290]) requires truncation or-der 

N ≥ ⌊ πΓ⌋ (8.8.7) to resolve the non-perturbative transition amplitude exp( −πΓ), independent of all other parameters. (ii) QSP/QSVT. The standard QSP polynomial degree dQSP = O(αt + log(1 /ε )) [35, 36] is a uniform bound in the eigenvalue argument and does not contain a gap-dependent 267 correction. However, the block-encoding normalization α for grid-based vibronic Hamil-tonians satisfies α ≥ Tmax ∝ 4n (Eq. 8.1.5), and gap closure provides no reduction in 

α. QSP therefore receives no cost benefit from the physical regime (∆ 0 → 0) where Trotter error vanishes. QSP does not necessarily require a higher polynomial degree near conical intersections, but its cost is anchored to the spectral range α, which is gap-independent, while Trotter’s cost decreases through the commutator norm ∥[Vd, V od ]∥ = ∆ 0 · W → 0. This one-sided scaling is the mechanism underlying a duality between Trotter and Dyson methods. 

8.8.4 Trotter–Dyson duality 

Let ∆ 0 = min Q |V1(Q) − V2(Q)| be the minimum energy gap. Then: (A) Trotter error vanishes at CI. The second-order Trotter error satisfies [127] 

∥S2(∆ t) − e−iH ∆t∥ ≤ (∆ t)3

12 

␀∥[Vd, [Vd, V od ]] ∥ + ∥[Vod , [Vod , V d]] ∥, (8.8.8) where ∥[Vd, V od ]∥ = ∆ 0 · W . As ∆ 0 → 0: Trotter error → 0. (B) Dyson order diverges at conical intersection. For a perturbative or interaction-picture expansion of e−iHt , the minimum truncation order satisfying |RN | ≤ ε is 

Nmin = ⌈πΓ⌉ =

 πW 2

2v ∆0/L 



∝ W 2Lv ∆0

. (8.8.9) As ∆ 0 → 0: Nmin → ∞ . The standard QSP degree bound dQSP = O(αt + log(1 /ε )) does not contain this divergence, but α ≥ Tmax provides no offsetting cost reduction as ∆ 0 → 0. This creates an asymmetry where Trotter becomes more accurate near small gaps and with strong couplings, while polynomial-approximation methods require higher degrees. The parametric verification using pyrazine parameters ( W = 0 .009 a.u., v = 0 .08 a.u., t =4000 a.u.) is given in Table 8.13. 268 A crossover occurs at ∆ 0 ≈ 1 meV; below this, Trotter requires fewer steps than Dyson requires orders. For conical intersections (∆ 0 → 0), the asymmetry becomes arbitrarily large. 

8.8.5 Physical Landau–Zener parameters 

Table 8.14 collects representative Landau–Zener parameters for eight molecular systems spanning the range of nonadiabatic chemistry. The pyrazine row is derived from the quadratic vibronic coupling (QVC) parameters of Raab et al. [183] used throughout this disserta-tion (Appendix H): the diabatic coupling W = c1 = 0 .2080 eV ≈ 0.0076 a.u. (rounded to 0.009 a.u.), the nuclear velocity v along ν10 a estimated from v = p2EZPE /μ with μ =46 ,484 me and ω = 0 .0739 eV, and the gradient difference |∆F | estimated from linear coupling constants κi and λ of the QVC Hamiltonian [188, 193]. The remaining seven sys-tems use order-of-magnitude estimates of W , v, and |∆F | representative of each coupling regime; exact values depend on level of theory and nuclear geometry and should not be taken as quantitative predictions. The purpose of the table is to illustrate the range of Γ and 

NDyson encountered in practice, not to provide system-specific benchmarks. Representative parameter ranges are informed by the conical intersection literature for ethylene [82, 173], retinal [168, 169], and transition-metal photochemistry [162, 166]. For systems with significant nonadiabatic coupling ( PLZ > 10 −10 ), the representative parameters yield πΓ > 1, requiring multi-term Dyson series. For strongly-coupled regimes (e.g., strong proton transfer, slow charge transfer, heavy-atom spin-orbit coupling), the con-vergence barrier can require dozens to hundreds of Dyson orders, illustrating the challenge for polynomial-approximation methods in these coupling regimes. 

8.9 Discussion 8.9.1 When QSP is appropriate 

QSP achieves optimal query complexity and is the preferred method when the block-encoding oracle is inexpensive (e.g., sparse electronic structure Hamiltonians with fixed nuclei), the 269 spectral range is moderate, and the dynamics is approximately adiabatic [35, 90]. The com-parison in this chapter is specific to nonadiabatic quantum molecular dynamics on diabatic surfaces , where the oracle cost dominates and the commutator structure of the Hamiltonian strongly favors product formulas. 

8.9.2 Randomized product formulas (qDRIFT) 

qDRIFT [291] is a randomized Trotter variant with cost scaling as λ21t2/ε , where λ1 =

P 

> j

∥Hj ∥ is the 1-norm of the Hamiltonian decomposition. For grid-based NA-QMD, λ1 =

O(4 n) through ∥T ∥max , incurring the same spectral range penalty as QSP’s α. Deterministic Trotter avoids this because its error depends on commutator norms ∥[Hj , H k]∥ ≪ ∥ Hj ∥ · ∥Hk∥ [127]. qDRIFT is therefore not competitive with deterministic Trotter for this problem class. 

8.9.3 Higher-order Trotter 

The present comparison uses second-order Suzuki–Trotter (Eq. 2.3.6). Fourth-order Suzuki decomposition [125, 126] incurs 5 × the gates per step but reduces the step count scaling from O(t3/2/ε 1/2) to O(t5/4/ε 1/4) [127]. At the pyrazine parameters, this may reduce Trotter steps by a factor of ∼ 3–5, further widening the gap with QSP. The primary comparison uses second-order to remain conservative. 

8.9.4 Commutator norm validation 

The commutator norm Γ ≈ 3.2 × 10 −4 a.u. 3 (d = 4 pyrazine) is validated by Richardson extrapolation of finite-grid commutator norms ( n = 3 through n = 7 for d = 2, extrapolated to n → ∞ ), giving Γ( d = 2) ≈ 1.624 × 10 −4 a.u. 3 and an estimated d = 4 value of ∼

3.248 × 10 −4 a.u. 3. Details are in Appendix K. 270 8.10 Summary 

For the QSP block-encoding strategies considered here, the vibronic Hamiltonian on a position-space grid carries a T-gate cost that exceeds REQWIEM by at least an order of magnitude at converged parameters, and the ratio diverges as O(4 n) with grid resolution. The QSP(Fock) encoding achieves near-parity (1 .5×) for harmonic potentials at d = 4, but collapses beyond the harmonic limit (Table 8.4) and grows to 38 × at d = 24 even for harmonic PES. Trotter–Dyson duality provides a representation-independent explanation. Near conical intersections, where nonadiabatic transitions are physically important, Trotter error scales as ∆0 · W → 0 while perturbative (Dyson) truncation order scales as W 2/(∆ 0 v) → ∞ and QSP cost is anchored to the gap-independent spectral range α. Trotterized architectures are not universally better for similar Hamiltonians, but can be applied in a similar fashion, allowing for intuitive extension into an array of other physical systems. Chapter 9 considers these systems, and the REQWIEM architecture as a scheme to simulate them in future work. 271 Table 8.1: Spectral range penalty: αT +V scaling with grid resolution ( d = 4, M = 2, pyrazine).

275 Chapter 9 REQWIEM as a Universal Coupled-Channel Simulation Principle 9.1 Introduction 

This dissertation constructs, validates, and resource-estimates a first-quantized, grid-based quantum algorithm for the nonadiabatic quantum molecular dynamics, mostly specified to di-abatic states with electronic channel coupling. Chapter 3 recovered the nonadiabatic dynam-ics of the Marcus model [44], benchmarking the circuit against known classical results and touched on the sensitivity of observables to circuit approximation. Chapter 4 constructed the full algorithm: the uniformly controlled rotation (UCR) encoding of the diabatic potential, basis-change protocol (Clifford transform), and the XOR-class decomposition of off-diagonal couplings. Chapter 5 estimates the resources required to perform this algorithm on ideal, error-corrected, fault-tolerant hardware. By comparing rotation counts, T-gate depths, and ancilla costs against QROM-based loading procedures, a scalable depth advantage was found from graph-optimized parallelism (Table 5.16), despite significant overhead from rotation synthesis. A hint toward quantum advantage is seen heuristically, primarily toward systems with many vibrational modes and many electronic channels, with low molecular symmetry. Chapter 6 extended the protocol to elastic and inelastic scattering, demonstrating faithful reproduction of phase shifts and state-to-state cross sections (Figures 6.3 and 6.4). Chapter 7 completed the potential-energy surface treatment by exhibiting closed-form, truncation-free encodings of smooth functions via multilinear expansion, preserving the UCR composition advantage at every order. Chapter 8 then placed REQWIEM in the broader algorithmic landscape, comparing QSP and theend-to-end fault-tolerant estimate of Eklund et al. [112], 276 confirming T -gate advantages of one to ten orders of magnitude across all tested configura-tions when diabatic surfaces are available. This chapter considers the foundational structure of REQWIEM apart from its appli-cation to any single physical system. The algorithmic content is broader than nonadiabatic quantum molecular dynamics, since the quantum circuit structure itself applies a decompo-sition that applies to every system admitting the Hilbert space factorization 

H = HX ⊗ H C , (9.1.1) where X encodes continuous degrees of freedom (internuclear distance, hyperradius, lat-tice position, propagation distance) and C encodes a discrete internal structure (electronic surfaces, angular momentum channels, spin projections, neutrino flavors). The Hamiltonian decomposes as 

H = TX ⊗ 1C + 1X ⊗ H(FBR)  

> C

+ V (X , C), (9.1.2) where TX is diagonal in the momentum representation of X , H(FBR)  

> C

is diagonal in the free-channel basis of C, and V is diagonal in the interaction-picture basis of C, the discrete variable representation (DVR), though dense in the finite basis representation (FBR). The circuit implements each piece with the cheapest available primitive: the quantum Fourier transform for T , a UCR in the FBR for H(FBR)  

> C

, the DVR basis-change UDVR followed by a UCR in the DVR for V , and XOR-class focusing Cliffords for any off-diagonal coupling that is not diagonalized by the DVR nor by the QFT. The structure of equations 9.1.1–9.1.2 is the content of the algorithm, while the physi-cal identity of the registers, the form of UDVR , and the XOR-class count are system-specific inputs. Post-Trotter simulation methods, including qubitization [33], quantum signal process-ing [35], and truncated Dyson series [290] achieve optimal asymptotic scaling in propagation time and error for Hamiltonians accessed through block-encoded oracles, but have not been extended to coupled-channel Hamiltonians whose potential operators are dense in the channel index and require nonadiabatic basis changes between complementary representations, iden-tified explicitly in Section 9.8.. Duality between DVR and FBR has no known analog in the 277 oracle query model. A Trotter-based framework is a natural algorithmic setting for this class of problems with a split-operator architecture allowing for efficient, stepwise propagation. The regimes in which post-Trotter methods are preferred include single-channel dynamics at long propagation times, high-precision static eigenvalue extraction, and second-quantized electronic structure, and are identified explicitly in Section 9.8. The present chapter develops this observation across eight sections, motivating a future for digital quantum computation across quantum mechanical systems. Section 9.2 establishes a universal protocol structure: the second-order Trotter step in the abstract register notation, a gate-count formula, and the FBR/DVR diagonalization as a closed-form procedure. The DVR unitary is constructed from the spectral decomposition of a known Hermitian operator and compiled deterministically without iterative or adap-tive subroutines. Section 9.3 instantiates this protocol across ten candidate coupled-channel systems, including reactive scattering, polaron dynamics, neutrino oscillations, and high-harmonic generation. The off-diagonal coupling mechanisms resolving XOR-class structure are derived in Section 9.4. Section 9.5 considers the efficiently implementable basis-change unitaries UDVR . Sec-tion 9.6 constructs REQWIEM as a front-end for quantum Krylov subspace diagonaliza-tion (QKSD), exploiting the overlap matrix’s Toeplitz structure and the commutator-norm Trotter error bound to extract eigenvalues. Section 9.7 identifies twelve additional phys-ical systems across nuclear physics, condensed matter, quantum optics, and astrophysics whose Hamiltonians admit a direct REQWIEM register assignment. Section 9.8 derives a REQWIEM applicability criterion, determined by four classically checkable conditions: a non-trivial channel dimension, non-separable coupling, smooth potential, and dynamical observables or moderate eigenvalue precision, to identify problems that are classically in-tractable and too dense for block-encoding approaches. No claim or proof is made here that REQWIEM is “optimal” for these problems, only that these problems are in the algorithm’s capacity. This chapter ends the dissertation, with closing remarks in Section 9.9 278 Notation 

The present chapter treats systems whose channel registers span nuclear, condensed-matter, optical, and gravitational physics. Several symbols that were unambiguous in the vibronic context of Chapter 4 now collide with standard notation in these broader domains. In par-ticular, the Krylov subspace dimension (Section 9.6) and the azimuthal quantum number in the Teukolsky equation (Section 9.7.4) both require m, and the phonon mode count in the polaron Hamiltonian, the black hole mass, the Dicke quantum number |J, M ⟩, and the quantum phase estimation (QPE) query count all use M . To avoid ambiguity, this chapter adopts a relabeled convention collected in Table 9.1. 279 9.2 The Universal Protocol Structure 

Every system treated in this chapter admits a simplified Hilbert space factorization 

H = HX ⊗ H C , (9.2.1) where X is the coordinate register (taken as nx qubits, Nx = 2 nx grid points) and C is the channel register ( nc qubits, d = 2 nc internal states). The Hamiltonian decomposes as 

H = TX ⊗ 1C + 1X ⊗ H(FBR)  

> C

> C

is diagonal in the free-channel basis (FBR) of C, and V is diagonal in the interaction-picture basis (DVR) of C but dense in the FBR. The second-order Trotter step is e−iH∆t ≈ e−iT ∆t/ 2 e−iH(FBR)

(9.2.3) The FBR →DVR →FBR alternation can be subdivided depending on internal structure of orthogonal subspaces; this internal structure is system-specific, detailed for each system in Section 9.3. The interaction potential decomposes into diagonal and off-diagonal parts in the channel register: 

V = X

> α̸=β

Nrot = Nx × nc × (Ndiag + NXOR ) + O(n2
