> c

) the DVR transform cost, and O(n2

> x

) the QFT cost. 280 9.2.1 The FBR/DVR diagonalization as a Closed-Form Procedure 

The preceding chapters show that the coupled-channel systems in the REQWIEM catalog shares a single algorithmic skeleton: a Trotter step in which the channel-register opera-tor is applied once partially diagonal, with compute-uncompute iterates applying the full nonadiabatic coupling. The physical identity of such a basis change varies between systems, arising from Legendre quadrature, Gauss–Hermite quadrature, the Wigner D-matrix, Pon-tecorvo–Maki–Nakagawa–Sakata (PMNS) mixing, Clebsch–Gordan recoupling, and configu-ration interaction diagonalization. The algorithmic procedure is the same. This subsection states this procedure explicitly and details a closed-form circuit for every class of UDVR en-countered in this work, requiring no iterative or adaptive circuit compilation. Since the DVR unitary is determined entirely by the spectral decomposition of a known Hermitian operator, it can be compiled by a fixed-depth reduction to elementary gates. 

Problem Statement 

Let HC ∼= Cd be the channel Hilbert space ( d = 2 nc ), equipped with two distinguished orthonormal bases: 1. The finite basis representation (FBR) {| α⟩} d−1

> α=0

, in which the free-channel Hamil-tonian H(FBR)  

> C

is diagonal: 

H(FBR)  

> C

=

> d−1

X

> α=0

E(free)  

> α

|α⟩⟨ α|. (9.2.6) 2. The discrete variable representation (DVR) {| s⟩DVR }d−1 

> s=0

, in which the interaction potential is diagonal at each coordinate grid point: 

V (ri) = 

> d−1

X

> s=0

V (ri, s ) |s⟩⟨ s|DVR . (9.2.7) The two bases are related by a DVR transform 

UDVR |α⟩FBR =

> d−1

X

> s=0

Tsα |s⟩DVR , (9.2.8) 281 where the matrix T = ( Tsα ) is unitary, further reduced to real orthogonal when the op-erator admits real eigenvectors. The FBR is identified with the nc-qubit channel register’s computational basis, so UDVR is a d × d unitary gate on C.The REQWIEM protocol requires: (R1) An explicit, closed-form expression for T in terms of some defining physical operator (angular momentum, harmonic oscillator, mixing matrix, etc.). (R2) A quantum circuit decomposition of UDVR into elementary gates {Rz , CNOT } with gate count polynomial in nc (or at worst O(d2) via the cosine-sine decomposition). (R3) The transform must be geometry-independent, wherein T is the same matrix for every coordinate grid point ri, so that the DVR round trip is applied once per Trotter step on C while X remains in superposition. DVR amortization is lost when condition (R3) is relaxed, just as in the geometry-dependent configuration-interaction transform of Section 9.5.4, and the cost per Trotter step rises from 

O(d2) + Nxd to Nx × O (d2). Whether this is acceptable depends on the density of avoided crossings; the closed-form structure of (R1)–(R2) is preserved in either case. The condition (R3) was implicitly applied in previous chapters via restriction to an orthogonal basis of vibrational modes, with electronic couplings requiring only a Clifford transform. 

Diagonalization Algorithm 

The construction of UDVR proceeds as follows, later to be system specified. Algorithm 1: DVR Unitary Construction. 

Input: A Hermitian operator O on HC whose eigenbasis defines the DVR, represented as a 

d × d matrix O in the FBR (computational basis). 

Output: A quantum circuit CDVR on nc qubits implementing UDVR such that UDVR O U †

> DVR

is diagonal in the computational basis. 282 Step 1. Operation specification. From the Hamiltonian decomposition 9.2.2, extract the operator O on C that the interaction potential V (X , C) would diagonalize if the coor-dinate dependence were removed. In each physical context, O can be represented (not comprehensively) by one of: Physical system Defining operator O

Angular potential (dipole, AT) cos θ on Legendre basis Phonon displacement (polaron) (a + a†)/√2 on Fock basis Neutrino flavor |e⟩⟨ e| on mass basis Rovibrational Euler angles (ϕ, θ, χ ) on symmetric-top basis Electronic structure (adiabatic) He(Rref ) on diabatic basis Spin–orbit (Hund’s case (a) →(c)) ˆHSO on |Λ, Σ⟩ basis Crystal momentum ˆTa (translation operator) on site basis Color-spin (quark model) σi · σj on product spin basis Symmetry reduction ˆUg for g ∈ G on channel basis 

Step 2. diagonalize O classically. Compute the eigendecomposition 

O = T† diag( λ0, . . . , λ d−1) T, (9.2.9) where the columns of T† are the eigenvectors of O. This is a classical O(d3) computation (or better when O has special structure: O(d2) for tridiagonal operators, O(d log d) for circulant operators). For systems where the operator O has a known analytic eigenbasis, Step 2 is replaced by direct evaluation of the closed-form eigenvectors. The following table records the closed-form expressions: 283 Operator Eigenvectors Tsα Closed form cos θ (Legendre) √ws Pα(cos θs) Gauss–Legendre quadrature (a + a†)/√2 (Hermite) √ws ψα(xs) Gauss–Hermite quadrature PMNS matrix (UPMNS )sα Experimental mixing angles Wigner D-matrix DJ∗ 

> MsKα

(ϕs, θ s, χ s) Gauss–Legendre + uniform grid ˆTa (translation) e2πisα/d /√d Discrete Fourier transform CG recoupling ⟨j1m1j2m2|JM ⟩ Clebsch–Gordan coefficients The existence of closed-form eigenvectors means T can be known as an evaluable func-tion of the physical parameters (quadrature nodes, mixing angles, angular momen-tum quantum numbers). This is the sense in which the FBR →DVR diagonalization is “closed-form.” 

Step 3. Compile T to a quantum circuit. The unitary T is decomposed into elementary gates, selected by the structure of T:(a) Cosine-sine decomposition (CSD). For a d × d unitary with no exploitable structure, the CSD [255] produces a circuit of depth O(d2) using at most 12 (d2 −d)CNOT gates and 12 (d2 + d) single-qubit rotations. This is a ‘fallback’ for the Legendre DVR, Gauss–Hermite DVR, PMNS matrix, Wigner D-matrix, fixed CI transform, and Clebsch–Gordan recoupling. (b) QFT structure. When T is the discrete Fourier transform matrix (as for crystal momentum or lattice translation), the standard QFT circuit achieves depth O(n2 

> c

)with nc(nc−1) 2 controlled-phase gates and nc Hadamards [292]. (c) Recursive/butterfly structure. Wavelet transforms (Haar, Daubechies) and certain symmetry projectors admit O(d) or O(|G| · nc) gate decompositions from their recursive (butterfly) structure, with depth O(nc) per level. 284 Compilation specifics are determined by the algebraic structure of T, selected at classi-cal preprocessing time. No iterative circuit optimization is required. To emphasize, this preprocessing does not diagonalize the operator, but gives a parameterization to the diagonalization operator, a far easier problem that maintains computational tractabil-ity. 

Step 4. Embed in the Trotter step. The compiled circuit CDVR is inserted into the second-order Trotter step 9.2.3 as the DVR round trip: 

C† 

> DVR

◦ UCR diag 

␀{V (ri, s )}; X → C  ◦ C DVR , (9.2.10) where the UCR encodes the diagonal potential values {V (ri, s )} as rotation angles via M¨ obius inversion (Chapter 7). The round trip acts on C while X is in superposition; its cost is 2 CDVR (one forward, one inverse application) plus the UCR cost Nx × d

rotations. 

Proof of Closure 

The defining operator O is always known, with an FBR matrix being available analytically. For quadrature-based DVR transforms (Legendre, Gauss–Hermite, Wigner D-matrix), O

is the coordinate operator’s tridiagonal matrix representation (cos θ, x, or cos β) in the orthogonal-polynomial basis. Tridiagonal eigenvalue problems have O(d2) classical cost with known error bounds (Golub–Welsch algorithm [293]). The eigenvalues are quadrature nodes while eigenvectors are quadrature weights multiplied into orthogonal polynomials evaluated at those nodes. For mixing-matrix transforms (PMNS, CG), the matrix T is a defining physical quantity, determined experimentally or via angular momentum algebra. For the QFT, T is the discrete FT matrix Tsα = e2πisα/d /√d.The CSD produces a unique circuit of fixed depth for a given given T, without variational parameters or convergence criteria. T is block-decomposed recursively into 2 × 2 blocks, each factored as a product of a cosine–sine pair and two diagonal unitaries. At the k-th level of 285 recursion ( k = 1 , . . . , n c), the 2 k × 2k block produces 2 k−1 uniformly controlled rotations, each compiled via the Gray-code UCR with 2 nc−k elementary rotations [255]. The total gate count is 

NCSD =

> nc

X

> k=1

2k−1 × 2nc−k = nc × 2nc−1 = 12 d log 2 d CNOTs , (9.2.11) which for the systems of this work ( d ≤ 215 ) ranges from 4 CNOTs ( d = 4, PMNS)to 

∼ 2.5 × 10 5 CNOTs ( d = 32768, reactive scattering). Analytic eigenvectors (Step 2) and deterministic compilation (Step 3) ensures that the DVR unitary is fully specified before any quantum circuit is executed. The FBR →DVR →FBR alternation is classical preprocessing, producing a gate list.

Quadrature DVR as the Canonical Instance 

The majority of the entries in Table 9.2 are quadrature DVR transforms, in which the defining operator is the coordinate operator ˆ x in an orthogonal-polynomial basis {Pα(x)}d−1

> α=0

with weight function w(x). The three-term recurrence of orthogonal polynomials gives a tridiagonal matrix representation [294]: 

⟨Pα|ˆq|Pβ ⟩ = δα,β −1 bβ + δαβ aα + δα,β +1 bα+1 , (9.2.12) with eigenvalues {xs}d−1 

> s=0

as quadrature nodes and eigenvectors giving the transform matrix 

Tsα = √ws Pα(xs), (9.2.13) where ws > 0 are the quadrature weights (determined by ws = [ P 

> α

Pα(xs)2]−1). This structure guarantees the transformed unitary is: 1. Exactly diagonal. The potential V (ri, x ) is diagonal in the DVR: ⟨xs|V |xs′ ⟩ =

V (ri, x s) δss ′ , up to quadrature error of order O(d−2p) where p is the polynomial degree of V in x. No off-diagonal residual is generated. 2. Orthogonal. The transform T is real orthogonal: TT T = I, so U † 

> DVR

= U T

> DVR

.3. Geometry independent. Quadrature nodes and weights depend only on the poly-nomial family and the truncation order d, not on the coordinate grid point ri. The transform is therefore the same at every ri, satisfying condition (R3) and enabling amortization being the source of the quantum advantage. 288 The Gauss–Legendre, Gauss–Hermite, and Gauss–Jacobi families (covering the angular, phonon, and hyperspherical DVR respectively) are instances of this construction. The Wigner 

D-matrix DVR extends to a product quadrature on SO(3), with Euler angles ( ϕ, θ, χ ) playing the role of quadrature nodes on the group manifold. 

Non-Quadrature diagonalization 

Three entries in Table 9.2 fall outside the quadrature DVR framework. The neutrino flavor-mass basis change is not a quadrature transform, but a fixed 3 × 3unitary determined by three mixing angles ( θ12 , θ 13 , θ 23 ) and a charge-parity-violating phase 

δCP [295–297]. On the quantum register (2 qubits, d = 4 with one padded state), the PMNS matrix is compiled directly by CSD into ≤ 3 CNOTs and ≤ 6 Rz gates. For the collective neutrino problem with Nang angular basis states, the DVR is the tensor product U ⊗Nang  

> PMNS

,costing Nang × 3 CNOTs. The Configuration Interaction transform UCI (R) is geometry dependent, and is the elec-tronic Hamiltonian’s eigenvector matrix He(R), by ab initio electronic structure calculation (CASSCF, MRCI, EOM-CC) at each reference geometry. Unlike the quadrature DVR, the eigenvectors are not known in closed analytic form; they are the output of a non-trivial clas-sical diagonalization. The circuit compilation of each UCI (Ri) is still deterministic, with the input to the circuit compiler being a d × d numerical matrix. The Haar and Daubechies wavelet transforms are not eigendecompositions of any single physical operator, being signal-processing transforms with multiresolution properties. The wavelet transform replaces the uniform-grid DVR on the coordinate register X (not the chan-nel register C), compressing the grid for potentials with different length scales. The potential becomes approximately diagonal in the wavelet basis, with off-diagonal elements controlled by the wavelet smoothness order. Circuit compilation follows the butterfly (recursive) route at O(d) or O(pd ) cost, preserving the Trotter step’s overall structure. 289 The REQWIEM-Amenability Criterion 

A Hermitian operator O on HC is REQWIEM-amenable if there exists a unitary U on HC

such that 

U O U † =

> d−1

X

> s=0

λs |s⟩⟨ s|, (9.2.14) is diagonal in the computational basis, and U admits an efficient O(poly( nc)) quantum circuit decomposition. The condition on U is not merely that it be efficiently implementable in principle: the DVR round-trip cost enters every Trotter step, so the constant in the poly( nc) bound matters. The compilation costs for all transforms in this work are recorded in Table 9.2. 

DVR/FBR Duality as a Spectral Theorem Application 

Instances of DVR/FBR duality in this work are applications of the spectral theorem for Hermitian operators on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces. Given any Hermitian O on HC , the spectral theorem guarantees the existence of a unitary T diagonalizing O. The REQWIEM protocol requires the identity of O with the interaction potential restricted to the channel space, where a diagonal basis to apply the potential step is given by spectral decomposition. This guarantees implementation as a diagonal UCR at cost O(Nx × d) rather than a dense matrix exponential at cost O(Nx × d2). The REQWIEM-amenability criterion (Eq. 9.2.14) is the precondition for an efficient quantum circuit following the spectral decomposition of O. The catalog of Table 9.2 demon-strates that this condition is well-satisfied among an array of quantum mechanical problems, as a consequence of decomposing Hamiltonians with separable kinetic and potential struc-tures H = T + V .

Algorithm Extensibility 

The FBR →DVR diagonalization’s closed-form constraint restricts the problems that are REQWIEM-amenable. The system must: 290 1. Have a Hamiltonian with a factorization H = HX ⊗ H C with a separable kinetic term 2. Have a Hermitian operator O on HC such that the interaction potential V (X , C) is diagonal in the eigenbasis of O, and 3. O has an eigenvector matrix T admitting an O(poly( nc)) or O(d2) circuit decomposi-tion. If these conditions are fulfilled, the system is REQWIEM-amenable and the full protocol of UCR potential encoding, M¨ obius inversion, XOR-class focusing, and Toeplitz autocorre-lation for QKSD applies without modification. The catalog of Table 9.2 is not comprehensive, allowing new systems (which may not be physical) satisfying questions 1–3 entering the REQWIEM framework with all necessary circuit primitives, resource estimates, and comparison bounds developed in this dissertation. DVR/FBR duality mechanizes a split-operator method to convert a dense coupled-channel problem into a sequence of diagonal operations, constructed by Algorithm 1. 

9.3 System Catalog: Hamiltonians and Register Architectures 

Each system treated below is an instance of Algorithm 1 (Section 9.2.1). The circuit is compiled by CSD or QFT and the off-diagonal coupling (if any) is resolved by one of the mechanisms derived in Section 9.4. This section collects the Hamiltonians, register architec-tures, and resource estimates into a single consolidated reference. The following subsections specify the Hamiltonian for each system, identify the coordinate and channel registers, and state the DVR class from Table 9.2. All register dimensions, qubit counts, rotation counts per Trotter step, and classical comparison costs are collected in Table 9.3. 291 9.3.1 Reactive scattering in hyperspherical coordinates. 

The H + H 2 → H2 + H reaction in Pack–Parker hyperspherical coordinates [298], after removal of center-of-mass motion and overall rotation and the substitution χ = ρ5/2ψ:

Hred = − 12μ∂2

The coordinate register Rρ encodes the hyperradius ( nρ ∼ 12 qubits); the channel register is C = Lθ ⊗ L ϕ ⊗ A ⊗ V (nc = 15 qubits, 

d = 32 ,768), where the angular registers carry a hyperspherical harmonic DVR (Table 9.2, row: hyperspherical harmonic), the arrangement register A encodes three chemical channels (2 qubits, padded to 4), and V encodes vibrational states (3 qubits). The centrifugal term [Λ 2 + 15 /4] /(2 μρ 2) is diagonal in the FBR; the potential V (ρ, Ω) is pointwise diagonal in the DVR, where Λ 2 quadrature means Gauss-Jacobi with Jacobi(3/2, 3/2) for θ and Jacobi(1/2, 1/2) for ϕ. An exponential space advantage ( nx + nc qubits versus Nx × d amplitudes) gives a pref-erence toward digital quantum computation over classical systems, the polaron ( d ∼ 10 9, 40 qubits), collective neutrinos ( d ∼ 10 6, 34 qubits), and CH 4 rovibrational dynamics ( d ∼ 2000, 

Nx ∼ 10 16 , 65 qubits) are all classically intractable at the stated grid sizes. In summary, the architecture decomposes a quantum mechanical problem into a single set of grids, coupling internal states and applying their actions diagonally, with parallelism limited by the gate set and no-go theorems. Each system in Table 9.3 records XOR-class count NXOR and a mechanism label. The next section derives these asserted mechanisms, including carry-chain, discrete-symmetry, and DVR/QFT absorption, recording per-system specialization. 296 9.4 Off-Diagonal Coupling Mechanisms 

The interaction potential decomposes into diagonal and off-diagonal parts in the channel register (Eq. 9.2.4: The off-diagonal part is handled by XOR-class decomposition (Chapter 4). The gate count per Trotter step is given by Eq. 9.2.5. Carry-chain XOR classes (Section 9.4.1) arise from ladder operators that increment a quantum number encoded in binary. Discrete-symmetry XOR classes (Section 9.4.2) arise from permutation-type exchanges between physically distinct internal states. Each mecha-nism is derived once, then specialized to the systems of Table 9.3. 

9.4.1 Carry-Chain XOR Classes: Rank-k Ladder Operators 

Five systems in Table 9.3 have off-diagonal coupling generated by ladder operators ˆA± :

|x⟩ → | x ± k⟩ acting on an n-qubit register encoding an integer q = 0 , . . . , 2n − 1. The binary representation x = P 

> ℓ

2ℓqℓ makes each transition x → x + k a binary addition with carry propagation, and distinct XOR patterns ζ(x) = x ⊕ (x + k) over all valid x determines the XOR-class structure. 

General Derivation 

For rank k = 1 (unit increment), the XOR pattern ζ(x) = x ⊕ (x + 1) is determined by the position of the lowest zero bit in x. If x has its lowest zero at position ℓ (i.e. q0 = q1 = · · · =

qℓ−1 = 1, qℓ = 0), then the carry propagates through bits 0 , 1, . . . , ℓ , flipping all of them: 

ζ(x) = 1 · · · 1| {z } 

> ℓ+1

0 · · · 0| {z } 

> n−ℓ−1

= 2 ℓ+1 − 1. (9.4.1) The distinct XOR patterns are therefore {1, 3, 7, 15 , . . . , 2n − 1}, giving n distinct XOR classes. The ℓ-th class has Hamming weight ℓ + 1 and contains all transitions where the carry chain extends through ℓ + 1 bit positions. For rank k = 2 (double increment), ζ(x) = x ⊕ (x + 2). The lowest bit q0 is unaf-fected: adding by 2 is equivalent to incrementing bit q1 (with carry from position 1 onward), 297 and the carry-chain analysis applies to bits 1 , . . . , n − 1. The distinct XOR patterns are 

{2, 6, 14 , 30 , . . . }, where the rank-1 patterns left-shift by one bit and give n − 1 distinct classes. Broadly, rank-k increment with k = 2 p produces n − p classes, each with Hamming weight at most n − p.The focusing Clifford for a class with XOR pattern ζ of Hamming weight h = |hw( ζ)| and support S(ζ) = {s1, . . . , s h} is the circuit derived in Chapter 4: a cascade of h − 1 CNOTs with a designated pivot qubit s1 as control, 

Uζ = Hsh · CNOT sh→sh−1 · · · CNOT sh→s1 , (9.4.2) concentrating the multi-qubit flip into a single-qubit Xsh and mapping to Zsh via the Hadamard. The Clifford depth is h; the CNOT count is h − 1. 

Physical Instances

No XOR-class decomposition is required, as the carry-chain structure is latent in the DVR eigenvectors. 298 The off-diagonal cost per Trotter step is NXOR × Nx × d/ 2 rotations plus 2 NXOR Clifford gates (negligible). The explicit carry-chain XOR patterns for the spin–orbit case are: Λ = 0 → 1 : ζ = 001 , Λ = 1 → 2 : ζ = 011 ,

giving three distinct patterns. The nuclear tensor force and rovibrational asymmetric-top coupling follow the same pattern left-shifted by one bit position (rank 2). 

9.4.2 Discrete-Symmetry XOR Classes: Permutations and Flavour Ex-change 

Two systems in Table 9.3 have off-diagonal coupling not from a ladder operator on a single quantum number but from a discrete exchange between physically distinct internal states. These generate XOR classes by a different mechanism than the carry chain. 

Arrangement-Channel Coupling (Reactive Scattering) 

The three chemical arrangement channels of H + H 2 are encoded in a 2-qubit register A

with |00 ⟩ = α1, |01 ⟩ = α2, |10 ⟩ = α3, |11 ⟩ = unused. The three arrangement pairs give three nonzero XOR patterns on 2 qubits: Pair ( α, β ) XOR ζ Focusing Clifford (1 , 2): 00 ↔ 01 01 1 ⊗ H

(1 , 3): 00 ↔ 10 10 H ⊗ 1

(2 , 3): 01 ↔ 10 11 CNOT 10 (H ⊗ 1)Each class requires one UCR pass over Rρ with d/ 2 rotations per site. The total off-diagonal cost is 3 × Nρ × d/ 2 rotations. 299 Gell-Mann Decomposition (Collective Neutrino Oscillations)

Mean-field or spectral truncation of the angular distribution can reduce this at the cost of physical fidelity. 

9.4.3 Systems Without Off-Diagonal Channel Coupling 

Two systems in Table 9.3 require no XOR-class decomposition. 

Polaron: QFT-diagonalized Hopping 

The electron hopping term −thop 

P 

> ⟨i,j ⟩

|i⟩⟨ j| is off-diagonal in the coordinate register R, not the channel register C. The QFT on R diagonalizes it: 

−thop 

X

> ⟨i,j ⟩

|i⟩⟨ j| QFT 

−−−→ X

> k

εk |k⟩⟨ k|, εk = −2thop cos(2 πk/N s). (9.4.4) 300 The electron–phonon coupling is fully diagonal in the Gauss–Hermite DVR of the phonon registers. The DVR’s tensor-product structure UDVR = NMq=1 U (q)DVR arises from mutually commuting phonon modes [ aq, a † 

> q′

] = δqq ′ . Each per-mode DVR costs O(N 2 

> p

) gates; the full channel-register transform costs M × O (N 2 

> p

) rather than O(d2) = O(N 2Mp ), exploiting the product structure. 

HHG: DVR-Absorbed Dipole Coupling and Time-Dependent Protocol 

The single-active-electron Hamiltonian 9.3.9 contains only one coupling between angular momentum channels: the dipole interaction E(t) r cos θ. This coupling is diagonal in the DVR, defining the Gauss–Legendre DVR on L: the potential Vion (r)+ E(t)r cos θ is pointwise diagonal at each angular quadrature point θm. No XOR-class decomposition is needed; the entire angular coupling is handled by the DVR round trip. The time-dependent field is accommodated by the split-operator framework: the M¨ obius coefficients rescale classically at each step while the circuit template is invariant. The M¨ obius coefficients rescale with E(tn) at each Trotter step, so the circuit template is invariant and only classical angle rescaling is required. In qubitization, each distinct field value requires a new PREPARE oracle, multiplying the oracle count by the number of distinct time steps (∼ 23 ,000 for a 10-cycle pulse at 800 nm)[307]. 

9.4.4 Summary of Off-Diagonal Coupling

9.5 Extension to Other Orthogonal Subspaces 

Section 9.2.1 translates DVR transforms to quantum circuits to satisfy the amenability cri-terion (Section 9.2.1), and Table 9.2 catalogs the closed-form diagonalizations for systems treated in Section 9.3. This section extends the catalog with four additional families not repre-sented in those systems: symmetry-adapted bases derived from molecular point groups, Bloch crystal-momentum bases for periodic systems, wavelet multiresolution bases for potentials with disparate length scales, and geometry-dependent configuration-interaction transforms for strongly correlated electronic states. 

9.5.1 Symmetry-Adapted Bases and Point-Group Reduction Block-diagonalization by Finite Symmetry Groups 

Let G be a finite group of order |G| acting on the channel register by a unitary representation 

g 7 → Ug, with [ H, U g] = 0 for all g ∈ G. By Maschke’s theorem, HC decomposes into irreducible representations (irreps) [308]: 

HC = M

> Γ

H⊕mΓ 

> Γ

, (9.5.1) 303 where the direct sum runs over all irreps Γ of G, mΓ is the multiplicity, and dim HΓ = dΓ.The projector onto the Γ-sector is 

PΓ = dΓ

|G|

X

> g∈G

χΓ(g)∗ Ug, (9.5.2) where χΓ(g) = Tr[ Ug|HΓ ] is the character of g in irrep Γ [308]. Since [ H, U g] = 0, the Hamil-tonian is block-diagonal in the symmetry-adapted basis: H = L 

> Γ

H|HΓ . Dynamics initiated in a single symmetry sector remains in that sector, with an effective channel dimension for each sector dΓ ≪ d.

Symmetry Projector Circuit Implementation 

Representation matrices Ug for molecular point groups are permutations on the channel basis. A permutation matrix on nc qubits is a product of SWAP and controlled-NOT gates: if g maps basis state |s⟩ 7 → | g(s)⟩, then Ug is the circuit that, for each bit position k where 

g(s)k̸ = sk, applies a CNOT or SWAP conditioned on the appropriate control pattern. The circuit depth is O(nc) per group element. The symmetry-adapted basis change Usym that simultaneously block-diagonalizes all Ug

is constructed from the character table of G and the Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization of projected states {PΓ|s⟩} . This produces a real orthogonal matrix (for groups with real char-acters, e.g. all molecular point groups) of dimension d × d, compiles by CSD at cost O(d2)(same as Legendre DVR). Effective DVR within each irrep is dΓ × dΓ, costing O(d2Γ). 

XOR-Class Structure Under Symmetry 

The permutation g maps |s⟩ → | g(s)⟩, so its XOR pattern on C is ζg(s) = s ⊕ g(s). For a group element g of cycle type ( c1, c 2, . . . ) acting on the binary-encoded channel index, the set {ζg(s) : s = 0 , . . . , d − 1} is fixed by the group multiplication table and can be classically precomputed. The number of distinct XOR patterns over all g ∈ G is bounded by |G| − 1. For molecular point groups relevant to polyatomic dynamics (e.g. C2v with |G| = 4, Td with 304 |G| = 24, Oh with |G| = 48), symmetry compresses the XOR catalog as well as the channel dimension. 

Example: C2v Symmetry in H 2O

The water molecule belongs to C2v = {E, C 2, σ v, σ ′

> v

} with four one-dimensional irreps A1,

A2, B1, B2. For the rovibrational register of Section 9.3.6 ( nc = 14 qubits, deff ≈ 231, padded to 1024), the symmetry-adapted block structure reduces the effective channel dimension per sector to approximately 256. The per-Trotter-step UCR cost falls by a factor of four within each symmetry block, and the off-diagonal Coriolis coupling (Section 9.3.6) connects only states within the same symmetry block: C2v selection rules forbid B1 ↔ B2 coupling, reducing the number of active XOR classes by half. 

9.5.2 Bloch Basis for Periodic and Lattice Systems Translational Symmetry and Crystal Momentum 

For a Hamiltonian with discrete translational symmetry under lattice translation by a,

Ta H T † 

> a

= H, Ta|Rj ⟩ = |Rj+1 ⟩, (9.5.3) the Bloch theorem states that eigenstates factor as |ψn,k ⟩ = eikR |un,k ⟩, where k ∈ [−π/a, π/a )is the crystal momentum and n is the band index [309]. In the REQWIEM register architec-ture, the coordinate register X encodes the Ns = 2 nx lattice sites Rj = ja . The QFT on X

implements the discrete Fourier transform 

FNs |Rj ⟩ = 1

√NsNs−1X

> k=0

e2πijk/N s |k⟩, (9.5.4) mapping site basis to crystal momentum basis. This transform is already present as the kinetic-energy step of the REQWIEM Trotter sequence. 305 Multi-Band Channel Register 

For a system with Nb bands, the channel register C encodes the band index n = 0 , . . . , N b −1, requiring nc = ⌈log 2 Nb⌉ qubits. The single-band tight-binding Hamiltonian of Section 9.3.2 generalizes to 

H = X

> n,k

> n̸=n′

X

> k

> n,k

εn(k)|n, k ⟩⟨ n, k | is encoded as a UCR on C with the band dispersions as angle vectors, evaluated after the QFT on X . Interband coupling Vnn ′ (k) is off-diagonal in the band index and handled by the XOR-class protocol: the XOR pattern ζ = n ⊕ n′ on the 

nc-qubit band register, focused by the same Clifford construction derived in Chapter 4. 

Dispersion Encoding and Momentum-Dependent UCR 

Band dispersion εn(k) is nonlinear in crystal momentum k. The kinetic phase e−iε n(k)∆ t in the Trotter step must be applied conditioned on both the band index n ∈ C and the crystal momentum k ∈ X (after QFT). This is a UCR on X multiplexed by C:

Ukin =

> Nb−1

X

> n=0

> Ns−1

X

> k=0

εn(k)|k⟩⟨ k|

!

. (9.5.6) This is the DVR/UCR step structure, with a band dispersion table {εn(k)} playing the role of a potential energy surface. The M¨ obius inversion of Chapter 7 converts the tabulated dispersion to Gray-code UCR angles at cost O(Nb × Ns) classically with zero additional gate overhead. 

Scope 

This construction encompasses multi-band Hubbard models (orbital and spin channels in C,site index in X ), the Kondo lattice (localized spin register ⊗ conduction electron register), 306 and topological insulators (Chern bands with Berry curvature encoded in the off-diagonal coupling Vnn ′ (k)), leaving the circuit template invariant with specific register assignment and M¨ obius coefficient tables. 

9.5.3 Wavelet Multiresolution Basis Motivation 

A uniformly-spaced DVR allocates equal resolution across all of X . For potentials with disparate length scales, such as deep core repulsion at r ≲ 1 a0 and diffuse van der Waals attraction at r ∼ 10–100 a0, most grid points lie in the smooth asymptotic region and contribute negligible potential variation, inflating Nx without improving accuracy. This is seen clearly in Chapter 6, where n = 13 , N = 8192 was required for a single-channel He-He scattering event. A multiresolution basis concentrates grid points where the potential varies rapidly, reducing effective Nx for a fixed error target. 

Quantum Wavelet Transform 

The Haar wavelet transform on nx qubits is the recursion 

W (1) :

 |2j⟩|2j + 1 ⟩

 7 →

(|2j⟩ + |2j + 1 ⟩)/√2(|2j⟩ − | 2j + 1 ⟩)/√2

 , j = 0 , . . . , 2nx−1 − 1, (9.5.7) applied iteratively from the coarsest scale down. On a quantum register, each level of recur-sion is a layer of Hadamard gates on alternating qubits, interleaved with CNOT gates that propagate the separation. The full nx-qubit Haar transform requires 

N (Haar) CNOT = 2 nx − 1, N (Haar)  

> H

= 2 nx , (9.5.8) gates, with depth O(nx). The butterfly structure parallelizes across 2 nx sites. The Daubechies 

D4 wavelet replaces each Hadamard with a 2 ×2 orthogonal matrix 

h0 h1

h2 h3

, implementable as a single Ry rotation sandwiched between two CNOTs, adding a factor of 3 to the CNOT count while improving the approximation order from 1 (Haar) to 2 (D 4). 307 Adaptive Potential Encoding 

Smooth potential regions in the wavelet basis are represented by low-frequency scaling coef-ficients while sharp features are captured by high-frequency detail coefficients at fine scales. The multilinear coefficient aS for a high-order subset S (large |S|) is negligible when the potential is smooth at the corresponding scale, so most rotations are near zero and can be truncated below a threshold θcut without accumulating Trotter error. The effective number of nontrivial rotations per Trotter step falls from Nx × d to N (eff)  

> x

× d, where 

N (eff)  

> x

= # {k : |ϕk| ≥ θcut } ≪ Nx, (9.5.9) for potentials that are smooth on the coarse wavelet scale. Potential structure and truncation threshold dictate the sparsity ratio N (eff)  

> x

/N x, estimated estimated system by system; the reduction can be substantial for long-range potentials with Coulomb or dispersion tails. 

Limitation: Approximate DVR Diagonality 

A uniform-grid DVR has the property that the potential is diagonal at the quadrature points: 

⟨θm|V |θm′ ⟩ = V (θm)δmm ′ . In the wavelet basis, exactness is lost, where the potential operator 

V is not diagonal in the wavelet basis, being approximately sparse with off-diagonal elements decaying as wavelet smoothness order increases. For the Daubechies D 2p family, off-diagonal elements in the wavelet basis decay as O(∆ x2p) where ∆ x is the fine-scale grid spacing [310]. Using the wavelet transform as UDVR therefore introduces a truncation error proportional to the off-diagonal residual, balanced against the grid compression gained. For potentials satisfying the Nyquist condition of Chapter 2, the D4 wavelet at p = 2 achieves fourth-order accuracy, and the off-diagonal residual is smaller than the Trotter error for grid spacings satisfying the coherent propagation criteria. 308 9.5.4 Geometry-Dependent Configuration-Interaction Transforms 

R-Dependent Electronic Basis Problem 

For a diatomic molecule with strong avoided crossings or dense conical intersection seams, neither a fixed adiabatic basis (singular at the crossing) nor a fixed diabatic basis (approxi-mate everywhere) is optimal. A geometry-dependent unitary UCI (R) diagonalizing the d × d

electronic Hamiltonian matrix He(R) at each internuclear distance can be written as 

UCI (R) He(R) U †

> CI

(R) = diag ␀E1(R), . . . , E d(R), (9.5.10) provides the adiabatic potential energy surfaces Ek(R) as the diagonal entries. The columns of UCI (R) are the adiabatic electronic eigenstates at geometry R, obtained from ab initio electronic structure calculation (CASSCF, MRCI, EOM-CC) at each reference geometry. 

Circuit Implementation as a Coordinate-Multiplexed UCR 

The geometry-dependent transform on a quantum cirucit is a unitary acting on the channel register, controlled by the coordinat register: 

UCI =

> Nx−1

X

> i=0

|ri⟩⟨ ri|X ⊗ UCI (ri)C . (9.5.11) This is a uniformly controlled unitary (UCU) on C, parameterized by X . Each UCI (ri) is a 

d×d unitary, compiled by CSD [255] into O(d2) CNOT and Rz gates. The full UCU therefore costs 

CCI = Nx × O (d2), (9.5.12) gates, compared to the geometry-independent DVR which costs O(d2) regardless of Nx. This is the loss of amortization identified in Table 9.2: the geometry-independent DVR acts on C

while X is in superposition, requiring the geometry-dependent UCU to branch over all Nx

coordinate values. 309 Diabatization and Residual Off-Diagonal Structure 

After application of UCI , the potential is diagonal (adiabatic surfaces Ek(R)), but the kinetic energy acquires off-diagonal derivative coupling matrix elements (NACMEs): 

Fkl (R) = ⟨ϕk(R)|∇ R|ϕl(R)⟩ = ⟨k|UCI (R) ∇R U †

> CI

(R)|l⟩, (9.5.13) arising from R-dependence in the adiabatic basis. These are the nonadiabatic coupling matrix elements seen throughout this dissertation. Derivative coupling in the split-operator method contributes an off-diagonal term to the potential step, entering through the commutator [T, U CI ]. This is handled by the XOR-class protocol with coupling matrix elements 

V (NAC)  

> kl

(ri) ≈ − 12μFkl (ri) + Fkl (ri+1 )2∆ r , (9.5.14) estimated by finite difference on the coordinate grid. Each off-diagonal pair ( k, l ) with Fkl ̸ = 0 contributes one XOR class (with pattern k ⊕ l) and one UCR pass. A fully diabatic treatment avoids the NACMEs by choosing a fixed UCI that approx-imately diagonalizes He over nuclear configuration space. Residual off-diagonal elements 

Vkl (R) are treated by the XOR-class protocol, as in Sections 9.3.3–9.3.4. Geometry-dependent UCU becomes unnecessary, the DVR amortization is recovered, and the total cost returns to O(d2) + Nx × d × NXOR per Trotter step. Whether the adiabatic UCU or the diabatic XOR approach is preferred depends on the density of avoided crossings: a dense seam (many 

R-values with strong coupling) favors the adiabatic UCU; an isolated crossing or a slowly varying coupling favors the diabatic approach. 

9.6 REQWIEM as a Front-End for Quantum Krylov Subspace diagonaliza-tion 

Real-time propagation and eigenvalue extraction are complementary tasks. REQWIEM is de-signed to evolves an initial state |ψ0⟩ under the coupled-channel Hamiltonian H and produces the time-dependent state |ψ(t)⟩ along with the expectation values of physical observables, 310 channel populations, coordinate-space densities, and transition dipoles at each Trotter step. Eigenvalues are not a direct output of the propagation; extracting them requires additional structure. The real-time variant of Quantum Krylov subspace diagonalization (QKSD) [311–313] builds a projected representation of H from a set of time-displaced states {| ψ(tk)⟩} m−1 

> k=0

,solving a classical generalized eigenvalue problem in the projected subspace. Extraction to low-lying energy eigenvalues never requires a full d × Nx-dimensional Hamiltonian diago-nalization. This section derives the overlap and Hamiltonian matrix elements needed for QKSD from the REQWIEM propagator, constructs the controlled-REQWIEM circuit re-quired for their estimation on a fault-tolerant device, and analyzes conditions under which the combination is advantageous. 

9.6.1 The Real-Time Krylov Subspace 

Let |ψ0⟩ ∈ H X ⊗ H C be a normalized initial state with a Krylov time step ∆ τ > 0. The real-time Krylov basis of dimension m is 

Km = span |ψ(tk)⟩ m−1 

> k=0

, |ψ(tk)⟩ = e−iHk ∆τ |ψ0⟩. (9.6.1) The overlap matrix S ∈ Cm×m and the Krylov–Hamiltonian matrix HK ∈ Cm×m in this basis are 

Sjk = ⟨ψ(tj )|ψ(tk)⟩ = ⟨ψ0|eiHj ∆τ e−iHk ∆τ |ψ0⟩ = ⟨ψ0|e−iH (k−j)∆ τ |ψ0⟩, (9.6.2) (HK )jk = ⟨ψ(tj )|H|ψ(tk)⟩ = i ∂ tk Sjk . (9.6.3) The projected eigenvalue problem 

HK c = E S c , (9.6.4) is solved classically. Its eigenvalues {E(m) 

> n

} converge to the true eigenvalues {En} of H as m

increases and as the initial state |ψ0⟩ acquires overlap with more eigenstates [313]. 311 9.6.2 Overlap Matrix Toeplitz Structure 

Sjk depends only on the time difference ℓ = k − j, not j and k independently (Eq. 9.6.2) 

Sjk = C(ℓ ∆τ ), ℓ = k − j, (9.6.5) where 

C(t) = ⟨ψ0|e−iHt |ψ0⟩, (9.6.6) is the initial state’s autocorrelation function. The overlap matrix is Hermitian Toeplitz: 

Sjk = C(( k − j)∆ τ ) and Sjk = S∗ 

> kj

.Being Hermitian Toeplitz, the m × m matrix S is determined by m complex numbers 

{C(0) , C (∆ τ ), . . . , C (( m−1)∆ τ )}, the autocorrelation function sampled at integer multiples of ∆ τ . Rather than estimating m(m − 1) /2 independent overlaps, only m − 1 independent quantum circuits are needed (plus a known C(0) = 1). Hamiltonian matrix elements follow from Eq. 9.6.3 by finite difference: (HK )jk ≈ i C(( k − j + 1)∆ τ ) − C(( k − j − 1)∆ τ )2∆ τ , (9.6.7) determining HK by the same autocorrelation samples, at the cost of one additional point 

C(m∆τ ). The autocorrelation function C(ℓ∆τ ) is a complex-valued transition amplitude. Extrac-tion on a fault-tolerant device requires the controlled-Uℓ construction developed below. This overhead is specific to eigenvalue extraction via QKSD. Dynamical observables central to the preceding chapters (channel populations Pα(t) = ⟨ψ(t)|Πα|ψ(t)⟩, coordinate-space densities) are real-valued expectation values obtainable by measuring the channel register C after an uncontrolled REQWIEM propagation, with no ancilla qubit, no controlled gates, and no Hadamard test. The controlled-REQWIEM circuit of Section 9.6.4 is therefore optional, in-voked only when spectroscopic eigenvalue resolution is the target rather than time-dependent dynamics. 312 9.6.3 Overlap Estimation via the Hadamard Test 

On a fault-tolerant device, |ψ(t)⟩ is not directly readable. Each complex autocorrelation value C(ℓ∆τ ) is estimated by Hadamard test: 

|0⟩A H • H Re[ C]

|ψ0⟩ Uℓ

(9.6.8) where Uℓ = e−iHℓ ∆τ is the REQWIEM propagator for ℓ Krylov steps and H denotes the Hadamard gate on the ancilla. Measuring the ancilla in the X basis (equation 9.6.8) gives Re[ C(ℓ∆τ )]; replacing the final Hadamard with S†H (i.e. measuring in the Y basis) gives Im[ C(ℓ∆τ )]. The probability of outcome +1 in each case is Pr(+1) X = 12

␀1 + Re[ C(ℓ∆τ )] , (9.6.9) Pr(+1) Y = 12

␀1 + Im[ C(ℓ∆τ )] . (9.6.10) Each estimate requires O(ε−2) shots to achieve additive precision ε by standard concentration bounds. For an m-point Krylov expansion, the total shot count is 2( m − 1) × O (ε−2), linear in m.

9.6.4 Controlled-REQWIEM: Circuit Construction and Overhead 

The Hadamard test requires the controlled-Uℓ operation, where Uℓ is applied if and only if the ancilla is in state |1⟩. Since Uℓ = ( U∆τ )ℓ is a product of Trotter steps, it suffices to construct the controlled single-step operator CU ∆τ and repeat it ℓ times. Each Trotter step consists of three types of operations on X ⊗ C : QFT layers on X , UCR diagonal phases on X or C, and Clifford XOR-class focusers on C. The controlled versions are constructed as follows. The QFT on nx qubits is a sequence of Hadamard and controlled-phase gates [292]. For the Hadamard gate, CH costs 3 CNOTs and 4 Rz gates [314]. For a controlled phase P (θ), the controlled version CP (θ) is the Toffoli-phase gate costing 2 CNOTs and 3 Rz gates. The 313 total overhead factor for the controlled-QFT relative to the uncontrolled QFT is O(1) in gate count. The UCR on register R (n qubits) is the Gray-code diagonal unitary diag( eiθ 0 , . . . , e iθ 2n−1 ). Under ancilla control, this becomes the block-diagonal operator 

c-UCR = |0⟩⟨ 0|A ⊗ 1R + |1⟩⟨ 1|A ⊗ diag( eiθ k ), (9.6.11) diagonal on the ( n + 1)-qubit register A ⊗ R :

c-UCR = diag ␀1, . . . , 1

| {z }

> 2n

, e iθ 0 , . . . , e iθ 2n−1

. (9.6.12) Rather than converting each gate of the n-qubit UCR to its controlled version, the controlled-UCR simply acts as itself on n + 1 qubits, with the ancilla A entering the parity network as an ordinary qubit. The angle vector for the ( n + 1)-qubit compilation is the zero-padded extension ˜θ = (0 , . . . , 0

| {z }

> 2n

, θ 0, . . . , θ 2n−1) ∈ R2n+1

Cc-UCR = 2 n+1 CNOTs + 2 n+1 Rz , (9.6.14) a factor of 2 overhead in both CNOTs and Rz gates relative to the uncontrolled n-qubit UCR (2 n CNOTs, 2 n Rz ). The focusing Cliffords Uζ are products of Hadamard and CNOT gates on C. Their con-trolled versions are standard controlled-Clifford circuits: each CNOT becomes a Toffoli (6 CNOTs) and each Hadamard becomes a controlled-Hadamard (3 CNOTs) [315]. This over-head is negligible relative to the UCR cost, with total Clifford depth scales as O(nc) and 

nc ≪ 2nc per XOR class. 314 The Hadamard test for a single autocorrelation value at lag ℓ requires ℓ applications of the controlled single-step circuit. The total gate count for all m − 1 lags is 

CQKSD = 2 × CREQWIEM ×

> ℓ=1

9.6.5 Error Budget for the Krylov Overlap and Optimal Step Size 

QKSD eigenvalue approximation resolution improves as the Krylov window tmax = ( m−1)∆ τ

increases: eigenvalues separated by ∆ E require tmax ≳ π/ ∆E to be resolved in the projected spectrum. Autocorrelation fidelity degrades by errors accumulated over the propagation. On an ideal device, three independent sources contribute to the overlap error at lag ℓ∆τ ,following the error budget of Chapter 5: 

|Ccircuit (ℓ∆τ ) − Cexact (ℓ∆τ )| ≤ εT(ℓ∆τ, ∆t) + εdisc (ℓ∆τ ) + εsynth (ℓ∆τ, ∆t), (9.6.16) For the second-order Strang splitting with per-step error εstep ≤ C2 ∥[T, V ]∥ ∆t3, accu-mulated Trotter contribution at propagation time t = ℓ∆τ is 

εT(ℓ∆τ, ∆t) ≤ C2 ∥[T, V ]∥ ℓ∆τ ∆t2, (9.6.17) decreasing quadratically with ∆ t [126]. The finite grid Hamiltonian Hgrid differs from the continuum Hamiltonian by an operator-norm distance δH = ∥Hgrid −Hcont ∥, determined by the grid spacing ∆ r = L/N x and potential surface smoothness. Standard Hamiltonian perturbation theory gives 

εdisc (ℓ∆τ ) ≤ δH ℓ∆τ, (9.6.18) For potentials satisfying the aliasing bound of Chapter 2, ( ∥V ∥∞/T max ≤ 1), δH decreases exponentially with nx on a well-resolved grid, and this term is subdominant. It is nevertheless 315 a hard floor on the overlap fidelity: no reduction of ∆ t can improve it, and it imposes a ceiling on the achievable Krylov window, 

tmax < η/δ H , (9.6.19) beyond which the discretization error alone exhausts the fidelity budget η.Each of the Nrot parameterized rotations per Trotter step (Eq. 9.2.5) is synthesized from the Clifford+ T gate set to per-gate precision εθ [204]. Over Nsteps = ℓ∆τ / ∆t Trotter steps, coherent accumulation of synthesis errors gives 

εsynth (ℓ∆τ, ∆t) ≤ ℓ∆τ

∆t Nrot εθ, (9.6.20) increasing as ∆ t decreases, since more Trotter steps are required to cover the same propa-gation time. After subtracting the discretization floor, the effective fidelity budget is ηeff = η −δH tmax ,and the joint constraint at the maximum lag tmax = ( m − 1)∆ τ is

This is a second, indirect benefit of the smooth-potential regime, where smooth potentials reduce both the Trotter error directly and the synthesis precision required to support the resulting step size. For the Ar ∗ 

> 3

trimer ( d = 64, Nx = 2 24 , nc = 6, NXOR = 1, Ndiag = 1), the per-step rotation count from Eq. 9.2.5 is Nrot ≈ Nx × nc × 2 ≈ 2 × 10 8. With ∥[T, V ]∥ ∼ 10 −5 a.u., m = 20, ∆τ = 10 3 a.u. ( tmax = 1 .9 × 10 4 a.u.), and target overlap fidelity η = 10 −3:The critical per-gate precision from equation 9.6.23 is ε(crit)  

> θ

≈ 7 × 10 −15 , achievable with CT ≈ 141 T -gates per rotation via bare Ross–Selinger synthesis ( ≈ 28 after opti-mization [204]). At εθ = 10 −14 , which lies above this threshold, the minimum combined Trotter+synthesis error is fmin ≈ 1.2 × 10 −3 > η : no step size can achieve the target fidelity. The small value of ∥[T, V ]∥ relative to the LCU 1-norm α = d N x ⟨| V |⟩ permits Trotter step sizes of O(10 1) a.u. rather than O(10 −3) a.u., keeping the number of steps moderate. A QKSD front-end based on qubitization, whose query count scales as O(αt ) with α ∼ 10 6 a.u., would require ∼ 10 10 queries for the same Krylov window, a factor of ∼ 10 7 more circuit evaluations than the REQWIEM-based approach, even after the synthesis-corrected step count, assuming the nonadiabatic system can be implemented. 

9.6.6 Physical Observables from REQWIEM Within QKSD 

REQWIEM does generates the full state |ψ(t)⟩ ∈ H X ⊗ H C , from which physical observ-ables are extracted by partial measurement or by operator expectation values estimated via additional Hadamard test circuits. Channel-register populations are given as 

Pα(t) = ∥⟨ α|ψ(t)⟩∥ 2 = ⟨ψ(t)|Πα|ψ(t)⟩, Πα = |α⟩⟨ α|C ⊗ 1X , (9.6.25) where α labels an electronic surface, angular momentum channel, spin projection, or neutrino flavor. These populations are estimable by measuring the channel register C directly after the propagation circuit. These are the physical observables most relevant to nonadiabatic 317 dynamics, tracking population transfer between coupled surfaces, recurrence of wavepacket amplitude, and the time-dependent branching ratio between product channels. Within QKSD, channel populations at the Krylov times provide observable-resolved spec-tral information. The population autocorrelation in channel α,

Cα(t) = ⟨ψ0|Πα e−iHt |ψ0⟩, (9.6.26) has a spectral decomposition Cα(t) = P

> n

⟨ψ0|Πα|n⟩⟨ n|ψ0⟩e−iE nt, where |n⟩ are energy eigen-states. Fourier transforming Cα(t) resolves the energy spectrum weighted by an overlap of each eigenstate with the α-th channel, providing state-resolved partial densities of states without additional circuit modifications. The same Hadamard test circuit estimating C(t)for QKSD is modified by inserting Π α between the state preparation and the propagator: 

Cα(t) = ⟨ψ0|Παe−iHt |ψ0⟩ ≈ ⟨ ψ0|eiHt Παe−iHt |ψ0⟩ t→0, (9.6.27) estimated by a Hadamard test with the observable Π α inserted into the circuit. 

9.6.7 Krylov Collapse and Optimal Subspace Dimension 

The overlap matrix S approaches singularity with increasing Krylov dimension m, with time-displaced states becoming approximately linearly dependent as tmax exceeds the system’s recurrence time. This is the Krylov collapse problem [313]. In the presence of shot noise in autocorrelation estimates, the overlap matrix’s condition number S grows as O(ε−1eκm ) for some system-dependent rate κ, making the generalized eigenvalue problem 9.6.4 numerically ill-conditioned. A standard mitigation technique is to threshold the eigenvalues of S: eigenvalues below a cutoff εS are discarded and the projected problem is solved in the reduced subspace. The optimal m balances spectral resolution against conditioning, dependent on target precision and shot budget. For coupled-channel systems, the symmetry-block structure of Section 9.5.1 reduces the effective Krylov dimension needed. If the initial state |ψ0⟩ belongs to a single symmetry sector of dimension dΓ, then the Krylov subspace is confined to that sector, and m

318 need only be large enough to resolve the spectrum within HΓ rather than all of HC . Combined with a wide Krylov window accessible at small ∥[T, V ]∥, this makes the REQWIEM-QKSD combination well-suited to mid-size coupled-channel systems where classical Lanczos encoun-ters memory limits but full-time quantum propagation to convergence is unnecessary. With the protocol, its extensions, and its eigenvalue extraction capability established, the next section identifies physical domains to which the framework applies. 

9.7 Physics Subfields Amenable to REQWIEM 

The coupled-channel structure of equation 9.1.2 emerges wherever a quantum system de-composes into an (effectively) continuous degree of freedom (a coordinate, a propagation distance, a lattice site index) coupled to a discrete internal structure whose free evolution is diagonal in a basis different from the basis in which the coupling is diagonal. This sec-tion identifies some of these classes of problems that admit a REQWIEM-amenable register assignment, derives the Hamiltonian decomposition and XOR-class structure for each, and records the register architecture and gate count in the unified format of Table 9.4. The criterion for inclusion is the REQWIEM-amenability of Eq. 9.2.14: the interaction potential must be diagonalizable on the channel register by an efficiently implementable unitary. Satisfactory systems inherit the full protocol: UCR potential encoding, M¨ obius in-version, XOR-class focusing, and potential Toeplitz autocorrelation for QKSD. 

9.7.1 Nuclear and Hadronic Physics 

Two extensions substantially broaden the scope of applicability within nuclear physics, being multi-nucleon transfer reactions and the no-core shell model for light nuclei. 

Multi-Nucleon Transfer Reactions 

Consider the deuteron-stripping reaction d + AX → p + A+1 X∗ in the distorted-wave Born approximation (DWBA) [316]. The post-form transition amplitude requires the simultaneous 319 propagation of the proton–nucleus and neutron–nucleus relative motions, coupled by the residual pn interaction Vpn (rpn ). The Hamiltonian in the Jacobi coordinate system ( rp, rn) is 

H = − 12μp

∇2 

> rp

− 12μn

∇2 

> rn

+ Up(rp) + Un(rn) + Vpn (|rp − rn|), (9.7.1) where Up and Un are the proton and neutron optical potentials. Expanding in the coupled basis |lp, l n, L, S, J, M J ⟩ of angular momenta for each Jacobi vector and their couplings, the Hamiltonian takes the form 9.1.2 with two coordinate registers: 

X = Rp ⊗ R n, C = Lp ⊗ L n ⊗ S ⊗ J , (9.7.2) where Rp and Rn encode the proton and neutron radial coordinates on Nr-point grids, and Lp, Ln, S, J encode the angular momentum quantum numbers. The kinetic energy is diagonal after QFT on each of Rp and Rn independently. The optical potentials Up(rp) and 

Un(rn) are diagonal in the DVR of each radial register and are channel register independent, contributing a UCR on Rp (or Rn) multiplexed by the identity on C. Residual interactions 

Vpn couple all partial waves of both particles, giving off-diagonal channel coupling. In the partial-wave basis, matrix elements of Vpn are

The selection rules ∆ lp = 0 , ±λ and ∆ ln = 0 , ±λ determine XOR-class structure on Lp ⊗ L n: each multipole λ of Vpn contributes O(log lmax ) XOR classes via carry-chain analysis (Section 9.3.4), applied independently to Lp and Ln. For λmax = 2 (quadrupole residual interaction) and lmax = 10: approximately 2 × 4 = 8 XOR classes. 

No-Core Shell Model Dynamics 

The no-core shell model (NCSM) represents the nuclear many-body state in a harmonic oscillator (HO) basis truncated at Nmax oscillator quanta [317]. For a nucleus with mass 320 number A, the intrinsic Hamiltonian in Jacobi coordinates is

In present register assignment, the center-of-mass Jacobi coordinate ρ1 provides the coordinate register X and all remaining Jacobi coordinates ( ρ2, . . . , ρA−1) together with the spin-isospin quantum numbers ( msi , m ti ) form the channel register C. The HO basis for C diagonalizes the center-of-mass kinetic term and the harmonic confining potential. Residual NN and 3NF interactions are off-diagonal and handled by XOR-class decomposition on the spin-isospin register. For 4He with Nmax = 8, the channel dimension is d ∼ ␀Nmax +3 3

×(2 s+1) A ×(2 t+1) A ∼ 10 3.The reduction factor ∼ d = 10 3 over classical NCSM diagonalization is modest for light nuclei but grows rapidly: for 12 C at Nmax = 6, d ∼ 10 6 and the classical NCSM matrix dimension 

∼ 10 9 is already at the limit of present supercomputer memory.

In a two-band superconductor (e.g. MgB 2, Fe-based pnictides), the BCS mean-field Hamil-tonian in Nambu notation is [319]

The quasiparticle dispersion ξnk τz is diagonal in the Nambu basis (FBR); the gap ∆ nn ′ (k)τ+ is off-diagonal in both band and particle–hole indices. The gap matrix contributes one XOR class per off-diagonal band pair: for a two-band system, |τ = + , n = 1 ⟩ ↔ | τ = −, n = 2 ⟩ gives XOR pattern ζ = 11 on the 2-qubit channel register, focused by a CNOT followed by a Hadamard. Interband scattering V01 (k) (pair breaking between bands) gives a second XOR class ζ = 01, focused by a Hadamard on the band qubit. The total XOR count is 2, and the Clifford depth is ≤ 2 gates. The k-dependent gap ∆ nn ′ (k) is encoded by the M¨ obius inversion of Chapter 7, treating the gap as an arbitrary smooth function on the Nk-point momentum grid. 

Topological Insulators and Chern Bands 

The Qi–Wu–Zhang model for a two-dimensional Chern insulator on a square lattice is [320] 

H(k) = sin kx σx + sin ky σy + ( m0 + cos kx + cos ky) σz , (9.7.8) 322 where σx,y,z act on the two-band channel register ( nc = 1 qubit, d = 2) and m0 is the Dirac mass parameter. The coordinate register encodes the two-dimensional crystal momentum 

k = ( kx, k y) using two nx-qubit registers Kx ⊗ K y, reached by QFT on the real-space position registers. The σz term is diagonal in the channel (FBR) basis, contributing a UCR on Kx ⊗K y

multiplexed by σz . The σx and σy terms are off-diagonal with XOR pattern ζ = 1 on the single channel qubit; both are focused by a Hadamard ( σx term) or a phase-then-Hadamard (σy term). The Chern number 

C = 12π

Ce2/h via the Kubo formula [321] applied to the time-dependent current–current correlator. The current-current correlator by itself is a channel-register observable of the type described in Section 9.6.6. 

Quantum Spin Liquids and Emergent Gauge Fields 

The Kitaev honeycomb model [322] is 

HKit = −Jx

X

> ⟨ij ⟩x

σxi σxj − Jy

X

> ⟨ij ⟩y

σyi σyj − Jz

X

> ⟨ij ⟩z

σzi σzj . (9.7.10) By the Jordan–Wigner transformation on the honeycomb lattice, this maps to a free Majo-rana fermion Hamiltonian in a static Z2 gauge field uij = ±1 on each bond [322]. For each gauge sector (fixed configuration of {uij }), the fermionic Hamiltonian is quadratic and can be solved classically. The quantum problem simultes the full gauge-unfixed dynamics includ-ing the flux excitations (visons). In the REQWIEM assignment, sublattice site indices are encoded in X and the gauge-sector label |{ uij }⟩ is encoded in C, with d = 2 Nbonds . The free-fermion Hamiltonian for each gauge sector is diagonal in the Bogoliubov quasiparticle basis, providing the FBR; the vison hopping (flux pair creation and annihilation) is off-diagonal in the gauge register and contributes XOR classes whose patterns are determined by the honeycomb lattice’s bond connectivity. Total XOR count is bounded by 3 Nunitcells (one per 323 bond type x, y, z per unit cell), and each class is focused by a Hadamard on the relevant bond qubit. 

9.7.3 Quantum Optics and Cavity Quantum Electrodynamics Jaynes–Cummings and Tavis–Cummings Models 

The Jaynes–Cummings Hamiltonian for a single two-level atom (transition frequency ω0)coupled to a single cavity mode (frequency ωc, annihilation operator a) is [323] 

HJC = ωc a†a + ω0

2 σz + g (a†σ− + a σ +), (9.7.11) where g is the vacuum Rabi coupling. In the REQWIEM assignment, the cavity Fock states 

|n⟩ (n = 0 , . . . , N Fock − 1) are encoded in X using nx = ⌈log 2 NFock ⌉ qubits, and the atomic state | ↑⟩ , | ↓⟩ is encoded in C with nc = 1 qubit. The free-field term ωc a†a is diagonal in the Fock basis (FBR on X ) and the atomic term ω0σz /2 is diagonal in the spin-z basis (FBR on 

C). The Jaynes–Cummings interaction g (a†σ− + a σ +) is simultaneously off-diagonal in X

(by ±1 Fock excitation) and off-diagonal in C (by ±1 spin flip). The Gauss–Hermite DVR maps the Fock register to the quadrature displacement basis (as in Section 9.3.2), in which 

a + a† = √2 x is diagonal. The interaction then becomes 

g (a†σ− + a σ +) DVR 

−−−→ g√2 xm σx + O(n−1/2), (9.7.12) where xm is the DVR quadrature point and the correction O(n−1/2) vanishes in the large-

NFock limit. The σx coupling is off-diagonal with XOR pattern ζ = 1 on the spin qubit, focused by a Hadamard. The full Trotter step is: 

e−iH JC ∆t ≈ e−iω cn∆t/ 2 e−iω 0σz ∆t/ 4 U † 

> HO

e−ig √2xmσx∆t UHO e−iω 0σz ∆t/ 4 e−iω cn∆t/ 2, (9.7.13) where UHO is the Gauss–Hermite DVR transform on X . The Tavis–Cummings generalization to Na atoms replaces the single-spin channel register with the collective Dicke register |J, M ⟩

(J = 0 , . . . , N a/2, M = −J, . . . , J ), encoded on nc = ⌈log 2(Na + 1) ⌉ qubits with d = Na + 1. 324 The coupling g√Na (a†J− + aJ +)/√J produces O(log Na) XOR classes from the ∆ M = ±1transitions in the Dicke ladder, following the same carry-chain analysis as the molecular spin–orbit case of Section 9.3.3. 

Ultrastrong Coupling Regime 

In the ultrastrong coupling (USC) regime, g/ω c ≥ 0.1, the rotating-wave approximation that justifies equation 9.7.11 breaks down and the full quantum Rabi Hamiltonian must be used [324]: 

HRabi = ωc a†a + ω0

2 σz + g (a† + a) σx. (9.7.14) The counter-rotating terms a†σ+ and a σ − (absent in the Jaynes–Cummings model) are now present. In the DVR, ( a† + a) → √2 xm, so the full coupling becomes g√2 xm σx, the same diagonal-times-Pauli structure as the rotating-wave case. Counter-rotating terms generate no additional XOR classes and add no circuit complexity beyond the Jaynes–Cummings case, giving a structural advantage of the DVR approach where the approximation useful in classical numerics has no benefit.

For a one-dimensional array of Ncav cavities with nearest-neighbor photon hopping amplitude 

Jc:

> i

aj is off-diagonal in the cavity site index; after QFT on X , it becomes diagonal in the crystal photon-momentum basis with dispersion −2Jc cos(2 πk/N cav ). The per-cavity atom–photon coupling maps to one UCR per cavity mode. Between-cavity hopping requires no XOR classes. For Ncav = 64 cavities each with NFock = 16 levels and Na = 2 atoms per cavity, the 325 channel dimension is d = 16 64 × 464 ≈ 10 154 , being classically intractable and in the regime where the nx + nc qubit encoding provides an exponential space advantage. 

9.7.4 Astrophysics and Relativistic Wave Equations Collective Neutrino Problem Extensions 

Section 9.3.5 treated the Mikheyev-Smirnov-Wolfenstein (MSW) system and collective neu-trino oscillation problems with three active flavors [325, 326]. Two extensions are immediate within the existing circuit template. Sterile neutrinos add a fourth flavor state |νs⟩ that does not interact via the weak force. The flavor register F extends to nf = 2 qubits ( d = 4, all four states occupied), and the PMNS matrix is replaced by the 4 × 4 unitary U (4) PMNS parameterized by three additional mixing angles and two additional charge-parity (CP)-violating phases. The DVR is U (4) PMNS ,again compiled by CSD at cost O(d2) = O(16) gates. Additional XOR classes from active– sterile transitions are νe ↔ νs, νμ ↔ νs, ντ ↔ νs, each with XOR pattern ζ = 11 on the 2-qubit flavor register, this is three additional XOR classes, each focused by CNOT + Hadamard. CP-violating phases in the PMNS matrix enter as complex entries in the DVR uni-tary UPMNS , requiring no modification to the XOR-class decomposition, being absorbed into UCR complex rotation angles via the M¨ obius inversion applied to the real and imag-inary parts of the potential matrix elements separately. The Jarlskog invariant JCP =Im[ Ue1Uμ2U ∗

> e2

U ∗

> μ1

] [327], controlling the magnitude of CP violation in neutrino oscillations, is accessible as a post-processed function of the channel-register density matrix elements 

ραβ (t) = ⟨ψ(t)|ΠαΠβ |ψ(t)⟩.

Neutron Star Crust and Superfluid Vortex Dynamics 

The inner crust of a neutron star contains a neutron superfluid coexisting with a crystalline nuclear lattice. The coupled superfluid–lattice dynamics is governed by scattering equations 326 for neutron quasiparticles scattering off lattice nuclei, taking the same form as the nucleon– nucleus optical model of Section 9.3.4 [328]. The coordinate register R encodes the neutron radial position within a Wigner–Seitz cell of radius RWS ∼ 50 fm, and the channel register

Black Hole Quasi-Normal Modes: Teukolsky Equation 

Gravitational perturbations of a Kerr black hole (mass M , spin a) are governed by the Teukolsky equation [329]. In Boyer–Lindquist coordinates, separating ψs(t, r, θ, ϕ ) =

327 for aω ̸ = 0. This is precisely the structure of the coupled-channel centrifugal problem: the angular register L encodes l = |s|, |s| + 1 , . . . , l max , the angular eigenvalue matrix sAlm (aω )is dense in the l-basis (FBR) and diagonal in the spheroidal harmonic basis (DVR), and the DVR transform is the matrix that diagonalizes equation 9.7.19 at the given frequency ω. The radial coordinate r encoded in R on a tortoise coordinate grid r∗ = r + 2 M ln |r/ (2 M ) − 1|

that compresses the near-horizon region. The spin weight s and azimuthal index m label distinct, non-coupled problems (no XOR class between different s or m); within a fixed (s, m ) sector, the l-channel coupling has selection rule ∆ l = ±2 from the a2ω2 cos 2 θ term (rank-2 in l, identical to the nuclear tensor force of Section 9.3.4). Quasi-normal mode frequencies ωQNM are the Teukolsky operator’s complex eigenvalues. They are directly accessible via the REQWIEM-QKSD combination of Section 9.6: propagate an initial wavepacket in the tortoise coordinate, form the autocorrelation function C(t) = 

⟨ψ0|e−iHt |ψ0⟩, and extract the complex poles of its Laplace transform by QKSD. The complex part of the QNM frequency (the decay rate 1 /τ QNM ) is accessible because the outgoing-wave boundary conditions on the tortoise grid impose a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian (via a complex absorbing potential at the grid boundary), shifting eigenvalues of HK off the real axis in direct proportion to the ringdown rate. 

9.7.5 Strong-Field Atomic Physics and Quantum Chaos 

An atom in an external static or oscillatory electric field, at energies near the ionization threshold, focuses on chaotic quantum dynamics. The hydrogen atom in a uniform field 

F ˆz separates in parabolic coordinates ( ξ, η ); the phase space is foliated by invariant tori labeled by a parabolic separation constant β. For any non-hydrogenic atom, the ionic core breaks the dynamical SO(4) symmetry of the Coulomb problem, producing a mixed regular– chaotic phase space with a quantum defect ( δl)-dependent structure and energy scaling 

ε = EF −1/2 [218, 330]. 328 Experimentally, the chaotic dynamics manifests in recurrence spectroscopy: the Fourier transform of the photoabsorption cross section at fixed ε is plotted versus the scaling vari-able w = F −1/4 and identifies peaks at scaled actions ˜Sk of the classical closed orbits, starting and ending at the nucleus [277]. Core scattering produces combination recurrences at ˜S = ˜Sk1 + ˜Sk2 absent in hydrogen, providing experimental signatures of non-integrability. Reproducing these spectra requires propagating a Rydberg wavepacket through ∼ 10 4–10 5

atomic time units while resolving d ∼ 64–128 angular momentum channels coupled by the Stark interaction and differentiated by the l-dependent core potential. 

Register Assignment and Hamiltonian Decomposition 

The single-active-electron Hamiltonian in the reduced radial representation ( χ = rψ ) is 

H = −12

∂2

∂r 2 + l2

2r2 − 1

r + Vc(r) + F r cos θ, (9.7.20) where Vc(r) encodes the non-hydrogenic core. This maps directly onto the REQWIEM reg-ister decomposition: 

X = R (nr qubits, radial grid) , C = L (nl qubits, angular momentum) . (9.7.21) The free-channel Hamiltonian in the FBR is H(FBR)  

> C

= l(l + 1) /(2 r2), diagonal in |l⟩. The Stark interaction F r cos θ is tridiagonal in l via dipole selection rule ⟨l| cos θ|l′⟩ ∝ δl′,l ±1. Since cos θ is defines the Gauss–Legendre quadrature (Table 9.2), the DVR round trip absorbs the full l–l′ coupling without an XOR-class decomposition: NXOR = 0. The Trotter step is e−iH∆t ≈ e−iTr ∆t/ 2 e−iVcent ∆t/ 2 U † 

> DVR

e−iVDVR ∆t UDVR e−iVcent ∆t/ 2 e−iTr ∆t/ 2, (9.7.22) where VDVR (ri, θ m) = −1/r i + Vc(ri) + F r i cos θm is diagonal at each DVR grid point. The single radial coordinate and absence of an electronic register or off-diagonal XOR step make this the simplest non-trivial instance of the coupled-channel framework. 329 Core Scattering as an Interferometric Mechanism 

The DVR round trip acts as a multipath interferometer on the angular register. At each Trot-ter step, UDVR splits each |l⟩ into d angular paths |θm⟩, each path accumulates a phase from the diagonal potential V (ri, θ m), and U † 

> DVR

recombines them. In hydrogen ( Vc = 0), a hidden parabolic symmetry causes the multi-step interferometer to preserve the parabolic quantum number n1: the DVR phases eventually returnn the l-distribution to its initial pattern. The core potential Vc(r) perturbs this, modifying the radial wavepacket shape between Trotter steps so r-values operated on by the angular interferometer no longer align with parabolic eigenstates. The l-distribution drifts stroboscopically (with synchronized oscillations), with drift rate controlled by quantum defects δl [331]. The quantum circuit encodes this mechanism without an explicit core-scattering op-erator is required. The l-dependent sensitivity arises from DVR transform weights Tml =

√wm ˜Pl(cos θm) interacting with the radial wavepacket shape at the grid points where Vc(r)is appreciable ( r ≲ 5 a0). 

Gate Count and Computational Advantage 

For the reference parameters of the lithium system ( nr = 12, Nx = 4096; nl = 6, d = 64), the per-step rotation count is 

Nrot = 3 Nx × d + 2 Nx = 3 × 4096 × 64 + 8192 ≈ 8 × 10 5, (9.7.23) comprising two centrifugal UCR half-steps (2 × Nx × d), one DVR potential step ( Nx × d), and two kinetic half-steps (2 × Nx). The DVR transform itself costs O(d2) elementary gates (concretely, 990 CNOTs for the parity-optimized Legendre CSD at d = 64), applied twice per step, and is negligible relative to the UCR cost ( ∼ 1%). The classical cost of the same Trotter step is the cost of d × d matrix–vector multiplies at each of Nx grid points: Cclassical = Nx × d2 ≈ 1.7 × 10 7 operations per step on a matrix of 330 approximate dimension Nx × d = 2 12 × 26 ≈ 2.62 × 10 5. The per-step reduction factor is 

Cclassical 

Nrot 

= Nx × d2

3 Nx × d + 2 Nx

≈ d

3 ≈ 21 . (9.7.24) Complexity reduction is linear in d, deriving from DVR amortization: the O(d2) basis-change cost is applied once on L while R remains in superposition, rather than at each of the Nx

grid points. 

Scaled-Energy Scans and Time-Dependent Fields 

Recurrence spectroscopy requires scanning the field strength F across Nw ∼ 100–300 values at fixed scaled energy ε, with each scan point requiring a full time propagation of Nsteps ∼

6 × 10 4 Trotter steps (to resolve Rydberg states near n ∼ 40). The total circuit cost is 

Ctotal ∼ Nw × Nsteps × Nrot ∼ 300 × 6 × 10 4 × 8 × 10 5 ∼ 10 13 rotations . (9.7.25) Each w-point is an independent propagation. The circuit template is invariant across the scan, only requiring a rescaling of rotation coefficients of the Stark contribution. This scaling by F (w) = w−4 at each scan point, while the Coulomb, core, and centrifugal coefficients are precomputed once and reused. For time-dependent fields (ramp functions, half-cycle terahertz pulses, or Floquet driving F (t) = F0 + F1 cos( ωt ), the same rescaling applies at each Trotter step, with no change to the gate sequence. 

Extensions Within the Class 

The register architecture generalizes without modification to several related strong-field prob-lems: 1. Diamagnetic Stark problem. [330] Adding a magnetic field B ˆz introduces a term (B2/8) r2 sin 2 θ with Legendre components ℓ = 0 and ℓ = 2. This adds two nontrivial Walsh sectors to the DVR potential UCR but requires no XOR classes and no change to the circuit template. 331 2. Multi-channel quantum defect theory. [332] For atoms with multiple ionization thresholds (e.g. alkaline earths), the ionic core states are encoded in an electronic register E with ne qubits. The core S-matrix enters as off-diagonal coupling, handled by the XOR-class protocol. The register cost grows to nr + nl + ne.3. Two-electron atoms. For helium in a strong field, the inter-electron repulsion 1 /|r1 −

r2| couples two radial and two angular registers. The channel dimension grows to d1 ×d2

and the quantum advantage factor scales as d1d2/3, placing this firmly in the classically intractable regime. A broader catalog of amenable scientific domains, with register encodings and XOR-class counts, is given in Table 9.6. 9.8 The Applicability Criterion: Identifying the Natural Domain 

This section gives a classical preprocessing test to help answer the question if REQWIEM is the appropriate tool for a given problem, a resource cost heuristic, and what method is preferred. Inputs to this test are the potential’s multilinear coefficient matrix, the potential 332 smoothness on the proposed grid, and the target precision criterion partitions the space of coupled-channel problems into classes, organized below by the partition’s specific properties.
