symmetry group is not present, as it contains only the single ν10 a mode and contributes no bilinear pairs. 468 Appendix G Phase Gradient Resource Recalculation 

The resource estimates reported by Motlagh et al. [43] for their benchmark systems are com-puted under simplifications that significantly understate the true cost of simulating vibronic dynamics with their phase gradient algorithm. Each vibrational mode is discretizeed onto 

N = 2 n = 16 grid points ( n = 4 qubits per mode) without justification from a convergence or Nyquist analysis; the concrete gate counts include only single-mode terms ( xr and x2 

> r

), omitting the bilinear cross-mode terms xrxs that their own formalism introduces; and the Trotter step cost conflates the on-diagonal and off-diagonal potential fragments, obscuring the non-commutativity of the three-way splitting. In this section, corrected resource esti-mates are derived by adopting a physically justified grid resolution of n = 10 ( N = 1024), increasing the phase gradient register to b = 45 bits, including all bilinear vibronic coupling terms, and properly accounting for the second-order Trotter structure with kinetic merging across timesteps. 

Grid resolution 

The Motlagh et al. proposal discretizes each vibrational mode onto a real-space grid of N =2n points with spacing ∆ = p2π/K , representing position eigenvalues Q|x⟩ = ∆( x−K/ 2) |x⟩

for x ∈ { 0, . . . , N − 1}. The choice n = 4 ( N = 16, ∆ ≈ 0.63) is taken without analysis. The harmonic ground state (Motlagh et al., Eq. 14) has momentum-space width σP =

p2π/K in the grid representation. For the wavefunction to be adequately sampled, the grid spacing must resolve the highest significant momentum component: ∆ ≤ π/p max . Nonadia-batic dynamics populate vibrational levels up to nmax ∼ 10–15 through linear displacement 469 and vibrational redistribution [183, 184]. With N = 16 and nmax = 10, the number of grid points per oscillator period is N/ (2 nmax + 1) ≈ 0.76. This is well below Nyquist sampling, and the discretization error is O(1). Converged MCTDH calculations require 32–64 primitive basis functions per mode for 2-state models [183] and 8–32 per mode for 24-mode models [184], depending on displace-ment and coupling strength. For the singlet fission systems of Pradhan and Zeng [196, 197], which involve larger displacements, n = 10 ( N = 1024) is adopted as a conservative choice, consistent with the first-quantization grid resolutions of Su et al. [202]. 

Phase gradient precision 

The phase gradient resource state accumulates phases with precision 2 π/ 2b. For a bilinear term γrs xrxs at n = 10, the maximum phase magnitude is θ(BVC) max = πτ K |γrs |/2. With 

N = 1024, τ ∼ 1 a.u., and |γ| ∼ 0.01–0 .05 a.u., θmax ∼ 16–80 rad. Requiring per-step phase error εphase ≲ 10 −10 (negligible over ∼ 10 4 Trotter steps at ε = 0 .01 target) gives 

b ≥ log 2

2πθ max 

εphase 



≈ 43 . (G.0.1) A higher-precision accumulator is taken with b = 45, providing precision 2 π/ 245 ≈ 1.8×10 −13 ,giving a comfortable margin for accumulation across O(M 2) terms per step. Motlagh et al. use b = 20 (precision ≈ 6 × 10 −6), which is marginally adequate at n = 4 but becomes insufficient at n = 10 where phase magnitudes grow by Nnew /K orig = 64 for quadratic terms. 

Per-term Toffoli costs 

Following the circuit structure of Motlagh et al. (their Section 3 and companion note-book [43]), each potential energy term proceeds as QROM → arithmetic → phase accu-470 mulation → uncompute. The costs of the individual primitives at n = 10, b = 45 are: 

Csq = k(k−1) 2 = 45 Toffoli (out-of-place square [202]) , (G.0.2) 

Cmult = n2 = 100 Toffoli (out-of-place multiply) , (G.0.3) 

Csemi (nb) = nbb − nb(nb−1) 2 (controlled semi-adders into b-bit register [201]) , (G.0.4) giving Csemi (10) = 405 and Csemi (20) = 710. A dirty-ancilla QROM selecting among 2 n bitstrings costs 2 n Toffoli [282]. For the di-agonal fragment ( ζ = 0), the QROM addresses all N electronic states with cost C(0) QROM =2⌈log 2 N ⌉. For off-diagonal XOR class ζ with Hamming weight h(ζ), the focusing CNOTs (Motlagh et al., Section 3.2) reduce the effective address space, giving 

C(ζ)QROM = 2 m−h(ζ), m = ⌈log 2 N ⌉. (G.0.5) This distinction provides a modest per-term reduction for off-diagonal terms. Each term type requires one forward and one inverse QROM (2 CQROM ), the appropriate arithmetic, and phase accumulation:

Cxr xs − CQ2 = 2( n2 − k(k − 1) /2) = k(k + 1) = 110 Toffoli. Representative per-term costs are shown in Table G.1.

Trotter error recalibration 

The empirical Trotter errors reported by Motlagh et al. were calibrated at N = 16 without bilinear terms. Two corrections are required. Grid-induced norm increase. The leading Trotter error for the second-order formula [127] is governed by nested commutators ∥[Ha, [Ha, H b]] ∥. The dominant contributions are the kinetic–potential commutators. In the grid representation, ∥xr∥ = ∥Pr∥ = pπK/ 2, and the nested commutator norms for each term type are:

Circuit depth and serialization 

The Motlagh et al. paper reports only Toffoli count. For this algorithm, circuit depth is essentially equal to Toffoli count because there is almost no opportunity for parallelism. Within each term, the operations QROM → arithmetic → phase accumulation → uncompute 477 form a single data-dependency chain. Across terms within a fragment, all terms share the phase gradient register |R⟩ and must be applied in sequence; the caching protocol (Motlagh et al., Section 3.1) makes this explicitly serial. Across fragments within a Trotter step, the three-way splitting G.0.9 is inherently sequential. Across Trotter steps, each step depends on the output of the previous one. For fault-tolerant execution at tcycle ∼ 1 μs per logical Toffoli [205], the wall-clock time is twall = Ctotal × tcycle . To maintain overall logical error δ = 0 .01 across depth D = Ctotal ,the per-gate logical error must satisfy pL < δ/D , requiring surface code distances d ≥

2 log 10 (D/δ ) − 1 and ∼ 2d2 physical qubits per logical qubit. The resulting fault-tolerant overhead is summarized in Table G.4. Table G.4: Fault-tolerant overhead for corrected estimates (moderate scenario, δ = 0 .01, 

tcycle = 1 μs, 10 4 shots for 1% population accuracy).                                             

> System Total Toffoli twall (1 shot) twall (10 4shots) dPhys. qubits (NO) 4-Anth 1.88 ×10 10 5.2 h 6.0 yr 25 ∼385 000 Dimer 5.21 ×10 91.4 h 1.7 yr 23 ∼347 000 Anth/C 60 (M= 11) 2.39 ×10 84.0 min 27 .6 d 21 ∼200 000 Anth/C 60 (M= 246) 4.55 ×10 11 5.3 d 144 yr 27 ∼3 760 000

Even the smallest system (Anth/C 60 , M = 11) requires ∼ 28 days at the moderate coupling scenario with 10 4 shots, or ∼ 3 days with amplitude estimation ( ∼ 100 rounds at doubled depth). The largest system ( M = 246) is categorically infeasible. These wall-time estimates exclude the use of the Motlagh et al. algorithm in the iterative materials discovery pipeline depicted in their Figures 1 and 4. 

Decomposition of the cost increase 

Table G.5 separates the correction into three multiplicative factors. The three factors compound multiplicatively. The Trotter step correction (dominated by the 8× factor from pβgrid = √64) is the largest single contributor for small systems. Figures H.1 and H.2 demonstrate convergence of the Landau–Zener bowtie model with re-spect to box size and timestep, respectively, quantified by the Wasserstein-1 distance. 483 Figure H.1: Convergence for Landau-Zener bowtie model for changing box size. Earth mover’s distance (Wasserstein-1 distance) acts as a measure for convergence, with coher-ent wavefunction propagation and spectral peak instenties requiring a sufficient timestep, register size, and box size. 484 Figure H.2: Convergence for Landau-Zener bowtie model for changing time step. 485 Appendix I Suboptimal Scaling for Pairwise Coupling I.1 Basic Nonadiabatic Coupling 

This appendix reviews the nonadiabatic coupling scheme of Ollitrault et al. [44], showing that it achieves suboptimal scaling of O(M 2 log M ) Toffoli gates for M electronic channels. 

I.1.1 Coupling Primitives for M = 2 The two-channel case ( m = 1) has the channel register as a single qubit |s⟩. These primitives form the building blocks for the general construction and are originally due to Ollitrault et al. [44]. 

Linear Vibronic Coupling (LVC) 

A linear coupling applies 

Rx

␀f (x) |s⟩ = Rx(a x + b) |s⟩ , (I.1.1) for a given channel state |s⟩, where the position variable x is encoded in the binary-weighted values of the n grid qubits. The result is n singly controlled Rx gates (with binary weights 20a, 21a, . . . , 2n−1a) plus one unconditional rotation by b, as shown in Figure I.1. For d vibrational modes, LVC requires dn singly controlled Rx gates and 2 dn CNOTs (from their standard decomposition), with no multi-qubit gates beyond two-body. 

Quadratic Vibronic Coupling (QVC) 

A quadratic coupling applies 

Rx

␀f (x) |s⟩ = Rx(a x 2 + b x + c) |s⟩ . I.1.2 Information-Theoretic Lower Bound 

Off-diagonal coupling is constrained to be real and symmetric: 

V (ij ) = c(ij )␀|i⟩⟨ j| + |j⟩⟨ i|, c(ij ) ∈ R, (I.1.4) where c(ij ) depends on the nuclear coordinates (via LVC, QVC, or BVC terms). For each nuclear-coordinate site (i.e., each term in the binary expansion of the coupling function), the coupling matrix is an M × M real symmetric matrix with vanishing diagonal, having 

␀M

> 2

 = M (M − 1) /2 independent parameters. 488 Each generator Gij = |i⟩⟨ j| + |j⟩⟨ i| is a real symmetric matrix generating the Lie algebra 

so (M ), with dim ␀so (M ) = M (M − 1) 2 . (I.1.5) Any scheme that independently addresses all M (M − 1) /2 couplings must therefore use at least M (M − 1) /2 = O(M 2) independent rotation parameters. O(M 2) is taken as the target complexity for an optimal scheme. 

I.1.3 Pair-Isolation Approach 

The natural extension of the Ollitrault et al. [44] scheme to M > 2 channels couples each pair ( i, j ) independently via multi-controlled rotations (Equation S7 of Ollitrault et al. [44]). 

Construction 

For a given pair ( i, j ) with i̸ = j, the unitary that couples only channels |i⟩ and |j⟩ with angle θ is 

Uij (θ) = 1 + (cos θ 

> 2

− 1) ␀|i⟩ ⟨ i| + |j⟩ ⟨ j| − i sin θ

> 2

␀|i⟩⟨ j| + |j⟩⟨ i|. (I.1.6) On the m-qubit channel register, this can be implemented by the following subroutine: 1. Compute ζ = i ⊕ j (classically). 2. Select a target qubit t such that ζt = 1. 3. For each qubit k̸ = t: if bit k of i is 0, apply Xk (to condition on |0⟩). 4. Apply Cm−1Rx(θij ) with all m − 1 non-target qubits as controls and qubit t as target. 5. Reverse the conditioning X gates from step 3. The Cm−1Rx gate is the bottleneck: it requires computing a truth value (AND of m − 1control qubits), implemented as a linear chain of Toffoli gates. 489 Toffoli Cost 

Using one ancilla qubit, a Cm−1Rx gate decomposes as: 

• m − 2 Toffoli gates to compute the ( m − 1)-bit AND onto an ancilla, 

• one singly controlled CR x gate (Toffoli-free), 

• m − 2 Toffoli gates to uncompute the ancilla, for a total of 2( m − 2) Toffoli gates per pair. Summing over all M (M − 1) /2 pairs: 

Tpair = M (M − 1) 2 · 2( m − 2) = ( m − 2) M (M − 1) = O(M 2 log M ). (I.1.7) This exceeds the optimal O(M 2) target by a factor of Θ(log M ). An example of one such pairwise coupling circuit for M = 4 is shown in Figure I.4. 

|s0⟩ H • • H

|s1⟩ H Rz (f ) HFigure I.4: Quantum circuit to couple the |00 ⟩ and |11 ⟩ electronic channels via exp

, from the supplemental material of Ollitrault et al. [44]. For m = 2 this requires no Toffoli gates, but the extension to m ≥ 3 does. 

I.1.4 XOR Class Decomposition 

A natural optimization groups pairs that share the same XOR pattern, allowing simultaneous coupling via a single generator. Independently, Motlagh et al. [43] employ an equivalent XOR fragmentation of the potential Hamiltonian, loading state-dependent coefficients via quantum read-only memory (QROM). 

Definitions 

For two channel-register basis states |i⟩ and |j⟩, define the XOR pattern ζ = i ⊕ j ∈ { 0, 1}m \{0m} and the Hamming weight h = wt( ζ). The 2 m − 1 nonzero patterns partition all M (M −

1) /2 pairs into disjoint XOR classes Cζ .490 Class size. For any nonzero ζ, the class Cζ contains exactly 

|C ζ | = 2 m−1, (I.1.8) pairs, independent of h. To see this, note that for each i ∈ { 0, . . . , 2m − 1} there is a unique partner j = i ⊕ ζ, and the ordering constraint i < j selects exactly half of the 2 m values of i.Summing over all nonzero ζ:

X 

> ζ̸=0

|C ζ | = (2 m − 1) · 2m−1 = M (M − 1) 2 , (I.1.9) confirming the partition is complete. Active and spectator qubits. Within class Cζ (weight h), the h qubit positions where ζ has a 1 are active: these qubits flip between i and j. The remaining m−h positions are spectator: they have the same value in i and j. Two pairs in the same class are distinguished by: 

• the m − h spectator-qubit values (2 m−h configurations), 

• the h − 1 active-qubit values of the lower-indexed state on the non-target active qubits (2 h−1 configurations, after fixing the ordering i < j on the most significant active bit). The product is 2 m−h · 2h−1 = 2 m−1, consistent with Eq. I.1.8. 

Generator Structure 

The generator XS(ζ) = Q 

> k∈S(ζ)

Xk, where S(ζ) is the set of active qubit positions, simul-taneously couples all 2 m−1 pairs in Cζ with the same rotation angle. To assign independent coupling strengths to each pair, one must further condition on the distinguishing bits. As an illustrative example, applying Rx(θ) to the lowest channel qubit |s0⟩ in a 3-qubit register ( m = 3) simultaneously couples four pairs: 

|000 ⟩ ↔ | 001 ⟩ , |010 ⟩ ↔ | 011 ⟩ , |100 ⟩ ↔ | 101 ⟩ , |110 ⟩ ↔ | 111 ⟩ . (I.1.10) 491 This class has h = 1, so all distinguishing bits are spectator bits ( m − h = 2 spectators), and all four pairs can be independently addressed by conditioning on |s1s2⟩. However, for the class ζ = 011 ( h = 2), the four pairs are: 

|000 ⟩ ↔ | 011 ⟩ , |001 ⟩ ↔ | 010 ⟩ , |100 ⟩ ↔ | 111 ⟩ , |101 ⟩ ↔ | 110 ⟩ . (I.1.11) Here there is only m−h = 1 spectator qubit, giving 2 1 = 2 spectator configurations, while the class contains 2 m−1 = 4 pairs. The remaining factor of 2 h−1 = 2 comes from the non-target active qubit, which the spectator-only scheme cannot distinguish. 

I.1.5 Spectator-Qubit Multiplexing 

The multiplexed approach attempts to achieve independent control within each XOR class by conditioning on spectator qubits only. 

Construction 

For a class Cζ with weight h, select a target active qubit t and apply: 1. Clifford diagonalization: CNOT from h − 1 non-target active qubits to t, followed by a Hadamard on t. This conjugates XS(ζ) into Zt, reducing the multi-qubit coupling to a Z-rotation on qubit t.2. Spectator-controlled rotations: Apply 2 m−h rotations Rzt (˜ ca), indexed by a ∈{0, 1}m−h, where rotation a is controlled on spectator qubits at positions where a

has a 1. 3. Uncompute: Reverse the Clifford diagonalization. The 2 m−h transformed coefficients {˜ca} are obtained from physical couplings {cb}b∈{ 0,1}m−h

by M¨ obius inversion on the Boolean lattice: ˜ca = X 

> b:a≤b

(−1) |b|−| a| cb, (I.1.12) 492 where a ≤ b denotes componentwise bit comparison and | · | is the Hamming weight. This ensures that the total rotation angle accumulated by spectator configuration b is exactly cb,by the inclusion-exclusion principle: 

θtot (b) = X 

> a:a≤b

˜ca = X

> a≤b

X

> k≤a

(−1) |a|−| k| ck = cb, (I.1.13) where the last equality follows because the inner sums cancel for all k̸ = b. The inversion I.1.12 is computed classically in O(2 m−h) operations. 

Degrees of Freedom 

The multiplexed scheme assigns 2 m−h independent rotation angles per class (one per specta-tor configuration), but each class contains 2 m−1 pairs (Eq. I.1.8). Since the Clifford diagonal-ization step treats all 2 h−1 active-qubit configurations identically, pairs that share the same spectator values but differ in their non-target active-qubit values receive the same coupling strength. 

Hamming weight h Degrees of freedom Pairs per class Fraction addressed 

1 2m−1 2m−1 12 2m−2 2m−1 1/23 2m−3 2m−1 1/4

h 2m−h 2m−1 21−h

For classes with h = 1 (all m such classes), the scheme is fully expressive: every pair is independently addressable. For h ≥ 2, each spectator-controlled rotation conflates 2 h−1

pairs, so only a 2 1−h fraction of the couplings can be set independently. The total number of independent rotation parameters across all classes is

M > 2. 

I.1.6 Restoring Full Expressivity via Truth-Value Computation 

To independently address all 2 m−1 pairs within each XOR class, the rotation within each class must be conditioned not only on the m − h spectator qubits but also on the h − 1non-target active qubits. This requires m − 1 total control qubits per rotation, restoring the truth-value computation overhead. 

Construction 

Within class Cζ (weight h), after Clifford diagonalization, each of the 2 m−1 pairs is uniquely identified by an ( m − 1)-bit string: the m − h spectator values concatenated with the h − 1non-target active-qubit values. To apply an independent rotation to pair b ∈ { 0, 1}m−1, one must compute the truth value “all m − 1 control qubits match pattern b,” which requires: 494 • conditioning X gates on the control qubits where b has a 0 (to convert the pattern to all-ones), 

• computing the ( m − 1)-way AND via a Toffoli chain: m − 2 Toffoli gates to compute, 

m − 2 to uncompute, totaling 2( m − 2) Toffoli gates per pair, 

• one CR z gate (Toffoli-free), 

• reversing the conditioning X gates. 

Toffoli Count 

The total Toffoli cost over all classes is: 

Tfull = (2 m − 1) 

| {z }

> classes

× 2m−1

| {z } 

> pairs/class

× 2( m − 2) 

| {z }

> Toffoli/pair

= ( m − 2) M (M − 1) = O(M 2 log M ), (I.1.16) identical to the pair-isolation cost I.1.7. The XOR class grouping and Clifford diagonalization reduce the Clifford overhead (fewer single-qubit gates and CNOTs) but do not reduce the Toffoli count, because the number of control qubits per rotation remains m − 1. 

Why XOR Grouping Alone Cannot Eliminate the Overhead 

The Θ(log M ) Toffoli overhead is intrinsic to truth-value-based pair isolation: identifying a specific ( m − 1)-bit pattern among m − 1 control qubits requires computing an AND, which costs Θ( m) Toffoli gates regardless of how the pairs are organized into classes. The spectator-only scheme (Section I.1.5) avoids Toffoli gates for high-weight classes precisely by surrendering degrees of freedom: it conditions on only m − h < m − 1 qubits, so each rotation addresses 2 h−1 pairs simultaneously rather than one. The savings in Toffoli gates come at the cost of an exponentially growing expressivity deficit. The decision to partially diagonalize with XOR fragmentation follows with a potentially optimal gate scaling, given in Motlagh et al. [43], used in the main text. 495 Appendix J T-Gate Reduction Pipeline for REQWIEM 

This appendix provides a self-contained treatment of the T -gate optimization pipeline applicable to the REQWIEM circuit architecture. Each synthesis and optimization stage is defined, with a composite reduction factor calculated, including estimates for the fraction of rotations eligible for Hamming weight phasing in representative molecular systems. Throughout, the empirical linear fits to the numerical benchmark data reported in [355] are used rather than the asymptotic theoretical bounds, more accurately capturing the per-rotation T -count at the finite precisions relevant to molecular simulation. 

J.0.1 Stage 1: Single-Gate Synthesis Ross–Selinger Synthesis (Baseline) 

The Ross–Selinger algorithm [204] provides an asymptotically optimal decomposition of an arbitrary rotation Rz (θ) into the Clifford+ T gate set. For any θ ∈ R and synthesis precision ε > 0, there exists a Clifford+ T circuit U with 

∥U − Rz (θ) ∥ ≤ ε and T -count( U ) = 3 log 2

1

ε



+ O



log log 1

ε



. (J.0.1) This result is gate-optimal in the sense that no single-gate synthesis protocol over Clifford+ T

can achieve a smaller leading coefficient than 3. The numerical benchmarks of [355] confirm the theoretical scaling and yield the empirical linear fit 

TRS (ε) = 3 .02 log 2(1 /ε ) + 1 .77 , (J.0.2) for random rotation angles drawn uniformly from [0 , 2π], where the additive constant absorbs the lower-order correction from J.0.1. 496 Mixed-Fallback Synthesis over Clifford+ √T

Kliuchnikov et al. [355] introduced a refined protocol that operates over the extended gate set Clifford+ √T , where 

√T =

1 00 eiπ/ 8

 . (J.0.3) A mixed-fallback strategy probabilistically selects among multiple candidate unitaries using ancilla measurements, achieving shorter expected gate sequences. For random rotation angles Rz (θ) and precision ε > 0, mixed-fallback synthesis over Clifford+ √T produces a circuit with expected T -count fitting the empirical linear relation 

TMF (ε) = 0 .53 log 2

1

ε



+ 4 .86 . (J.0.4) Every rotation in the REQWIEM circuit is eligible for mixed-fallback synthesis: the UCR decomposition produces Rz (θ) gates on diagonal blocks and Rx(θ) = H R z (θ) H gates on off-diagonal blocks, both of which are Clifford conjugates of Rz rotations. The mixed-fallback reduction factor at precision ε is 

RMF (ε) = TRS (ε)

TMF (ε) = 3.02 log 2(1 /ε ) + 1 .77 0.53 log 2(1 /ε ) + 4 .86 . (J.0.5) The asymptotic ratio of leading coefficients is 3 .02 /0.53 ≈ 5.70, which is approached in the limit ε → 0. At finite precisions relevant to molecular simulation, the additive constants reduce the realized improvement. Evaluating Eq. J.0.5 at representative precisions (J.1) yields RMF ≈ 4.5× at ε = 10 −10 , increasing slowly toward the asymptotic limit at higher precisions. 

J.0.2 Stage 2: Global Phase Cancellation via ZX-Calculus (PyZX) 

After single-gate synthesis (either Ross–Selinger or mixed-fallback), the REQWIEM circuit becomes a Clifford+ T circuit. The ZX-calculus [356, 357] provides a graphical framework for optimizing such circuits beyond what is achievable by local gate-level transformations. 497 Table J.1: Per-rotation T -counts (empirical fits) and mixed-fallback reduction factor RMF at representative synthesis precisions. Precision ε log 2(1 /ε ) TRS TMF RMF 

10 −6 19.93 62.0 15.4 4.02 ×

10 −8 26.58 82.0 19.0 4.32 ×

10 −10 33.22 102.1 22.5 4.54 ×

10 −12 39.86 122.2 26.0 4.70 ×

10 −14 46.51 142.2 29.5 4.82 ×

10 −16 53.15 162.3 33.0 4.92 ×

ZX-Calculus Foundations 

A ZX-diagram is an open graph whose vertices (“spiders”) are labeled by phases and colored either green ( Z-type) or red ( X-type). The two fundamental generators are: A Z-spider with phase α and m inputs, n outputs represents the linear map  

> α

: |0⟩⊗n ⟨0|⊗m + eiα |1⟩⊗n ⟨1|⊗m , (J.0.6) and an X-spider is defined analogously in the Hadamard-conjugate basis. ZX-calculus permits rewrites, preserving linear mapping while potentially reducing the num-ber of non-Clifford ( π/ 4-phase) spiders: (i) Spider fusion. Two adjacent spiders of the same color merge into a single spider whose phase is the sum of the individual phases: 

α −− β −→ (α + β). (J.0.7) When α + β ≡ 0 (mod 2 π), the merged spider is Clifford; when α + β ≡ 0 (mod π/ 2), it is also Clifford. In both cases, two non-Clifford spiders are eliminated. (ii) Phase commutation through CNOT. In the ZX-calculus, a CNOT is represented as a Z-spider (on the control wire) connected to an X-spider (on the target wire) by a Hadamard edge. This structure enables two commutation identities: 498 • A Z-phase on the target wire can be pushed through the CNOT to become an 

X-phase on the control wire (color change): CNOT ( I ⊗ Rz (α)) = ( Rx(α) ⊗ I) CNOT . (J.0.8) 

• An X-phase on the control wire can be pushed through the CNOT to become a 

Z-phase on the target wire (color change in the reverse direction): (Rx(α) ⊗ I) CNOT = CNOT ( I ⊗ Rz (α)) . (J.0.9) These identities enable non-local cancellations by transporting T -phases across CNOT barriers, exposing fusion opportunities that are invisible to circuit-level optimization. (iii) Pivoting and local complementation. Graph-theoretic operations on the ZX-diagram’s adjacency structure that rearrange spider connectivity, exposing new fusion opportunities invisible to circuit-level optimization [358]. 

The PyZX Optimization Framework 

The PyZX library [359] implements: 1. Circuit → ZX-diagram. The input Clifford+ T circuit is translated to a ZX-diagram. 2. Full reduction. The diagram undergoes iterative application of spider fusion, local complementation, and pivoting rules until a fixpoint is reached. 3. Circuit extraction. The reduced diagram is converted back to a Clifford+ T circuit via Gaussian elimination over F2 [359]. Kissinger and van de Griend [359] report T -count reductions of 40–60% on benchmark circuits with alternating CNOT-Rz structures. The T -gate patterns produced by mixed-fallback differ structurally from those of Ross– Selinger due to the probabilistic fallback structure and √T decomposition. 499 The following distinction is made. Let f (RS) ZX denote the fraction of T -gates removed by PyZX when applied to a Ross–Selinger-synthesized circuit, and f (MF) ZX the corresponding fraction for a mixed-fallback-synthesized circuit. Based on the reported range and the reduced redundancy in mixed-fallback output, 

f (RS) ZX ∈ [0 .40 , 0.60] , f (MF) ZX ∈ [0 .25 , 0.45] . (J.0.10) The lower range for f (MF) ZX reflects the fact that mixed-fallback synthesis already performs internal phase optimization that partially overlaps with the cancellations PyZX discovers. 

Structural favorability for REQWIEM. UCR produces circuits with a highly regular CNOT-Rz -CNOT-Rz -· · · alternation pattern within each Gray code sequence. In the ZX representation, this yields long chains of phase spiders separated by single Hadamard edges, precisely the topology for most effective spider fusion. Repetition of identical UCR structures across Trotter steps creates inter-step cancellation opportunities: the final CNOT of one UCR sequence and the initial CNOT of the next target the same qubit pair, enabling spider fusion across step boundaries. These structural features place REQWIEM circuits at the favorable end of the ranges in Eq. J.0.10. 

J.0.3 Stage 3: Symmetric Tensor Optimization (TODD) 

The TODD (T-Optimization by Diagonal Decomposition) algorithm [360] targets residual 

T -gate redundancies that survive ZX-calculus optimization by recasting the problem in terms of symmetric tensor decomposition. 

Algebraic Framework 

After Clifford+ T synthesis and ZX reduction, the remaining non-Clifford content of a circuit can be encoded as a phase polynomial: 500 Definition J.0.1 (Phase polynomial) . A phase polynomial over n qubits is a diagonal unitary of the form 

Uphase = X

> x∈{ 0,1}n

ei p (x) |x⟩⟨ x| , (J.0.11) where p(x) = P 

> S⊆[n]

αS

Q 

> j∈S

xj is a multilinear polynomial over F2 with coefficients αS ∈ R.Each term αS with αS /∈ { 0, π/ 2, π, 3π/ 2} contributes to the T -count. TODD represents the non-Clifford phase terms via their parity matrices. Let the circuit contain m T -gates, each acting on a computational basis parity vk ∈ Fn 

> 2

(determined by the CNOT structure preceding the T -gate). The n × m matrix P = [ v1 | · · · | vm] encodes the “Pauli frame” of each T -gate. 

Definition J.0.2 (TODD reduction) . The TODD algorithm finds a matrix P′ ∈ Fn×m′ 

> 2

with 

m′ ≤ m and an associated set of phases {α′

> k

} such that 

> m

X

> k=1

π

4 vk ≡

> m′

X

> k=1

α′ 

> k

v′ 

> k

(mod 2 π), (J.0.12) where each α′ 

> k

∈ { π/ 4, −π/ 4}, thereby reducing T -count from m to m′. The optimization proceeds by identifying columns of P that are identical (direct cancellation) or related by 

F2-linear combinations (indirect cancellation via re-synthesis). Heyfron and Campbell [360] report 20–36% T -count reductions beyond PyZX on structured circuits. As with PyZX, adjusted fractions for post-mixed-fallback circuits are given as: 

f (MF) TODD ∈ [0 .15 , 0.30] , (J.0.13) reflecting partial overlap with prior optimization stages. Binary-weighted rotation angles θℓ = 2 ℓθ0 within each UCR produce T -gate decomposi-tions whose phases differ by exact powers of two. In the parity matrix P, this manifests as columns related by F2-shifts, mapping to low-rank tensors in the TODD formalism. The Walsh–Hadamard relationship further imposes F2-linear relations among synthesized 

T -sequences across the spectator-qubit index, creating inter-gate correlations amenable to TODD optimization. 501 J.0.4 Stage 4: Hamming Weight Phasing Protocol Definition 

Hamming weight phasing [201] addresses the scenario where g rotations share a common angle θ (up to synthesis precision). Given g rotations Rz (θ) on distinct qubits, each requiring m T -gates when synthesized independently, Hamming weight phasing implements all g rotations jointly using 

THWP (g, m ) = 4( g − ⌈ log 2 g⌉) + m ⌈log 2 g⌉ (J.0.14) 

T -gates, yielding a per-rotation cost of 

THWP 

g ≈ 4 + m log 2 gg , (J.0.15) for large g. The protocol computes the Hamming weight of the g-qubit register into ⌈log 2 g⌉

ancilla qubits via a sorting network (costing 4( g − ⌈ log 2 g⌉) T -gates), applies ⌈log 2 g⌉ individ-ually synthesized rotations Rz

␀2kθ on the ancillas, and uncomputes the Hamming weight. As an illustrative worked example at ε = 10 −14 (m ≈ 30 via mixed-fallback from Table J.1), a degenerate group of g = 8 yields THWP = 4(8 − 3) + 30 × 3 = 20 + 90 = 110 total, or 

≈ 13 .8 per rotation, being a 2 .2× reduction relative to independent synthesis. At g = 16: 

THWP = 4(16 − 4) + 30 × 4 = 48 + 120 = 168, or 10 .5 per rotation (2 .9× reduction). 

Degeneracy Estimation for REQWIEM on Vibronic Hamiltonians 

Considering pyrazine, assessment for Hamming weight phasing viability is presented. Within each symmetry-allowed coupling block, modes related by approximate internal symmetries of the pyrazine ring (e.g., C–H stretching modes of similar frequency) produce Duschinsky matrix elements Jrs and displacement components dr that are numerically close. This induces near-degeneracies in the circuit rotation angles θℓ,r,s = 2 ℓ+p ∆r∆s ϕ(rs ) 

> g

.Two rotation angles θi and θj belong to the same εsynth -degeneracy group if |θi − θj | <εsynth , where εsynth is the synthesis precision. All rotations in a degeneracy group can be replaced by a single angle (e.g., their mean) with error bounded by εsynth .502 To estimate the fraction of eligible rotations, the total number of rotation angles and the symmetry-induced structure is given: (a) Total rotations per Trotter step. The REQWIEM Hamiltonian for M modes con-tains M diagonal (frequency) terms, ␀M

> 2

 quadratic coupling terms, and M linear dis-placement terms, giving Nrot = M + ␀M

> 2

 + M = M 2/2 + 3 M/ 2 distinct coupling param-eters. For M = 24, this yields Nrot = 324 base coupling parameters, each generating a UCR with multiple individual rotations. (b) Symmetry-equivalent pairs. Under D2h, coupling parameters related by the symme-try operations of the point group are exactly degenerate. The eight irreducible repre-sentations of D2h partition the modes more finely than lower-symmetry groups, concen-trating the off-diagonal couplings into well-separated blocks. Within each block, approx-imate degeneracies among C–H and C–N stretches of similar frequency typically group couplings into clusters of 2–4. Across all symmetry blocks, an estimated ∼ 60–90 of the 324 base parameters (18–28%) fall into degeneracy groups of size g ≥ 2 at εsynth = 10 −10 .(c) Binary weighting amplification. Within each UCR, the binary weighting θℓ = 2 ℓ θ0

preserves degeneracy: if |θ0,i − θ0,j | < ε synth /2L for the highest level L, then |θℓ,i − θℓ,j | <εsynth for all levels ℓ ≤ L. This propagates base-parameter degeneracies to all L + 1 rotation levels within the UCR. Combining these estimates, approximately 18–28% of the total rotation count is eligible for Hamming weight phasing with typical group sizes g ∈ [2 , 8]. The resulting T -count saving on the eligible fraction is summarized in Eq. J.2. The explicit computations for each row are: g = 2: THWP = 4(2 − 1) + 23 × 1 = 4 + 23 = 27; 

g = 4: THWP = 4(4 −2)+23 ×2 = 8+46 = 54; g = 6: THWP = 4(6 −3)+23 ×3 = 12+69 = 81; 

g = 8: THWP = 4(8 − 3) + 23 × 3 = 20 + 69 = 89; g = 16: THWP = 4(16 − 4) + 23 × 4 = 48 + 92 = 140. Note that g = 6 and g = 8 share the same ⌈log 2 g⌉ = 3 but differ in the sorting-network overhead (4( g − ⌈ log 2 g⌉)), which dominates for large g.503 Table J.2: Hamming weight phasing savings for representative degeneracy group sizes at 

ε = 10 −10 (TMF ≈ 23, rounding to the nearest integer for T -gate counts). Group size g ⌈log 2 g⌉ Tindep = g · m THWP Per-rotation Saving 2 1 46 27 13.5 1.70 ×

The conservative estimate of 7 .7× uses the low end of all reported reduction ranges, ignores inter-Trotter-step cancellation opportunities specific to REQWIEM, and applies the lowest pyrazine degeneracy fraction. The moderate scenario ( ∼ 10 ×) is more representative expected performance from numerical pipeline benchmarks on circuits exhibiting the regular UCR topology of REQWIEM. Numerical validation on full REQWIEM circuits for specific molecular systems remains an important direction for future work. J.0.6 Discussion: Composability and Overlap 

Mixed-fallback vs. PyZX overlap. Mixed-fallback synthesis produces shorter T -gate se-quences per rotation, which means fewer total spiders in the ZX-diagram and correspondingly fewer opportunities for inter-gate cancellations. However, the type of redundancy removed differs: mixed-fallback optimizes within each single-gate synthesis (exploiting the algebraic structure of the Clifford+ √T ring), whereas PyZX optimizes across gates (exploiting the circuit topology). The overlap is therefore partial, justifying the reduced but nonzero f (MF) ZX 

range in Eq. J.0.10. 

PyZX vs. TODD overlap. PyZX and TODD both target non-Clifford phase cancella-tions, but through different mathematical lenses: PyZX operates on graph topology (spider fusion, local complementation), while TODD operates on the F2-linear algebra of parity matrices. Cancellations found by PyZX’s graph rewriting may correspond to column elim-inations in TODD’s parity matrix, but TODD’s global optimization over the full parity matrix can find reductions that PyZX’s local rewrite rules miss. The TODD reduction frac-tions in Eq. J.0.13 are residual (i.e., applied to the post-PyZX circuit), consistent with the reported benchmarks in [360]. 

Hamming weight phasing independence. Hamming weight phasing exploits angle degeneracy rather than phase cancellation. It is therefore largely independent of Stages 2–3, 507 with the caveat that the per-rotation cost m entering J.0.14 should be evaluated after the Stage 1–3 pipeline has established the per-rotation T -count. In the formulation (J.0.22), Hamming weight phasing applies as a multiplicative correction to the Stage-3 output, cor-rectly accounting for this sequential dependence. 508 Appendix K Trotter Error Calculation 

In this appendix, error bounds are calculated for the second-order Trotter decomposition of the expanded vibronic Hamiltonian and provide system-specific Trotter step estimates for the molecular benchmarks considered in this work. Throughout, M denotes the number of diabatic electronic states, d the number of vi-brational modes, n the number of grid qubits per mode, and N = 2 n the number of grid points per mode. Mode coordinates are xr with conjugate momenta pr and reduced masses 

μr (r = 1 , . . . , d ). Correspondence with the Raab et al. parameters used in the pyrazine benchmark is given explicitly in Section K.0.8. For ease of calculation ¯ μ is used, being the same mass for all modes.

Choice of product formula 

Symmetric (second-order) Trotter decomposition is employed, as ˜U (τ ) = e−iT τ / 2 e−iV τ e−iT τ / 2, (K.0.7) rather than a higher-order product formula. Higher-order decompositions (e.g. fourth-order Suzuki formulas) reduce the required number of Trotter steps k at the cost of increasing the gate count per step by a constant factor (typically 5 p/ 2−1 for order p). For the vibronic Hamiltonians considered here, the per-step circuit is dominated by phase-gradient rotations whose count scales linearly in d and n; the modest reduction in k from a fourth-order for-mula does not offset the ∼ 5× increase in per-step gates, so the second-order decomposition provides the lowest total gate count at the grid sizes of interest ( n = 8–12). The alternative V T V ordering e−iV τ / 2 e−iT τ e−iV τ / 2 yields an identical error bound at second order [127], differing only at fourth order. The circuit implementations in the main text use T V T ordering, so the bounds derived here apply directly. The error bound of Childs et al. [127] gives 

εTrotter ≤ t3

24 k2



[V, [V, T ]] + 2 [T, [T, V ]] 



, (K.0.8) 510 where t is the total evolution time and k the number of Trotter steps. Since T acts only on nuclear coordinates and is proportional to 1el , the electronic opera-tors factor cleanly out of all commutators: [T, V ] = X

> i

[T, V (ii )] ⊗ ˆpi + X

[T, V (ij )] ⊗ ˆσij . (K.0.9) [T, [T, V (ij )]] has the same nuclear-operator structure regardless of whether the potential term is diagonal (ˆ pi) or off-diagonal (ˆ σij ); only the electronic prefactor and coupling constants differ. The bound in Eq. K.0.8 controls the operator norm ∥U (τ ) − ˜U (τ )∥, which in turn bounds the error in any observable: |⟨ ψ| ˆO|ψ⟩ − ⟨ ˜ψ| ˆO| ˜ψ⟩| ≤ 2∥ ˆO∥ εTrotter . For the autocorrelation function ( ˆO = |ψ0⟩⟨ ψ0|, ∥ ˆO∥ = 1) and electronic populations ( ˆO = ˆ pi, ∥ ˆO∥ = 1), operator-norm bounds apply directly with no additional prefactor.

K.0.4 Cross-Terms Between Potential Components 

The full [ V, [V, T ]] commutator expands as [V, [V, T ]] = [ Vdiag , [Vdiag , T ]] + [ Voff , [Voff , T ]] + [ Vdiag , [Voff , T ]] + [ Voff , [Vdiag , T ]] . (K.0.36) Cross-terms between different potential components could in principle produce additional 

O(N 2) contributions if both parents are individually O(N 2) in [ T, [T, V ]]. All cross-terms scale as O(1) in N .515 Linear × linear (different blocks).

K.0.8 Worked Example: Pyrazine 

The error bound calculation for the standard two-mode, two-state vibronic coupling model of pyrazine [183] is given as a validation benchmark. 520 System Parameters and Notation Mapping 

The model comprises two diabatic electronic states S1 (1B3u) and S2 (1B2u), coupled through two vibrational normal modes: 

• Mode ν6a (ag symmetry, “tuning”): ω6a = 0 .0739 eV = 2 .716 × 10 −3 a.u. 

• Mode ν10 a (b1g symmetry, “coupling”): ω10 a = 0 .1139 eV = 4 .186 × 10 −3 a.u. The reduced masses are taken as μ ≈ 6 amu = 10 ,937 a.u. for both modes (an upper-bound approximation; the actual normal-mode reduced masses differ slightly). The vibronic coupling parameters from Raab et al. [183] map onto the present notation as follows:

λ10 a (interstate linear coupling) c(12) 10 a 7.394 × 10 −3

The simulation in the main text additionally includes an interstate bilinear coupling 

c(12) 6a, 10 a x6a x10 a; this parameter is small (Appendix H) and its contribution to C2 is eval-uated below. Grid parameters: N = 1024 ( n = 10), L = 10 a.u., t = 100 fs = 4134 a.u., and ε = 1 .6 mE h

(chemical accuracy). 

Symmetry-Allowed Terms 

In D2h symmetry, the product Γ( S1) ⊗ Γ( S2) = B3u ⊗ B2u = B1g. Thus: 

• Diagonal quadratic (x2 

> r

∼ Ag): nonzero for both modes on both states. This is the dominant O(N 2) contribution. 

• Diagonal bilinear (x6a x10 a ∼ Ag ⊗ B1g = B1g̸ = Ag): vanishes by symmetry. 521 • Off-diagonal quadratic (x2 

> r

⊗ ˆσ12 ): requires Ag = B1g, which fails. Both modes forbidden. 

• Off-diagonal bilinear (x6a x10 a ⊗ ˆσ12 ): requires B1g = B1g. Symmetry-allowed. The coupling c(12) 6a, 10 a is included in the main-text simulation; its value is small relative to the linear couplings.

Comparison with Empirical Trotter Error 

The bound of k ≈ 45 ,800 is a significant overestimate. Childs et al. [127] demonstrated that commutator-based Trotter bounds typically overestimate the actual error by one to three orders of magnitude due to triangle inequality losses, partial cancellations between the two nested commutators, and the replacement of spectral information by operator norms. For pyrazine, the main text uses k = 2,500 Trotter steps, validated numerically by comparing the Trotterized propagator against the exact time evolution. The ratio 

kanalytic /k empirical ≈ 18 is consistent with the typical tightening observed for molecular Hamil-tonians. The sources of this overestimation are analysed systematically in Section K.0.9. 523 K.0.9 System-Specific Trotter Step Requirements 

Trotter step estimates for the singlet fission and charge transfer systems considered in the resource analysis, using the commutator bounds derived above. The molecular parameters are collected in Table K.1. Table K.1: Molecular system parameters for Trotter step estimation. M denotes the number of electronic states and d the number of vibrational modes. 

System M d ωmax (cm −1) ωmax (a.u.) Description

Computing C2 exactly via Eq. K.0.42 requires the full set of vibronic coupling parame-ters {a(i)

With a characteristic coupling |¯a| ≈ f · ¯μ ω 2max where f denotes the ratio of a typical bilinear coupling 524 to the harmonic force constant (typically f ∼ 0.01–0 .1 for Duschinsky-active systems; cf. C(bilin) 2 ∼ 2π2f · M · d(d − 1) · ω2max

f (d − 1) ≈ 2.5.

Sources of overestimation and the empirical tightening factor 

Analytic commutator bounds are known to overestimate the actual Trotter error substan-tially [127]. For pyrazine, the ratio kanalytic /k empirical ≈ 18 (Section K.0.8) is typical of molecu-lar Hamiltonians. To apply these bounds to larger systems where exact numerical validation 525 is infeasible, it is useful to decompose the overestimation into its constituent sources so that the transferability of the tightening factor can be assessed. 

(i) Triangle-inequality looseness. 

The bound ∥[A + B, [A + B, C ]] ∥ ≤ ∥ [A, [A, C ]] ∥ + 2 ∥[A, [B, C ]] ∥ + ∥[B, [B, C ]] ∥ is saturated only when all commutator contributions are co-aligned in operator space. In practice, can-cellation between terms of opposite sign reduces the true commutator norm below the sum of individual norms. For molecular Hamiltonians with mixed-sign coupling constants, this cancellation is typically a factor of 2–5. 

(ii) Momentum bound versus wavepacket support. 

The substitution ∥pr∥ → pmax = πℏ/∆x uses the maximum eigenvalue of the discrete momentum operator. A physical wavepacket occupies a fraction of the Brillouin zone, so 

⟨p2⟩/p 2max ≪ 1. For a Gaussian wavepacket centred at equilibrium on an N -point grid, the effective momentum fraction scales as ⟨p2⟩/p 2max ∼ (∆ x/σ )2 ≪ 1 for well-resolved grids (σ ≫ ∆x). This is the dominant source of overestimation in C2.

(iii) Symmetry overcounting. 

The upper bound on C2 (Eq. K.0.60) sums over all mode pairs ( r, s ) and surface pairs ( i, j )regardless of molecular symmetry. Symmetry selection rules (Appendix F) set many coupling constants to zero: 

a(i) 

> rs

= 0 unless Γr ⊗ Γs ⊃ Γtot. sym. , (diagonal blocks) (K.0.62) and similarly for off-diagonal blocks where the product representation must contain the representation of the relevant electronic coupling operator ˆVij . For pyrazine ( C2v), symmetry eliminates all diagonal bilinear terms and all off-diagonal quadratic terms, reducing N (tot) BVC by roughly a factor of 2 relative to the unsymmetrised count. For higher-symmetry molecules such as anthracene ( D2h, 8 irreps), the reduction factor can reach 4–8. 526 Rather than restricting the sum in Eq. K.0.60 to symmetry-allowed pairs, the full sum is retained, absorbing the overcounting into the empirical tightening factor κtight . This means 

κtight encapsulates contributions from all three sources listed above: 

κtight ≈ κtri.ineq. × κp2 × κsym , (K.0.63) where κtri.ineq. ∼ 2–5 from commutator cancellation, κp2 ∼ 3–10 from the momentum bound, and κsym ∼ 1–8 from symmetry (system-dependent). 

Remark K.0.1 (Transferability of κtight ). Because the three factors in Eq. K.0.63 depend on different molecular properties (coupling topology, grid resolution, and point-group symmetry, respectively), the composite κtight is not universal. The values adopted in Table K.3 reflect conservative estimates: denser coupling topologies (e.g. ( N O )4-Anthracene) yield smaller 

κsym and hence smaller overall tightening, while near-harmonic subsystems with high sym-metry (e.g. the C60 cage modes in Anth/ C60 ) benefit from larger κp2 and κsym . Crucially, the relative comparison between REQWIEM and alternative approaches is insensitive to κtight ,since both methods use the same Trotter step count k.

Trotter Step Estimates for Resource Tables 

The resource comparison tables in the main text require specific step-count values. These are obtained from upper bounds by applying the system-dependent tightening factors discussed above. For pyrazine, direct numerical comparison yields κtight ≈ 18 (Section K.0.8). For the larger benchmark systems, exact numerical validation of the Trotter error is not feasible, but several features of the vibronic Hamiltonians (near-harmonic structure, sparse bilinear coupling, symmetry cancellations) suggest comparable or more favorable tightening. The Trotter step counts used in the resource tables are: The conservative choice for (NO) 4-Anthracene (tightening factor of only ∼2×, near the top of the range) reflects the higher electronic-state count ( M = 5) and denser coupling 527 Table K.3: Trotter step counts used in the resource comparison. The range spans quadratic-only (lower) to quadratic + bilinear with f ≈ 0.05 (upper). The adopted values are obtained by applying system-specific tightening factors within the range established by the pyrazine calibration. 

System kanalytic (range †) Tightening kadopted τ (a.u.) Pyrazine (2 mode) 45,800 18 × 2,500 1.65 (NO) 4-Anthracene 8,400–22,000 ∼2× 12,290 0.34 (NO) 4-Anth Dimer 9,600–28,000 ∼10 × 2,792 1.48 Anth/C 60 (reduced) 3,000–8,000 ∼10 × 790 5.2 Anth/C 60 (full) 27,000–77,000 ∼10–20 × 3,274 1.26    

> †Range spans quadratic-only (lower) to quadratic + bilinear with f≈0.05 (upper).

topology, which reduce the cancellation opportunities that benefit simpler systems. By con-trast, the highly structured Anth/C 60 complex benefits from the near-harmonic character of the C 60 cage modes, supporting larger tightening factors. 

K.0.10 Grid Resolution Trade-Off 

From C2N 2 ≫ C0, the Trotter step count scales linearly with the grid size: 

k ∝ N = 2 n. (K.0.64) Each additional qubit per mode doubles the required step count. Table K.4 illustrates this for pyrazine: Table K.4: Effect of grid resolution on Trotter step count (pyrazine, upper bound). 

n (qubits/mode) N C2N 2 kanalytic k/k (n=10) 6 64 3.7 × 10 −6 19,200 0.42 8 256 5.9 × 10 −5 21,700 0.47 10 1,024 9.4 × 10 −4 45,800 1.00 12 4,096 1.5 × 10 −2 168,000 3.66 528 Reducing from n = 10 to n = 8 qubits per mode decreases both qubit count (by 2 d) and Trotter steps (by approximately 4 × in the C2N 2-dominated regime), but may compromise accuracy for high-frequency modes. This trade-off is system-specific and depends on the displacement amplitudes of the vibrational wavepacket relative to the grid spacing. The N -dependent scaling of the Trotter step count also precludes independent optimiza-tion of the Trotter circuit and the per-step gate synthesis: techniques such as Hamming weight phasing or quantum arithmetic that reduce per-step cost but assume a fixed step count must account for the k ∝ N dependence when comparing total resources. 529 Appendix L Grid Convergence for the Morse Oscillator 

This appendix details convergence studies summarized in Section L.4. The grid Hamil-tonian is constructed by combining the QFT-based kinetic energy operator with the Morse potential (Eq. 7.2.1) evaluated at each grid point, and diagonalized numerically. Two con-vergence metrics are reported: ∆ n(1) res and ∆ n(10) res , the number of bound states converged to within 1 cm −1 and 10 cm −1 respectively of the exact analytical eigenvalues (Eq. 7.2.2). An aliasing diagnostic ∥V ∥∞/T max gives the ratio of the maximum potential value on the grid to the maximum representable kinetic energy; values exceeding unity indicate local momenta beyond the Nyquist limit. Table L.1: Convergence of Morse oscillator eigenspectrum: N–O stretch ( De = 6 .5 eV, α =2.7 ˚ A−1, μ = 7 .468 amu, ωe = 6634 cm −1, nmax = 15). Box: [1 .66 , 3.25] ˚ A. 

n N δE 0 δE nmax ∆n(1) res ∆n(10) res Dissoc. err ∥V ∥∞/T max 

(cm −1) (cm −1) (cm −1)5 32 657.21 1684.74 0/16 0/16 104.9 126759 6 64 0.89 191.21 2/16 2/16 171.6 31690 7 128 0.00 11.23 15/16 15/16 172.8 7922 8 256 0.00 11.17 15/16 15/16 172.3 1981 9 512 0.00 11.14 15/16 15/16 172.1 495 10 1024 0.00 11.12 15/16 15/16 172.0 124 11 2048 0.00 11.11 15/16 15/16 171.9 30.9 12 4096 0.00 11.11 15/16 15/16 171.9 7.7 13 8192 0.00 11.11 15/16 15/16 171.8 1.9 The highest bound-state error plateaus (e.g., δE nmax ≈ 3.8 cm −1 for H–metal, constant from n = 8 through n = 13) reflect a box-size limitation: the classical turning point of the uppermost state lies near the box boundary, and refining the grid at fixed L cannot 530 reduce boundary truncation error. Resolving the dissociation threshold (70–206 cm −1 error depending on system) similarly requires increasing L rather than ∆ x. For non-scattering bound-state dynamics that do not populate the uppermost vibrational levels, n = 8–10 may be adequate; this is the regime where the polynomial decomposition of Chapter 4 operates at O(n2) sites per mode. 

L.1 Self-Dual Grid 

The self-dual grid convention, in which ∆ p = p2π/N in natural oscillator units, has been employed in some proposals for quantum simulation of vibrational dynamics. Table L.2 presents the convergence study for the H–metal stretch using this convention. Table L.2: Convergence of Morse oscillator eigenspectrum on the self-dual grid: H–metal stretch (same parameters as Table L.3). The self-dual convention maps grid points into physical coordinates via the natural length scale ℓ = 1 /√μω e. At n ≥ 12, the leftmost grid point enters the deep repulsive wall, producing catastrophic loss of all converged eigenvalues. 

n N δE 0 δE nmax ∆n(1) res ∆n(10) res ∥V ∥∞/T max 

(cm −1) (cm −1)5 32 0.00 — 2/6 3/6 32 6 64 0.00 477.60 4/6 4/6 180 7 128 0.00 44.95 5/6 5/6 2.6 × 10 3

8 256 0.00 1.33 5/6 6/6 1.4 × 10 5

9 512 0.00 0.01 6/6 6/6 5.3 × 10 7

10 1024 0.00 0.00 6/6 6/6 3.1 × 10 11 

11 2048 0.00 0.01 6/6 6/6 9.0 × 10 16 

12 4096 1100.59 — 0/6 0/6 6.3 × 10 24 

13 8192 — — 0/6 0/6 1.0 × 10 36 

The self-dual grid converges all six bound states by n = 9, performing well at moderate register sizes. However, as N increases, the self-dual mapping xj = p2π/N · j/ √μω e + Q0

extends the leftmost grid point further into the repulsive wall, where the Morse potential rises exponentially. The ∥V ∥∞/T max ratio consequently grows exponentially with n: by n = 12, the maximum potential value exceeds 10 24 times the kinetic energy cutoff, and all previously 531 converged eigenvalues are destroyed. This is a fundamental incompatibility between the self-dual convention and any potential with an exponential wall. A real-time evolution on the self-dual grid at n ≥ 12 would produce catastrophically aliased dynamics, as the potential generates local momenta exceeding the Nyquist limit by dozens of orders of magnitude. This observation is relevant to proposals that adopt the self-dual convention without veri-fying its compatibility with the functional form of the potential being simulated. Functionally, these norms can be significantly reduced by regularizing the potential surface, afforded by REQWIEM’s finite-difference approach but not by QROM caching protocol [251] 

L.2 Detailed Resolution Tier Analysis 

Three independent physical requirements determine the minimum grid resolution for scatter-ing or dissociative dynamics. Each establishes a lower bound on n from a distinct mechanism. 

L.2.1 Measurability of Scattering Observables (Right-Edge Requirement) 

For a scattering problem, the box size L must contain the interaction region, initialization zone, and sufficient propagation space before encountering a periodic boundary. The conver-gence data for all four systems (Tables L.3–L.4, including the N–O stretch in Table L.1) show that bound-state box sizes are L ≈ 2.9 ˚ A and L ≈ 3.6 ˚ A respectively. A scattering simulation with partial wave ℓ = 0 requires L ≥ 15–25 ˚ A to accommodate the asymptotic wavepacket, at minimum quadrupling the coordinate range. For higher partial waves, L increases further to > 100 ˚ A, as the centrifugal potential extends far beyond the well. The grid spacing ∆ x = L/ 2n must remain fine enough to resolve the potential features near the well: ∆ x ≲ 0.01 ˚ A for stiff potentials (C–O, N–O stretches with α > 2 ˚ A−1). The minimum register size (Eq. 7.2.3) is driven by the product of large L (from the right-edge requirement) and small ∆ x (from the potential curvature requirement): for Lscat = 20 ˚ A and ∆xreq = 0 .01 ˚ A, nscat = 11; for Lscat = 25 ˚ A and ∆ xreq = 0 .005 ˚ A (appropriate for the stiffest systems), nscat = 13. 532 The momentum-space resolution ∆ p = 2 π/L must also be fine enough to resolve the scat-tering channels: for translational energy Etrans , resolving the energy-dependent transmission coefficient requires ∆ p ≪ p0 = √2μE trans . This constraint is automatically satisfied when L

is large enough to contain the asymptotic wavepacket, but provides an independent lower bound on L that reinforces the right-edge requirement. 

L.2.2 Aliasing-Free Dynamics (Left-Edge Requirement) 

During a scattering event, the wavepacket physically probes the repulsive wall. The local momentum at coordinate x on the repulsive wall, 

plocal (x) = 

q

2μEtot − VMorse (x), (L.2.1) increases as x decreases below x0 until the classical turning point V (x) = Etot is reached. For states near the dissociation threshold ( Etot ≈ De), the turning point lies deep in the repulsive wall where the potential varies rapidly. The ∥V ∥∞/T max ratio quantifies this aliasing across Tables L.3–L.4. For the H–metal stretch, the ratio exceeds unity at all n ≤ 11 and drops below unity only at n = 12 (∥V ∥∞/T max = 0 .78). For the H 2–surface translation, the crossover similarly occurs at n = 12 (∥V ∥∞/T max = 0 .81). For the stiff, deep-well systems, the C–O stretch has ∥V ∥∞/T max = 4 .2even at n = 13, and the N–O stretch has ∥V ∥∞/T max = 1 .9 at n = 13, requiring n ≥ 14–15 before the repulsive wall is fully resolved. The aliasing criterion is more stringent than eigenvalue convergence because it constrains full quantum dynamics. A static eigenvalue calculation can tolerate aliased grid points at the boundary because bound-state wavefunctions are exponentially suppressed there. A real-time evolution cannot, because the Trotter propagator applies e−iτ V (x) at every grid point regardless of wavefunction amplitude. Any amplitude reaching the repulsive wall during the scattering event is subject to aliased phase kicks. Research into non-Hermitian Hamiltonians has begun to consider Trotterized post-selection for complex absorbing potentials on the grid edges [361]; such methods may partially suppress this criterion while maintaining a coherent, 533 Table L.3: Convergence of Morse oscillator eigenspectrum: H–metal stretch ( De = 1 .5 eV, 

α = 1 .2 ˚ A−1, μ = 1 .008 amu, ωe = 3855 cm −1, nmax = 5). Box: [1 .83 , 4.70] ˚ A. 

n N δE 0 δE nmax ∆n(1) res ∆n(10) res Dissoc. err ∥V ∥∞/T max 

(cm −1) (cm −1) (cm −1)5 32 0.76 26.83 1/6 3/6 183.0 12822 6 64 0.00 3.96 5/6 6/6 208.0 3205 7 128 0.00 3.89 5/6 6/6 206.8 801 8 256 0.00 3.86 5/6 6/6 206.2 200 9 512 0.00 3.84 5/6 6/6 205.8 50 10 1024 0.00 3.83 5/6 6/6 205.7 13 11 2048 0.00 3.83 5/6 6/6 205.6 3.1 12 4096 0.00 3.82 5/6 6/6 205.5 0.78 13 8192 0.00 3.82 5/6 6/6 205.5 0.20 non-unitary wavefunction. For an insufficient box size, periodic boundary conditions cause high-momentum components at the right edge to reflect off the hard-core potential at the left edge, inextricably corrupting phase shift calculations (Chapter 6). 

L.2.3 Spectral Completeness of the Bound-State Manifold 

In a nonadiabatic scattering event, population transfer through a conical intersection or avoided crossing distributes the wavepacket across vibrational levels of the product electronic state. The distribution can extend to high-lying levels near the dissociation threshold, partic-ularly for exothermic channels where the electronic energy gap is deposited into vibrational excitation. If upper levels are absent from the discretized spectrum due to undersampling, the simulation produces incorrect branching ratios, energy distributions, and state-resolved cross sections. At n = 7, the C–O stretch resolves only 10 of 23 bound states to within 1 cm −1 (Ta-ble L.5), with 5 additional states within 10 cm −1. For the N–O stretch, 15 of 16 states are resolved at n = 7, but the highest state carries an 11 cm −1 error persisting through n = 13, indicating a box-size rather than grid-spacing limitation. For the C–O stretch at n = 12, 534 Table L.4: Convergence of Morse oscillator eigenspectrum: H 2–surface translation ( De =0.5 eV, α = 1 .0 ˚ A−1, μ = 2 .016 amu, ωe = 1312 cm −1, nmax = 5). Box: [2 .10 , 5.68] ˚ A. 

n N δE 0 δE nmax ∆n(1) res ∆n(10) res Dissoc. err ∥V ∥∞/T max 

(cm −1) (cm −1) (cm −1)5 32 0.32 7.11 2/6 6/6 77.3 13278 6 64 0.00 1.51 5/6 6/6 70.8 3320 7 128 0.00 1.49 5/6 6/6 70.4 830 8 256 0.00 1.47 5/6 6/6 70.2 207 9 512 0.00 1.47 5/6 6/6 70.1 51.9 10 1024 0.00 1.47 5/6 6/6 70.1 13.0 11 2048 0.00 1.46 5/6 6/6 70.1 3.2 12 4096 0.00 1.46 5/6 6/6 70.1 0.81 13 8192 0.00 1.46 5/6 6/6 70.0 0.20 the ν = 21 level spacing has an error of 1.45 cm −1 relative to the exact Morse anharmonic spacing, while lower levels are converged to < 10 −7 cm −1. This residual error is a boundary effect requiring increased L (and therefore n) to resolve. For dynamics populating only the lower half of the vibrational manifold, this requirement is more lax. However, for the scattering and dissociative dynamics motivating the smooth-potential extension, the full bound-state manifold and onset of the scattering continuum must be accurately represented, reinforcing the n ≥ 12 lower bound. 

L.3 Characteristic Energy Analysis for Multi-Surface Potentials L.3.1 Single-Surface Morse Bound-State Dynamics 

For a single Morse potential supporting bound-state dynamics, the characteristic energy is not simply De but rather the maximum potential value sampled on the grid: 

E(Morse, bound) char ≥ ∥ VMorse ∥∞ = De

␀1 − e−α(Qmin −Q0)2 , (L.3.1) 535 Table L.5: Convergence of Morse oscillator eigenspectrum: C–O stretch ( De = 11 .1 eV, 

α = 2 .3 ˚ A−1, μ = 6 .856 amu, ωe = 7707 cm −1, nmax = 22). Box: [1 .52 , 3.39] ˚ A. 

n N δE 0 δE nmax ∆n(1) res ∆n(10) res Dissoc. err ∥V ∥∞/T max 

(cm −1) (cm −1) (cm −1)5 32 1869.05 — 0/23 0/23 4.6 273859 6 64 2.65 1600.67 0/23 1/23 104.8 68465 7 128 0.00 6.62 10/23 18/23 102.0 17116 8 256 0.00 1.46 22/23 23/23 97.5 4279 9 512 0.00 1.46 22/23 23/23 97.3 1070 10 1024 0.00 1.45 22/23 23/23 97.3 267 11 2048 0.00 1.45 22/23 23/23 97.2 66.9 12 4096 0.00 1.45 22/23 23/23 97.2 16.7 13 8192 0.00 1.45 22/23 23/23 97.2 4.2 where Qmin is the left grid boundary. Since Qmin < Q 0, the exponential e−α(Qmin −Q0) grows rapidly with compression into the repulsive wall, and the maximum potential value can exceed De by an order of magnitude or more. The physical origin of this requirement is that the Trotter decomposition applies e−iτ V (x)

at every grid point, including those on the steep repulsive wall. The resulting phase imprint generates momentum components proportional to the local potential gradient; if these ex-ceed the Nyquist limit N , the wavefunction acquires spectral weight that aliases across the periodic momentum-grid boundary, producing irreversible corruption compounding at every Trotter step. For typical reactive-mode parameters ( De = 1–5 eV, α = 1–3 ˚ A−1), the left boundary extends approximately 1–2 ˚ A into the repulsive wall, yielding ∥VMorse ∥∞ ∼ 5–20 De. This increases nmin by ⌈log 2(∥VMorse ∥∞/D e)1/2⌉ ≈ 1–2 qubits relative to the harmonic estimate, placing this regime at n = 10–11. 536 L.3.2 Coupled Morse/Repulsive Bound-State Dynamics and Level Repul-sion 

For a multi-surface system, the characteristic energy must account for the coupled vibronic Hamiltonian. At each grid point Qk, the diabatic potential matrix 

V(Qk) = 

V00 (Qk) V01 (Qk)

V01 (Qk) V11 (Qk)

 , (L.3.2) has operator norm 

∥V(Qk)∥op = V00 (Qk) + V11 (Qk)2 +

sV00 (Qk) − V11 (Qk)2

2

+ V01 (Qk)2, (L.3.3) which exceeds both diagonal entries whenever V01 (Qk)̸ = 0. The characteristic energy for the coupled system, 

E(coupled) char ≥ max  

> k

∥V(Qk)∥op > max {∥ V00 ∥∞, ∥V11 ∥∞} , (L.3.4) is strictly larger than either single-surface maximum at any grid point where the coupling is nonzero and the surfaces are non-degenerate. Beyond the single-point operator norm, the full 2 N × 2N vibronic Hamiltonian ( N = 2 n

grid points per surface) exhibits eigenvalue statistics that further constrain grid resolu-tion. The off-diagonal coupling mixes vibrational levels across surfaces, producing extensive avoided crossings. Takatsuka and collaborators have established that such nonadiabatic cou-pling generically drives the nearest-neighbor level spacing distribution toward the Wigner– Dyson (GOE) distribution, reflecting quantum chaotic behavior [88, 362]. Extensive level repulsion occurs when the coupling mixes vibrational levels whose uncoupled energies are near-degenerate [363], generically manifesting at avoided crossings where the vibrational ladders of two diabatic surfaces interleave. Level repulsion pushes eigenvalues outward: in the GOE regime, P (s) ∝ s e −πs 2/4 sup-presses small spacings relative to the Poisson P (s) = e−s, redistributing spectral weight toward the tails. The grid must resolve these pushed-out eigenvalues, requiring 

E(WD) char ≥ ∥ Hvibronic ∥ > max  

> s

∥V (s)∥∞ + ∥T ∥, (L.3.5) 537 where ∥T ∥ = p2max /2μ and the inequality reflects the off-diagonal coupling contribution. The Wigner–Dyson regime requires both sufficiently strong coupling and sufficiently dense vibrational spectra. Typical Morse potentials support nmax ∼ 15–50 bound states with mean spacings of tens of cm −1 near dissociation; nonadiabatic coupling strengths of V01 ∼ 0.01– 0.1 eV ( ∼80–800 cm −1) suffice to exceed this spacing and produce extensive level mixing [362]. The effect is further amplified for systems with multiple avoided crossings, as in VUV pho-todissociation where the wavepacket traverses a “forest” of coupled states [364]. The practical consequence is an additional ∼1–2 qubit increase: 

E(coupled, bound) char ≈ max  

> k

∥V(Qk)∥op + ∆ Erepulsion , (L.3.6) where ∆ Erepulsion ≤ max k |V01 (Qk)|, placing this regime at n = 11–12. 

L.3.3 Single-Surface Morse Scattering 

For scattering on a single Morse surface, two requirements simultaneously inflate the reg-ister. The box size must accommodate both the bound-state region and the translational wavepacket: 

Lscat ≥ 2Lbound , (L.3.7) adding ⌈log 2(Lscat /L bound )⌉ ≥ 1 qubit. Realistic scattering geometries may require Lscat ∼ 3– 4 × Lbound , adding up to 2 qubits. The characteristic energy must include translational kinetic energy: 

E(Morse, scat) char ≥ ∥ VMorse ∥∞ + Ekin , (L.3.8) where Ekin = p20/2μ. For thermal scattering ( Ekin ∼ 0.03–0 .1 eV), this correction is modest; for hyperthermal or reactive scattering ( Ekin ∼ 0.5–5 eV), it is comparable to or exceeds De.States with momenta exceeding pmax = π/ ∆x are projected out of the Hilbert space entirely, producing systematic errors in the scattering cross section uncorrectable by longer evolution times or smaller Trotter steps. The combined effect places this regime at n = 12–13 qubits per reactive mode. 538 L.3.4 Coupled Multi-Surface Scattering 

This most demanding regime combines multi-surface coupling with extended coordinate ranges and translational kinetic energy. The characteristic energy incorporates all contribu-tions: 

E(coupled, scat) char ≥ max  

> k

∥V(Qk)∥op + Ekin + ∆ Erepulsion . (L.3.9) The eigenvalue statistics of the coupled multi-surface scattering Hamiltonian are more strongly Wigner–Dyson than in the bound-state case: the continuum states above the dis-sociation threshold add spectral density, increasing the number of near-degenerate levels available for coupling-induced mixing, and the scattering wavepacket traverses the avoided crossing region with nonzero translational energy, accessing a broader vibronic spectrum. Takatsuka’s analysis identifies the continual passage of nuclear wavepackets through regions of strong nonadiabatic coupling as the mechanism generating chaotic electronic-state dynam-ics [88], with the nearest-neighbor level spacing distribution following the Wigner surmise even for as few as two coupled electronic states, provided the vibrational spectrum is suffi-ciently dense [362]. In the scattering regime, the dense continuum ensures this condition is generically satisfied. For representative reactive-mode parameters ( De = 2 eV, α = 1 .5 ˚ A−1, μ corresponding to a hydrogen–metal stretch, Ekin = 1 eV, max |V01 | = 0 .1 eV, Lscat = 20 ˚ A), the minimum register size yields nmin = 13–14 qubits. Even the minimum physically faithful representation requires n = 12 qubits. 

L.4 Carbon–Carbon Bond Rupture 

The two-surface C–C model is structurally identical to the NaI model of Section 7.5.2, but provides a convergence test in the regime of stiff molecular potentials with weak nonadiabatic coupling. This system couples a bound Morse surface to a repulsive exponential through a spatially localized Gaussian, probing the grid’s ability to simultaneously resolve vibrational fine structure and dissociative dynamics. 539 L.4.1 Hamiltonian Structure 

The diabatic Hamiltonian takes the form ˆH(R) = − 12μ∂2

∂R 2 12 +

VMorse (R) V01 (R)

V01 (R) Vrep (R)

 , (L.4.1) where the ground-state surface is a Morse potential representing the C–C σ bond, 

VMorse (R) = De

␀1 − e−β(R−Re)2 − De, (L.4.2) and the excited state is an exponential repulsive potential representing the σ∗ antibonding surface, 

Vrep (R) = A e −αR + V∞. (L.4.3) The diabatic coupling is a Gaussian centered between the equilibrium geometry and the outer classical turning point, 

V01 (R) = W0 e−γ(R−Rc)2

, (L.4.4) representing vibronic coupling that enables bond rupture via population of the σ∗ surface. Electronic channels are stored in a single channel qubit ( ⌈log 2(2) ⌉ = 1), with the ground (Morse) surface on |0⟩ and the excited (repulsive) surface on |1⟩, totaling n + 1 qubits per simulation.

The potential and simulation parameters are given in Table L.6. The vertical excitation energy at Re is ∆ V = Vrep (Re) − VMorse (Re) = 4 .36 eV, placing this system in the regime of large surface separation with weak coupling ( W0/∆V ≈ 0.03). The coupling V01 (R) peaks at Rc = 4 .0 a0 with a full width at half maximum of ∆ R =2pln 2 /γ ≈ 5.3 a0, extending from near Re to the outer classical turning point. 540 Table L.6: Potential and simulation parameters for the C–C two-surface model. L.4.3 Convergence Results 

The resource estimate of n = 7 qubits per vibrational mode proposed by Lang et al. [251] is demonstrably unconverged for the C–C bond rupture system, as given by Figure L.1 and L.2. At n = 7, W1 remains 2–4 × above the converged plateau for compact boxes and up to an order of magnitude above for boxes accommodating dissociative motion. This discrepancy arises because the harmonic approximation underlying their grid estimate does not account for the exponential repulsive wall, the anharmonic level spacing near dissociation, or the extended coordinate range required for nonadiabatic population transfer. Convergence to within W1 < 10 −2 eV requires n ≥ 9–10 for bound-state dynamics and n ≥ 11–12 for the dissociative regime, consistent with the resolution tier analysis of Section L.2. 541 Figure L.1: Spectral overlay for n = 13 for various L. At n = 13, there are N = 8192 grid points, with spectral convergence and resolvable peaks for higher bound states (closer to dissociation, leftward peaks). Sufficient spectral resolution is achieved around L = 100. 542 Figure L.2: Spectral overlay for L = 100 a.u. for n = 7 − 13. Resource estimates by Lang et al. [251] use n = 7 for resource benchmarking. For n = 7 the dominant vibrational peaks are present but upper bound states are missing and spurious features appear; by n ≥ 12, peak-by-peak agreement is achieved across the full progression. 543 Appendix M Classical Runtime Derivation M.1 The (ML-)MCTDH Wavefunction Ansatz M.1.1 Standard MCTDH

M.2 Cost per Integration Step M.2.1 Standard MCTDH cost 

The MCTDH equations of motion (derived from the Dirac–Frenkel variational principle) consist of: (i) A-vector propagation: The SIL (short iterative Lanczos) integrator applies the Hamiltonian to the A-vector nK times. Each application costs O(s·NA) where s = NSOP 

is the number of SOP Hamiltonian terms. (ii) Mean-field construction: For each mode κ and each Hamiltonian term r, the mean-field matrix ⟨ ˆHr⟩(κ) 

> jm

requires a contraction over all indices except κ, costing O(NA · nκ). Summing over p modes and s terms gives O(p · ¯n · s · NA). 545 (iii) SPF propagation: SIL on each SPF in the primitive basis, costing O(p · ¯n · N dκ

> prim

)per Krylov vector. This is typically subdominant. The dominant cost is the mean-field construction (ii), giving: 

CMCTDH step = α (p ¯n + nK) NSOP NA , (M.2.1) where α is a hardware-dependent prefactor (seconds per floating-point unit operation) and the nK term accounts for the A-vector propagation. 

M.2.2 ML-MCTDH cost 

In the multilayer formulation, the mean-field construction and coefficient tensor propagation are performed at each node of the tree independently. Local cost at node ν involves a tensor of size nbν +1  

> ν

(accounting for the parent index and bν child indices). Summing over all nodes: 

CML step = α X

> ν∈T

␀bν · nν + nK

 · sν · nbν +1  

> ν

, (M.2.2) where sν is the effective number of Hamiltonian terms relevant to node ν. Each term factorizes across the tree for SOP structures, so sν ≤ NSOP 

Tensors at each internal node have ¯ n3 entries for balanced binary trees ( bν = 2) with uniform SPF count ¯ n and sν ≈ NSOP , as a conservative upper bound. 

CML step = α N nodes (2¯ n + nK) NSOP ¯n 3 . (M.2.3) This replaces NA = ¯ np in the MCTDH formula with Nnodes · ¯n3, converting the exponential dependence on p into a linear dependence on Nnodes ∝ Nvib .For L = 1: Nnodes = 1 and broot = p. The root tensor has size ¯ np+1 (including the “parent” index, which for the root is just nroot = 1 for the wavefunction normalization, but the contraction structure still involves ¯ np ≡ NA). Eq. M.2.2 then gives: 

CML step L=1 

= α (p ¯n + nK) NSOP NA = CMCTDH step . (M.2.4) 546 M.3 SOP Hamiltonian Term Count 

For a vibronic coupling Hamiltonian in Nel electronic states with Nvib vibrational modes and molecular point group of order |G|, the SOP representation contains: 

NSOP = Nvib 

|{z}  

> ˆT(kinetic)

+ Nel · Nvib 

| {z }

> harmonic potentials

+ Nel · Nvib 

| {z }

> on-diagonal linear coupling

+ Nel (Nel − 1) 2 · ρ N vib 

|G|1/2

| {z }

> interstate coupling (symmetry-reduced)

(M.3.1) where ρ ∈ [0 , 1] is the coupling density (fraction of symmetry-allowed coupling terms that are numerically significant). This simplifies to: 

NSOP = Nvib 



1 + 2 Nel + ρ N el (Nel − 1) 2 |G|1/2



. (M.3.2) 

Justification for |G|1/2. Symmetry eliminates interstate coupling terms for which Γ a ⊗

Γi ⊗Γb̸ ⊃ A1, where Γ a,b label electronic state irreps and Γ i labels the vibrational mode irrep. For an Abelian group with |G| irreps, each mode belongs to one irrep, so a fraction ∼ 1/|G|

of modes couple a given state pair. Diagonal terms are not reduced by symmetry since totally symmetric operators always contribute. Since diagonal terms dominate for small Nel and the off-diagonal terms dominate for large Nel , the effective reduction of the total NSOP scales as 

∼ | G|−1/2 rather than |G|−1.For pyrazine ( Nvib = 24, Nel = 2, |G| = 8 via D2h, ρ = 1): 

NSOP = 24 



1 + 4 + 12√8



= 24 × 5.18 ≈ 124 .

The Raab et al. [183] Hamiltonian contains ∼110–130 terms after POTFIT compression; this agrees well.

M.5 Calibration of the Prefactor 

The prefactor α must be calibrated for standard MCTDH and ML-MCTDH. In the MCTDH formula, one “unit operation” is approximately one floating-point multiply within an NA-dimensional matrix-vector product. In the ML-MCTDH formula, one “unit operation” at a tree node involves a recursive single-hole function computation (tree-complement con-traction), a density matrix construction and regularization, and constant mean-field (CMF) integration substeps for the SPFs. These are ∼ 10 3–10 4 times more expensive per tensor element than a single FLOP. 

M.5.1 Standard MCTDH anchor: Pyrazine 24D 

The reference benchmark is the converged MCTDH calculation of the S2 absorption spectrum of pyrazine using the 24-mode, 2-state vibronic coupling Hamiltonian of Raab et al. (1999), 548 as computed by Vendrell & Meyer (2011): 

Tref = 7 hours = 25 ,200 s (single CPU core, c. 2000 hardware) 

NA = 4 .6 × 10 7 (reported coefficient count) 

p = 8 (24 modes in 8 combined groups) ¯n ≈ 8 (geometric mean over groups) 

NSOP ≈ 124 (from Eq. M.3.2) 

nK = 10 

Tprop /∆t = 150 /0.5 = 300 steps 

Mspec = 1 (OPA) From Eq. M.2.1: 25 ,200 = αMCTDH · (8 × 8 + 10) · 124 · 4.6 × 10 7 · 300 = αMCTDH · 1.27 × 10 14 , (M.5.1) yielding 

α(modern) MCTDH = 2 .0 × 10 −11 s / unit op (after 10 × hardware correction). (M.5.2) 

M.5.2 ML-MCTDH anchor: Pyrazine 24D (Vendrell 2011, ML-8) 

The converged ML-MCTDH calculation (simulation “ML-8”) used 4 .5 × 10 5 coefficients in a 5-layer binary tree. Vendrell & Meyer report that this calculation required “a fraction of the time” of the MCTDH reference; the fast ML-1 variant completed in 7 hours. The converged ML-8 wall time is estimated as ∼90 min on c. 2000 hardware (conservatively, ∼ 8× faster than the MCTDH reference, consistent with the ∼ 100 × compression in coefficient count offset by ML overhead). Using ML-8 parameters ( Nnodes ≈ 15, ¯ n = 8, nK = 10, NSOP = 124, Nt = 300): 5,400 = αML · 15 · 26 · 124 · 512 · 300 = αML · 7.43 × 10 9, (M.5.3) 549 yielding 

α(modern) ML = 7 .3 × 10 −8 s / unit op (after 10 × hardware correction). (M.5.4) The ratio αML /α MCTDH ≈ 3,600 reflects a per-node overhead of the multilayer algorithm: recursive mean-field computation via tree traversal, single-hole function assembly, density matrix regularization, and CMF integration substeps. 

M.5.3 Cross-validation 

Using αML = 7 .3 × 10 −8 s with central parameters ( κ = 3, ¯ n = 3 + Nel , ρ = 0 .6): 

System T central wall Reported Agreement 

Pyrazine 24D 1.8 min <7 hr (MCTDH, old HW) assumes hardware improvement Anthracene + 66D 8.4 hr ∼hours good Pentacene dimer 252D 2.9 days hours–days reasonable P3HT:PCBM 113D 3.2 yr (1 core) days (cluster) consistent ∗∗On a 64-core cluster: 3 .2 yr /64 ≈ 18 days; actual used optimized tree and fewer SPFs. 

M.6 Cross-Validation Against Published Benchmarks 

The unified formula can be tested against systems where both the calculation parameters and wall-clock times have been reported. 

M.6.1 Butatriene cation: 4D/4-state 

Parameters: Nvib = 4, Nel = 4, |G| = 4 (C 2v), p = 4 (no mode combination), ¯ n ≈ 8, ρ = 0 .8. Standard MCTDH (using αMCTDH ): 

NSOP = 4(1 + 8 + 0.8·12 2√4 ) = 4 × 11 .4 = 46 

NA = 8 4 = 4 ,096 

Twall = 2 × 10 −11 · 42 · 46 · 4096 · 400 = 0 .06 s 550 This is consistent with butatriene being a standard textbook exercise. For this 4-mode sys-tem, standard MCTDH is the appropriate method (no ML overhead). 

M.6.2 Anthracene cation: 66D/6-state (ML-MCTDH)

Twall = 76 × 400 = 30 ,400 s ≈ 8.4 hr Literature reports tens-to-hundreds of hours on a single core (Table II, [365]). The central estimate of 8.4 hr gives an optimistic estimate, with the availability of numerous cores for parallel execution between electronic states. 

M.6.3 Pentacene dimer: 252D/5-state (ML-MCTDH)

M.7.3 The SPF heuristic ¯n(Nel )Empirical data support ¯n ≈ c0 + c1Nel , c0 = 3 , c1 ∈ [0 .5, 1.5] , (M.7.3) with c1 = 1 as the central estimate. This heuristic is an upper bound for weakly coupled LVC models and may underestimate for systems with dense conical intersection seams. Empirical validation across several systems is presented in Table M.3. 552 M.8 Tabulated Runtime Estimates 

All estimates use nK = 10, ∆ t = 0 .5 fs, Mspec = 1 (OPA) unless otherwise stated. Standard MCTDH tables use αMCTDH = 2 × 10 −11 s; ML-MCTDH tables use αML = 7 .3 × 10 −8 s (cf. M.5). 

M.8.1 Standard MCTDH systems 

Runtime estimates for representative systems are given in Table M.4 (standard MCTDH) and Table M.5 (ML-MCTDH). Increasing from 30 to 36 modes (adding just two combined-mode groups) inflates runtime from 25 days to ∼5 years. This is why chemists switch to ML-MCTDH beyond ∼24 modes. 

M.8.2 ML-MCTDH systems: bounded estimates 

For each system, T min wall and T max wall uses αML = 7 .3 × 10 −8 s. The bounds vary three categories of parameters: 

• Prefactor uncertainty: αML /2 (lower) to αML × 2 (upper), reflecting the 0.67 dex scatter in cross-validation. 

• Tree parameters: κ = 4 (aggressive combination) to κ = 2 (conservative); ¯ nmin =max(6 , min( Nel , 15)) to ¯ nmax = max(10 , 3 + Nel ). 

• Coupling density: ρ = 0 .3 (sparse) to ρ = 1 .0 (dense). The ¯ n lower bound imposes a floor of 6 SPFs for vibrational convergence (independent of Nel )and a ceiling of 15 to reflect the fact that well-optimized ML trees do not require ¯ n = Nel 

at every node. All times are for a single CPU core; divide by core count for parallelized estimates, multiplying by an extra factor for inter-core overhead. 

M.8.3 Comparison: MCTDH vs. ML-MCTDH at fixed system size 

A direct comparison is shown in Table M.6. 553 M.9 Two-Bound Strategy for Quantum Advantage Claims 

For a quantum advantage assessment, bracketing classical runtime with upper and lower bounds reflect the uncertainty in αML , ¯ n, κcomb , and ρ for these estimations: 

M.9.1 Lower bound (optimistic classical, hardest to beat) 

Use αML /2, κcomb = 4 (aggressive mode combination), ¯ nmin = max(6 , min( Nel , 15)), and 

ρ = 0 .3 (sparse coupling): 

T min wall = αML

∆t ·Mspec (M.9.1) The ¯ n floor of 6 ensures vibrational convergence (single-surface dynamics requires ≥5 SPFs even for Nel = 1); the ceiling of 15 reflects the observation that well-optimized ML trees distribute SPFs non-uniformly and do not require ¯ n ≈ Nel at every node. 

M.9.2 Upper bound (pessimistic classical, easiest to beat) 

Use 2 αML , κcomb = 2 (conservative), ¯ nmax = max(10 , 3 + Nel ) (generous SPFs), and ρ = 1 .0(dense coupling): 

T max wall = 2 αML · Nvib · (2¯ nmax + nK) · Nvib
