The ratio T max wall /T min wall ∼ 28–45 reflects three sources of uncertainty, each contributing roughly half an order of magnitude: (i) the αML prefactor ( ×4 range), (ii) the SPF count ¯ n (∼1.5 ×

in ¯ n → ∼ 3.4 × via ¯ n3), and (iii) the κ and ρ variation ( ∼3×). 

M.9.3 Parallelization 

Modern ML-MCTDH codes (e.g., the Quantics package) achieve near-linear parallel speedup for the mean-field construction. On a 64-core node, the single-core times should be divided by ∼50–60 to obtain realistic wall times. For the “Large C 1” target: T min wall /64 ≈ 2.4 hr, 

T max wall /64 ≈ 3.7 days. 554 M.10 Summary: The Complete Formula 

Unified (ML-)MCTDH wall-clock runtime: 

Twall = α · C (T ) · Nvib 1 + 2 Nel + ρ N el (Nel − 1) 2p|G|

!

· Tprop 

∆t · Mspec , (Master Formula) where the complexity factor is: 

C(T ) = 

Nvib 

κ ¯n + nK



¯n Nvib /κ MCTDH ( L = 1) ,

2Nvib 

κ (2¯ n + nK) ¯ n3 ML-MCTDH, binary tree ( L ≥ 2) .

Default parameters: αMCTDH = 2 ×10 −11 s, αML = 7 .3×10 −8 s, nK = 10, ∆t = 0 .5fs, ¯n ≈ 3 + Nel .

Properties: 

• Standard MCTDH scales as O␀N 2vib N 2el ¯nNvib /κ : exponential in Nvib .

• ML-MCTDH scales as O(N 2vib N 2el ¯n3): polynomial in Nvib , cubic in ¯ n.

• The L = 1 (single-layer) limit of the ML formula reproduces the exact MCTDH cost. 

• ¯n encodes the entanglement entropy of the vibronic wavefunction and determines whether classical tensor network methods are efficient.

559 N.1.2 T +V split per-query cost 

Each QSP query to the T +V block encoding involves: (a) 2 d QFT applications, each costing n(n−1) /2 controlled rotations and n Hadamards (Eq. 2.4.1); for d = 4, n = 10: 4 × 90 = 360 rotations. (b) Diagonal phase kicks for p2 

> r

using O(n2) Toffoli gates per mode for arithmetic; for 

d = 4, n = 10: ∼ 400 Toffoli. (c) SELECT/PREPARE networks for the 2-term LCU: ∼ 1,000 additional Toffoli from comparators and controlled swaps. Total per query: ∼ 1,800 Toffoli + 360 rotations. At C(Toff)

N.1.3 Fock-basis sparsity and normalization 

In the Fock basis, the QVC Hamiltonian has nonzero entries from: 

• Diagonal: ωk(nk + 1 /2) — 1 entry per row per mode. 

• Off-diagonal (linear coupling): κk

√nk + 1 connects |nk⟩ ↔ | nk ± 1⟩ — 2 entries per row per mode. 

• Bilinear: γkk ′ (ak + a†

> k

)( ak′ + a† 

> k′

) — 4 entries per mode pair. The sparsity per row is bounded by 

s ≤ 1 + 2 d + 4 

d

2



= 2 d2 + 1 . (N.1.5) For d = 4: s = 33. This counts only on-diagonal electronic-block connections. Off-diagonal vibronic coupling Vij adds O(d) entries per state pair, making reported sparsity a lower bound. 560 The block-encoding normalization for sparse qubitization [282] is 

α2Q = s · max  

> i,j

|Hij | ≤ (2 d2 + 1) · d N max max( |κ|, |γ|, . . . ). (N.1.6) At Nmax = 30, d = 4, max |κ| = 0 .00904 a.u.: α2Q = 22 .0. 

N.2 Arbitrary Smooth PES: QROM Scaling 

For an arbitrary smooth potential V (Q1, . . . , Q d) that does not factorize into a polynomial of low degree, the Fock-basis sparse oracle must store the full d-dimensional tensor of matrix elements. The number of nonzero entries per row becomes 

seff = O(N d

> max

), (N.2.1) and the QROM oracle cost scales accordingly. REQWIEM avoids this by the multilinear expansion (Eq. 7.3.1), which decomposes V

into a sum over subsets S ⊆ { 1, . . . , d } with M¨ obius-inverted coefficients (Eq. 7.3.2). Each subset contributes a UCR circuit with 2 |S| − 1 CNOTs and 2 |S| Rz gates (Eq. 7.3.9), keeping the total cost polynomial in d for bounded interaction order. 

N.3 Dyson Series Convergence: Extended Derivation N.3.1 Connection to the Taylor series of exp( −x)The interaction-picture Dyson series for the Landau–Zener Hamiltonian (Eq. 8.8.1) produces an asymptotic series whose N th term has magnitude 

|cN | ∼ (πΓ) N

N ! . (N.3.1) This is precisely the N th term of the Taylor series of exp( −x) evaluated at x = πΓ. By Stirling’s approximation ( N ! ≈ √2πN (N/ e) N ), 

xN

N ! ≈ 1

√2πN 

e xN

N

. (N.3.2) This quantity is: 561 • Increasing for N < x (since e x/N > 1); 

• Maximum at N = ⌊x⌋, with value ∼ (2 πx )−1/2;

• Decreasing for N > x , with super-exponential decay for N > ex.The N th partial sum SN = PNn=0 (−x)n/n ! alternates in sign. During the growth phase (N < ⌊x⌋), successive partial sums overshoot and undershoot the true value exp( −x) by amounts far exceeding exp( −x) itself. Only after N ≳ ex do the terms become small enough for the alternating series to converge, being the Stokes phenomenon, where the exponentially small quantity exp( −πΓ) is “hidden” behind the factorial growth of the asymptotic series and cannot be resolved by any finite truncation with N < ⌊πΓ⌋ terms [288, 289]. For algorithms approximating e−iHt via a truncated Dyson series or interaction-picture perturbation expansion [290], the truncation order must satisfy N ≥ ⌊ πΓ⌋ to resolve the non-perturbative transition amplitude. QSP/QSVT approximates e−iHt by a polynomial Pd(x) of degree d in the eigenvalue 

x = E/α , using Chebyshev-type polynomial approximation rather than a perturbative ex-pansion. The standard degree bound dQSP = O(αt + log(1 /ε )) [35, 36] is a uniform bound in the eigenvalue argument and does not contain an additive gap-dependent term. The Dyson convergence barrier therefore does not directly impose an additional polynomial degree re-quirement on QSP. However, QSP receives no cost reduction from gap closure (∆ 0 → 0) because the block-encoding normalization α ≥ Tmax is gap-independent, while Trotter’s commutator-norm cost decreases as ∥[Vd, V od ]∥ = ∆ 0 · W → 0. This one-sided scaling causes the gap in implementation resource overhead. Trotter error depends on commutator norms ∥[T, V ]∥, which decrease near conical in-tersections where the diagonal potential surfaces approach each other (Section 8.8.4). The duality is exact: as ∆ 0 → 0, Trotter error ∝ ∆0 · W → 0, (N.3.3) Dyson order ∝ W 2/(∆ 0 v) → ∞ . (N.3.4) 562 Appendix O List of Initialisms and Acronyms

Where an abbreviation carries multiple meanings (e.g., CI for both Conical Intersection and Configuration Interac-tion), both definitions are listed. The rightmost column indicates the chapter or section of first appearance.

ZX ZX-calculus App. J 570 Appendix P Glossary of Technical Terms 

This appendix defines key technical terms, physical concepts, and quantum-computing jargon used throughout this dissertation. Terms are listed alphabetically; the rightmost col-umn indicates the chapter of first appearance. Acronyms and initialisms are catalogued separately in Appendix O. 

Term Definition First Use Ab initio multiple spawning 

A method that represents the nuclear wavefunction as a swarm of Gaussian basis functions (trajectories) that can “spawn” new functions at nonadiabatic cou-pling regions. Ch. 1 

Adiabatic repre-sentation 

Molecular quantum representation in which the elec-tronic basis states are instantaneous eigenstates of the electronic Hamiltonian at each nuclear geometry. Off-diagonal couplings then arise through the nuclear ki-netic energy operator. Ch. 1 

Ancilla qubit An auxiliary qubit used as temporary workspace dur-ing a quantum computation, typically initialized to |0⟩

and disentangled (uncomputed) before measurement. Ch. 2 

Continued on next page. . . 

571 Term Definition First Use Anharmonicity Deviation of a potential energy surface from quadratic (harmonic) form, leading to unequally spaced energy levels and mode–mode coupling. Ch. 1 

Autocorrelation function 

The overlap C(t) = ⟨ψ(0) |ψ(t)⟩ between an initial and time-evolved wavepacket; its Fourier transform gives the energy spectrum. Ch. 1 

Block encoding A technique for embedding a non-unitary matrix (such as a Hamiltonian) into the upper-left block of a larger unitary, enabling its use in quantum algorithms. Ch. 1 

Bogoliubov transformation 

A canonical transformation mixing particle creation and annihilation operators to diagonalize a quadratic Hamiltonian, used in superconductivity and Bose– Einstein condensation. Ch. 9 

Born– Oppenheimer approximation 

The assumption that electronic and nuclear motions decouple due to the large mass ratio, allowing the to-tal wavefunction to be written as a product of elec-tronic and nuclear parts. Ch. 1 

Branching space The two-dimensional subspace of nuclear coordinates (gradient-difference and derivative-coupling vectors) that lifts the degeneracy at a conical intersection. Ch. 4 

Channel register The set of qubits encoding the discrete electronic (or internal) state label in a coupled-channel quantum simulation. Ch. 3 

Continued on next page. . . 

572 Term Definition First Use Circuit depth The number of sequential time steps (layers) in a quantum circuit, where gates in the same layer act on disjoint qubits and execute in parallel. Ch. 1 

Clebsch–Gordan coefficient 

Expansion coefficients for coupling two angular mo-mentum eigenstates into a state of definite total an-gular momentum; appear in partial-wave decomposi-tions. Ch. 9 

Clifford gate A quantum gate from the Clifford group (Hadamard, CNOT, S-gate, etc.) that maps Pauli operators to Pauli operators; these gates can be implemented transversally in many error-correcting codes. Ch. 5 

Conical intersec-tion 

A molecular geometry at which two or more electronic potential energy surfaces become exactly degenerate, forming a cone-shaped crossing that mediates ultra-fast nonadiabatic transitions. Ch. 1 

Cooper pair A bound pair of electrons with opposite spin and momentum in a superconductor, formed via phonon-mediated attraction; the microscopic basis of BCS su-perconductivity. Ch. 9 

Coupled-channel Hamiltonian 

A Hamiltonian of the form H = T ⊗ I C +

P ijV ij (x)|i⟩⟨ j| coupling spatial dynamics to internal-state transitions. Ch. 4 

Coupling mode A vibrational mode that introduces off-diagonal cou-pling between electronic states, directly mediating nonadiabatic transitions. Ch. 4 

Continued on next page. . . 

573 Term Definition First Use Density matrix renormalization group 

A variational algorithm that optimizes a matrix prod-uct state representation of the ground state by itera-tively sweeping through lattice sites. Ch. 1 

Diabatic repre-sentation 

Molecular quantum representation in which the elec-tronic basis states are chosen to vary smoothly with nuclear coordinates, transferring coupling from the ki-netic operator into smooth potential-energy matrix elements. Ch. 1 

Discrete variable representation 

A grid-based method for quantum dynamics in which the wavefunction is represented at quadrature points and the potential operator is diagonal. Ch. 9 

Distorted-wave Born approxima-tion 

A perturbative scattering method that uses distorted (non-plane-wave) reference wavefunctions to improve accuracy for strong short-range potentials. Ch. 9 

Duschinsky rota-tion 

A unitary mixing of normal-mode coordinates be-tween two electronic states, arising when equilibrium geometries and/or force constants differ. Ch. 4 

Eigenstate A quantum state that, when acted on by a given op-erator, returns the same state multiplied by a scalar (its eigenvalue). Ch. 1 

Fan-out protocol A circuit motif that copies a channel-register state to ancillary qubits so that independent vibrational-mode operations can be applied in parallel across channels. Ch. 4 

Continued on next page. . . 

574 Term Definition First Use Fault-tolerant quantum com-puting 

Quantum computation using error-correcting codes and encoded (logical) qubits so that arbitrarily long computations can be performed reliably despite phys-ical gate errors. Ch. 1 

Fidelity A measure of closeness between two quantum states or operations, ranging from 0 (orthogonal) to 1 (iden-tical); F = |⟨ ψ|ϕ⟩| 2 for pure states. Ch. 2 

Finite basis rep-resentation 

Representation of a quantum state in a truncated set of basis functions (e.g., harmonic oscillator eigen-states), in which the kinetic operator is typically di-agonal. Ch. 9 

Flavor mixing matrix 

A unitary matrix (e.g., PMNS for neutrinos, CKM for quarks) relating mass eigenstates to flavor eigenstates. Ch. 9 

Franck–Condon factor 

The squared overlap integral between vibrational wavefunctions of two electronic states, governing the intensity of vibronic transitions. Ch. 3 

Gate count The total number of elementary quantum gates in a circuit; a primary metric for quantum resource esti-mation. Ch. 1 

Gray code An ordering of binary strings in which successive en-tries differ in exactly one bit; used to implement multi-controlled rotations with minimal CNOT overhead. Ch. 4 

Grid resolution The number of discrete spatial points per dimension used to represent the wavefunction; must be sufficient to resolve the shortest de Broglie wavelength. Ch. 2 

Continued on next page. . . 

575 Term Definition First Use Hamiltonian The quantum-mechanical operator whose eigenvalues are the system’s energy levels and whose structure governs time evolution via the Schr¨ odinger equation. Ch. 1 

Harmonic oscilla-tor 

A system with a quadratic potential V (x) = 12 kx 2; the simplest model for molecular vibrations, with equally spaced energy levels. Ch. 2 

Hermitian opera-tor 

A linear operator equal to its own conjugate trans-pose ( H = H†); observables in quantum mechanics are represented by Hermitian operators. Ch. 9 

High-harmonic generation 

A nonlinear optical process in which an intense laser field drives electron recombination with its parent ion, emitting photons at integer multiples of the driving frequency. Ch. 9 

Hilbert space The complete vector space of quantum states for a given system, equipped with an inner product that defines probabilities and expectation values. Ch. 1 

Irreducible repre-sentation 

A matrix representation of a symmetry group that cannot be reduced to smaller block-diagonal form; la-bels the transformation properties of electronic states and vibrational modes. Ch. 4 

Lennard-Jones potential 

An interatomic pair potential V (r) = 4 ε[( σ/r )12 −

(σ/r )6] combining short-range repulsion with long-range van der Waals attraction. Ch. 6 

Continued on next page. . . 

576 Term Definition First Use Logical qubit A qubit encoded redundantly across many physical qubits using a quantum error-correcting code, en-abling fault-tolerant operations. Ch. 5 

Magic state dis-tillation 

A protocol that consumes many noisy copies of a non-Clifford resource state (magic state) to produce fewer high-fidelity copies, enabling fault-tolerant T-gates. Ch. 5 

Marcus model A model for electron transfer in which two coupled harmonic (parabolic) diabatic surfaces cross, with the transfer rate governed by the electronic coupling and reorganization energy. Ch. 3 

Matrix product state 

A one-dimensional tensor network ansatz that repre-sents a quantum state as a chain of contracted ma-trices, with bond dimension controlling the entangle-ment capacity. Ch. 1 

Morse potential An anharmonic interatomic potential V (r) = D e (1 −

e−a(r−r e ))2 that models bond stretching with correct dissociation behavior, unlike the harmonic approxi-mation. Ch. 7 

MCTDH A variational method for quantum nuclear dynamics that expands the wavefunction in a time-dependent basis of single-particle functions, enabling treatment of moderate-dimensional systems. Ch. 1 

Continued on next page. . . 

577 Term Definition First Use Multilinear ex-pansion 

Expansion of a smooth multivariable function on a discrete grid into a hierarchy of terms (constant, lin-ear, bilinear, trilinear, . . . ) enabling systematic circuit construction. Ch. 7 

Neutrino oscilla-tion 

Quantum-mechanical phenomenon in which neutrinos change flavor (electron, muon, tau) during propaga-tion, arising from misalignment between mass and fla-vor eigenstates. Ch. 9 

Nonadiabatic dy-namics 

Molecular dynamics in which the Born–Oppenheimer approximation breaks down and nuclear motion is coupled to transitions between two or more electronic states. Ch. 1 

Oracle A black-box unitary subroutine assumed to imple-ment a specific function; oracle-based algorithms treat it as a given primitive whose internal structure is un-specified. Ch. 1 

Oracle-free algo-rithm 

A quantum algorithm that constructs all unitary op-erations explicitly from elementary gates, without re-lying on an abstract oracle subroutine. Ch. 1 

Partial wave A component of the scattering wavefunction with def-inite angular momentum quantum number ℓ, used to decompose the total cross-section into angular-momentum contributions. Ch. 6 

Continued on next page. . . 

578 Term Definition First Use Phase gradient method 

A quantum arithmetic technique that adds or mul-tiplies numbers stored in a quantum register using phase rotations rather than explicit binary adders. Ch. 5 

Phase shift The angular displacement acquired by a partial wave due to scattering relative to the free-particle solution; encodes the strength and sign of the interaction. Ch. 6 

Point group The set of all spatial symmetry operations (rotations, reflections, inversions) that leave a molecule’s nuclear framework invariant. Ch. 4 

Population dy-namics 

The time-dependent probability of occupying each electronic or internal state, obtained by tracing the total density matrix over spatial degrees of freedom. Ch. 1 

Position register The set of qubits encoding the discretized nuclear co-ordinate(s) on a spatial grid in a first-quantized sim-ulation. Ch. 3 

Potential energy surface 

A hypersurface giving the electronic energy as a func-tion of nuclear coordinates, governing nuclear motion within a single electronic state. Ch. 1 

Product formula A general term for Trotter–Suzuki-type decomposi-tions that approximate e−i(A+B)t as a product of ex-ponentials e−iAt e−iBt with controlled error. Ch. 2 

Quantum advan-tage 

The regime in which a quantum computer outper-forms the best known classical algorithm for a given problem, typically by an exponential or large polyno-mial factor. Ch. 1 

Continued on next page. . . 

579 Term Definition First Use Quantum Fourier transform 

The quantum-circuit implementation of the discrete Fourier transform, mapping a computational-basis state to an equal superposition with phase-encoded amplitudes. Ch. 2 

Quantum gate An elementary unitary operation on one or more qubits; the building block of quantum circuits, analo-gous to classical logic gates. Ch. 1 

Quantum Krylov subspace diago-nalization 

A quantum algorithm that builds a subspace of time-evolved states {e−iHt k |ψ 0⟩} and diagonalizes H

within it, accessing excited states without variational optimization. Ch. 1 

Quantum read-only memory 

A quantum data structure that loads classically pre-computed data into a quantum register via a tree of controlled-SWAP gates; its depth scales logarithmi-cally in the table size. Ch. 1 

Quantum scars Non-ergodic quantum eigenstates with anomalously high amplitude along unstable classical periodic or-bits, violating the eigenstate thermalization hypothe-sis. Ch. 1 

Quasiparticle An emergent excitation in a many-body system that behaves like a modified particle with renormalized mass, charge, or other properties. Ch. 9 

Qubit The fundamental unit of quantum information, a two-level quantum system whose state is a superposition 

α|0⟩ + β|1⟩.Ch. 1 

Continued on next page. . . 

580 Term Definition First Use Qubitization An optimal Hamiltonian simulation technique based on block encoding that achieves query complexity lin-ear in time and logarithmic in inverse error. Ch. 1 

Rabi oscillation Coherent oscillation of a two-level quantum system driven by a resonant perturbation, with frequency proportional to the coupling strength. Ch. 9 

Recurrence spec-trum 

The time-autocorrelation function ⟨ψ(0) |ψ(t)⟩ of a propagated wavepacket, whose Fourier transform yields the absorption or emission spectrum. Ch. 1 

Reorganization energy 

In Marcus theory, the energy cost to distort the nu-clear framework from the equilibrium geometry of one diabatic state to that of the other, without changing electronic state. Ch. 3 

Resource estima-tion 

The process of counting the quantum gates, qubits, circuit depth, and other resources required to imple-ment a quantum algorithm at a target precision. Ch. 5 

Ross–Selinger Synthesis 

Optimal ancilla-free decomposition of Rz rotations into Clifford+ T gates. Ch. 5, 8 

Rotation synthe-sis 

The compilation of an arbitrary single-qubit rotation into a sequence of Clifford+T gates with a speci-fied approximation error, typically using the Ross– Selinger or related algorithms. Ch. 5 

S-matrix The scattering matrix relating incoming and outgoing asymptotic states in a collision; its elements encode all transition amplitudes between channels. Ch. 6 

Continued on next page. . . 

581 Term Definition First Use Scattering cross-section 

A measure of the effective target area presented by a particle to an incoming projectile, encoding the prob-ability of a particular collision outcome. Ch. 6 

Smoothed parti-cle hydrodynam-ics 

A mesh-free Lagrangian method for fluid dynamics that represents the continuum by a set of particles with smoothed density kernels. Ch. 9 

Spectral Range Penalty 

The QSP polynomial degree scales with the Hamil-tonian spectral range α, which grows as 4 n for grid-based kinetic operators. Ch. 8 

Split-operator method 

A propagation scheme that alternately applies kinetic and potential operators in the position and momen-tum bases, connected by Fourier transforms. Ch. 2 

Strang splitting A second-order symmetric operator-splitting scheme: 

e−iHt ≈ e−iV t/ 2 e−iT t e−iV t/ 2, widely used in split-operator wavepacket propagation. Ch. 2 

Surface code A topological quantum error-correcting code defined on a two-dimensional lattice of physical qubits, widely considered the leading candidate for fault-tolerant quantum computing. Ch. 5 

Surface hopping A mixed quantum–classical method in which nuclei follow classical trajectories on a single electronic sur-face, with stochastic hops between surfaces at nona-diabatic coupling regions. Ch. 1 

Continued on next page. . . 

582 Term Definition First Use T-count The number of T-gates (or their equivalents) in a quantum circuit; the dominant cost metric in fault-tolerant architectures. Ch. 5 

T-depth The number of sequential layers containing at least one T-gate; determines the time cost of a fault-tolerant circuit when T-gates cannot be parallelized. Ch. 5 

T-gate The single-qubit π/ 8 phase gate diag(1 , e iπ/ 4); a non-Clifford gate whose count dominates the cost of fault-tolerant circuits. Ch. 5 

Tensor network A structured decomposition of a high-dimensional tensor (wavefunction) into a network of lower-order tensors, enabling efficient representation of entangled many-body states. Ch. 1 

Toffoli Floor The irreducible Toffoli gate cost in oracle-based meth-ods, independent of rotation-synthesis precision. Ch. 8 

Trotter error The approximation error introduced by replacing the exact time-evolution operator with a finite product of partial exponentials (Trotter–Suzuki formula). Ch. 2 

Trotter–Dyson Duality 

Near conical intersections, Trotter error vanishes while perturbative truncation order diverges and QSP cost remains anchored to the gap-independent spec-tral range, giving a one-sided structural advantage to split-operator methods. Ch. 8 

Continued on next page. . . 

583 Term Definition First Use Trotterization Approximation of the time-evolution operator e−iHt 

by a product of simpler exponentials, one for each term in the Hamiltonian, with error controlled by step size. Ch. 2 

Tuning mode A vibrational mode that modulates the energy gap between electronic states (shifts potentials along the diagonal), influencing the crossing geometry. Ch. 4 

Ultrastrong cou-pling 

A light–matter coupling regime where the interaction energy is a non-negligible fraction ( ≳ 10%) of the bare mode frequency, invalidating the rotating-wave approximation. Ch. 9 

Uniformly con-trolled rotation 

A quantum gate that applies a different rotation angle to a target qubit conditioned on the state of all con-trol qubits; the key primitive for encoding position-dependent potentials. Ch. 2 

Unitary operator A linear operator U satisfying U †U = I, guarantee-ing norm preservation; all quantum gates and time-evolution operators are unitary. Ch. 1 

Variational com-pression 

A hybrid quantum–classical technique that com-presses a many-Trotter-step circuit into a shallower parameterized ansatz optimized to match the full evo-lution. Ch. 3 

Continued on next page. . . 

584 Term Definition First Use Variational quan-tum eigensolver 

A hybrid quantum–classical algorithm that uses a pa-rameterized quantum circuit (ansatz) and classical op-timization to find the ground-state energy of a Hamil-tonian. Ch. 1 

Vibronic cou-pling 

Interaction between vibrational (nuclear) and elec-tronic degrees of freedom that drives transitions be-tween electronic states through nuclear displacement. Ch. 1 

Wavepacket A spatially localized quantum state constructed as a superposition of plane waves or basis functions, whose time evolution encodes dynamical observables. Ch. 1 

Wigner D-matrix Matrix elements of the rotation operator in the angular-momentum basis, used to transform between body-fixed and space-fixed reference frames. Ch. 9 

Work register Auxiliary qubits used for intermediate computations (e.g., arithmetic, phase kickback) that are uncom-puted before the end of a Trotter step. Ch. 3 

XOR-class de-composition 

A partitioning of off-diagonal coupling pairs ( i, j ) by the value i ⊕ j, enabling parallel application of all couplings sharing the same XOR label. Ch. 4 585
